Title: Comment submitted by Serena Silva
Comment:
I am a groundwater geologist with professional experience evaluating surface water–groundwater interactions and stream and river habitats. I submit this comment to express concern that the proposed revisions to 40 CFR Part 121 would significantly weaken protection of waters of the United States by limiting Clean Water Act Section 401 certification to point-source discharges alone.

Streams and rivers are fragile, interconnected systems in which degradation frequently occurs through hydrologic alteration, physical habitat modification, wetland and floodplain disconnection, and cumulative impacts rather than through exceedances of discharge-based pollutant criteria. Restricting Section 401 certification to discharges would prevent states and tribes from addressing these dominant mechanisms of impairment and from ensuring compliance with water quality standards designed to protect aquatic life.

Please see the attached comment for a detailed technical discussion and recommendations to ensure that Section 401 certification remains an effective tool for protecting water quality standards and the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of waters of the United States.

Title: Comment submitted by Skokomish Indian Tribe
Comment:
February 6, 2026

Ms. Lauren Kasparek
Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division 
Office of Water (4504-T) 
Environmental Protection Agency 
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW 
Washington, DC 20460 

Attention: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, “Updated the Water Quality Certification Regulations” 

Re: Comment Deadline Extension Request 

Dear Ms. Kasparek,
The Skokomish Indian Tribe submits this letter to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in response to the agencies’ request for written feedback on the proposal titled “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations,” published in the federal register on January 15, 2026. 

The federal register notice, unfortunately, identifies a very short comment period that does not adequately support tribal engagement or the required tribal consultation that are necessary procedural steps when considering this level of agency action. For this reason, The Skokomish Indian Tribe is formally requesting an extension of the current February 17, 2026 comment deadline.

Tribes have a substantial interest in the proposed rule and implementation and need more time to provide meaningful feedback. The federal government’s trustee obligations to protect treaty-reserved rights and resources, and the years-long dialogue about how to properly implement the Clean Water Act in a manner consistent with federal trust obligations, is symptomatic of the need to extend the comment period beyond the prescribed 30 days. We respectfully request a 90 day extension to the comment period.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Title: Comment submitted by Marissa Neal
Comment:
My Name is Marissa Neal. I am submitting this comment as a concerned Texas resident who is passionate about promoting clean water and protecting our environment. I am concerned that the EPA’s proposed limitation on Clean Water Act Section 401 certification for point-source discharges into WOTUS, combined with stricter limits on the reasonable period for state review, would disproportionately harm environmental justice communities and weaken protections for wildlife and interconnected water systems. With Texas operating under a more limited pre-2015 WOTUS framework, many waters that support fish, birds, wetlands, and communities, such as small streams, flood-connected waterways, and groundwater systems, are already excluded from federal jurisdiction. Further narrowing the scope of waters that can be considered for discharge impacts under Section 401 risks allowing pollution to affect these connected systems without adequate review or accountability. 
Discharges from industrial facilities, ports, pipelines, and stormwater infrastructure often affect non-WOTUS waters before impacts even reach federally regulated waters, causing direct harm to habitats, wildlife, and communities. Limiting what states may consider and how much time they are given to review applications undermines Section 401’s role as an environmental justice safeguard. Having a constrained “reasonable period of time” can pressure certifying authorities to rush decisions or overlook cumulative and indirect impacts, shifting environmental and public health risks onto vulnerable ecosystems. EPA should retain sufficient flexibility in both scope and timing to allow states and tribes to fully assess water quality impacts reasonably related to licensed discharge, consistent with the Clean Water Act’s goal of protecting the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. 

Sincerely, 
Marissa Neal

Title: Comment submitted by Owamniyomni Okhodayapi
Comment:
Administrator Lee Zeldin and Lauren Kasparek
United States Environmental Protection Agency
Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division, Office of Water (4504-T)
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20460
 
RE: Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule
 
Dear Administrator Zeldin,
I write on behalf of Owámniyomni Okhódayapi, a Dakota-led nonprofit based in Minneapolis, Minnesota, in response to the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. We respectfully urge the EPA not to adopt the proposed rule, as it would undermine state and tribal authority to safeguard waters essential to our communities, cultures and public health.
Owámniyomni Okhódayapi is leading the cultural and environmental restoration of a five-acre site on the Mississippi Riverfront at Owámniyomni, a place of cultural, spiritual and ecological significance for the Dakota people – Native Americans whose ancestral homelands are what is now known as Minnesota. Once a thriving gathering place, Owámniyomni (or St. Anthony Falls) was severely damaged by decades of industrialization. The falls collapsed due to tunneling and other milling impacts, the water was polluted and critical habitat destroyed. We work collaboratively with the four Dakota Nations in Minnesota to amplify their voices and visions including to improve water quality and restore habitats central to our heritage. 
Central to Dakota values is the understanding that water is a living relative with inherent rights, deserving of care and protection. This responsibility compels us to oppose policies that make it easier to pollute or harder for communities to prevent harm.
The Clean Water Act has long served as the nation’s strongest framework for protecting rivers, streams, wetlands and drinking water sources. A pillar of that framework is Section 401, which affirms the authority of states and authorizes tribes to review federally permitted projects to ensure they do not impact local water quality. This authority reflects the Act’s recognition that states and tribes are best positioned to evaluate local conditions, cumulative impacts and community needs.
Diluting state and tribal authority under Section 401 puts water quality at risk for millions of people and disproportionately harms Native communities already burdened by the impacts of pollution by industrialization and commercialization.
We are also concerned that this proposal continues the current administration’s rollback of Clean Water Act protections, which was designed to fast-track projects and reduce costs for developers at the expense of water quality safeguards. The EPA has provided no evidence that Section 401 authority has been misused or that a regulatory overhaul is necessary. In other words, we believe the proposed rule fails to demonstrate a problem and instead would make it easier for industries to pollute waterways without proper accountability.
We urge the EPA to withdraw its proposed Section 401 rule to fully preserve state and tribal authority under the Clean Water Act and protect water as the living relative it is.

Sincerely,
Shelley Buck
President
Owámniyomni Okhódayapi

Title: Comment submitted by E. Williams
Comment:
Lauren Kasparek
Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division
Office of Water (4504-T)
Environmental Protection Agency
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20460

Attention: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, “Updated the Water Quality Certification Regulations”

Dear Ms. Kasparek,

The proposed updates to section 401 of the Clean Water Act would remove comprehensive water quality assurance to the overall project. Because the proposal focuses on the point source discharge and not the entire body of water, the health of smaller streams, wetlands, and headwaters are at risk. The proposed rule also limits state and tribal power to regulate federally-permitted projects despite the potential effect of the projects’ impacts on the bodies of water. 

Reverting to the 2020 update to Section 401 that limits state and tribal authority of conditions to certifications will likely degrade our nation’s water quality and ultimately the health of our environment and communities.The unusually short window for public comment should be extended by 90 days to give the necessary and appropriate stakeholders to weigh in on the issue. 

I strongly urge the EPA to extend the period for public comment and seriously consider denying the proposed rule. 

Best,
E Williams

Dear Administrator Zeldin,

I live in an area dependent where clean, clear water is of extreme economic importance and vital to the quality of life. The three impounded lakes and numerous clear streams are critical to the Missouri Ozark region where I call home. Our own property has a spring fed Ozark stream that feeds in to Bull Shoals Lake. I therefore, on behalf of the National Wildlife Federation and affiliate partners from Missouri along with our millions of members and supporters, we write in opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposal to again revise regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act), a tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted or licensed projects.

Title: Comment submitted by Idaho Department of Environmental Quality (IDEQ)
Comment:
The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality (IDEQ) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certification rule (Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929) and the accompanying amendments to 40 CFR part 121.

IDEQ has reviewed the proposed rule updates and offers the following comments and recommendations provided in the attached document.

Title: Comment submitted by Ohio Environmental Council (OEC)
Comment: The attached comments are submitted on behalf of the Ohio Environmental Council (OEC). The OEC is committed to protecting the environment and the health of all Ohio communities and our thousands of dedicated members across the state. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on EPA’s proposed revisions to the Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. Ohio relies on Section 401 as a core tool for protecting water quality, ensuring responsible development, and maintaining the ecological integrity of our watersheds. For these reasons, we are concerned that several elements of the proposed rule would unnecessarily narrow state authority, reduce flexibility, and undermine the cooperative federalism framework Congress intended.

Title: Comment submitted by State of Utah Public Lands Policy Coordinating Office
Comment:
The State of Utah (State) supports the administration’s attempt to improve the clarity, consistency, and effectiveness of certification and appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification. Section 401 has long served as an essential mechanism for safeguarding Utah’s waters. Utah urges the EPA to implement any changes in 401 Certification through the framework of cooperative federalism, ensuring that the State retains its authority to regulate and protect its resources. Preserving Utah’s sovereign interests and the right to manage its own resources is critical to shielding the State from potential federal overreach by less supportive administrations.
Utah faces unique water resource challenges. Our state is experiencing a persistent historic drought, coupled with rapid population growth and significant public land holdings. These stressors place extraordinary pressure on Utah’s water supply and underscore the need for a regulatory framework that empowers states to manage water quality effectively. A rule that respects state sovereignty and promotes clarity and predictability is essential to ensuring sustainable water management in the West.
In addition, Utah recently submitted comments emphasizing the importance of defining “waters of the United States” (WOTUS) consistent with the Supreme Court’s decision in Sackett v. EPA . The Sackett standard requires clear, objective criteria for the exercise of jurisdiction over states’ resources. As the EPA considers revisions to Section 401, Utah urges the agency to recognize that “[r]egulation of land and water use lies at the core of traditional state authority.”   Utah appreciates federal efforts to improve the efficiency, clarity, and consistency of Section 401 certifications and requests that these efforts be implemented in a way that respects the State’s role as the primary regulator of its own resources.
In this spirit of cooperative federalism and state sovereignty, Utah highlights Congress’s intention to recognize the State as primary in regulating water resources. Specifically, Congress expressly recognized “the primary responsibilities and rights of states to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution” and “to plan the development and use… of land and water resources.”  Section 1370 further provides that, except as expressly stated, nothing in the Act shall impair state jurisdiction over waters within its borders . These provisions confirm that states are the primary stewards of water quality within their boundaries.
Accordingly, Utah asks the EPA to maintain the State’s right to request and evaluate additional information when processing a 401 Certification . As noted in the Federal Register, a request for Certification “may not necessarily represent the totality of information a certifying authority may need to act on a request.” Id. The states’ ability to request additional information enables the states to protect water resources and avoid denying Certifications when the “request for certification” lacks essential information needed for compliance. Further, Utah requests that the EPA respect the state’s authority to deny requests for incompleteness if additional information is not provided within the statutory maximum deadline. Maintaining states’ ability to request additional information within the statutory deadline will ensure determinations are timely and defensible.
To ensure the balance of cooperative federalism and further the purposes of this proposed rule change, Utah encourages clarity, consistency, and preservation of states’ rights. First, Utah supports a scope for 401 Certification consistent with cooperative federalism, preserving state authority to impose conditions necessary to protect its water resources and promote the welfare of its citizens. Second, to ensure consistency, Utah advocates for predictable timelines and standardized baseline requirements for certification requests and decisions, while allowing states flexibility to determine those requirements and timeline extensions – when extensions are mutually agreeable and in the best interest of the applicant. Third, states should retain the ability to address unique local conditions and impose requirements that safeguard each state’s water quality interests within a uniform federal framework that promotes consistency and clarity. Utah supports Congress’s policy that states are primary regulators of their own water and sees the State’s broad authority over 401 Certification as a successful application of this policy. 
PLEASE REFER TO THE ATTACHED LETTER FOR THE COMPLETE COMMENTS. THANK YOU

Title: Comment submitted by Minnesota Trout Unlimited
Comment:
Comment on EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 - Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.

I am writing on behalf of Minnesota Trout Unlimited and its thousands of members who fish the waters of the state to express strong opposition to the U.S. EPA's proposed rule revising the regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.  Minnesota Trout Unlimited (MNTU) is familiar with Section 401 Certification process, since MNTU frequently undertakes stream habitat restoration projects around Minnesota.  MNTU is required to obtain numerous state and federal permits and as well as 401 Certification from the Minnesota Pollution Control Agency.  Although the 401 Certification process takes additional time and effort, the existing water quality certification process is important to prevent degradation of water quality and protect our cherished coldwater fisheries.
We oppose the changes to the rules proposed by EPA because they would substantially weaken long-standing state and Tribal authorities that are essential for protecting fish, wildlife, and the aquatic habitats they depend on. 
Section 401 is one of the Clean Water Act’s most important provisions for ensuring that federally permitted activities do not degrade water quality or impair designated uses, including the protection of fish and aquatic life.  State and Tribal water quality certifications are essential opportunities to meaningfully address site-specific impacts to fish and wildlife from projects such as mines, pipelines, dredging, and other infrastructure projects. The changes to the rules EPA has proposed would significantly constrain that State and Tribal authority, putting Minnesota’s rivers, streams, and lakes at increased risk of pollution or damage.
Clean, healthy rivers, streams and lakes are crucial to support healthy fisheries, supply clean drinking water for downstream communities and to support numerous uses. Using the Section 401 certification process to evaluate the potential impacts of projects and development, and considering what additional conditions are warranted, is key to ensuring our state waters are not polluted or destroyed. 
We are particularly troubled by proposed rule’s narrowing of the scope of impacts that states and Tribes may consider as part of the Section 401 review process. Fish and wildlife are affected not just by direct discharges, but also by changes to flow regimes, habitat connectivity, temperature, and sediment transport.  Many aquatic and riparian species, including migratory fish, amphibians, and waterfowl, are sensitive to these cumulative and secondary effects. Limiting review to discharge only, rather than the project as a whole, does not reflect ecological realities and undermines protection of biological integrity.
The proposed rule would also weaken State and tribal authority to require conditions necessary to avoid harm to fish and wildlife, such as seasonal construction restrictions to protect spawning or migration, minimum flow requirements to sustain aquatic habitat, and fish passage and screening.  These types of state or tribal added conditions are often critical to prevent irreversible damage, including in waters already impacted by altered hydrology and habitat fragmentation.
Clean water is essential to healthy communities and economies.  Continuing to allow States to protect State waterways through the 401 Certification process is far less expensive than taxpayers paying to restore or repair damaged ecosystems.   is much more expensive than protecting them in the first place.  Clean water and healthy fisheries produce healthy communities and economies.  States and Tribes understand this.  The EPA should not impede their efforts to protect our nations waters.  The proposed rule would do just that - impede the thoughtful work of State and Tribal governments to protect our public waters and the fisheries they support.
MNTU urges the EPA to withdraw the proposed rule and retain the existing Section 401 procedures that allow States and Tribes to protect water quality, aquatic habitat, fisheries, and communities that rely on them.  Our thousands of members are users of these important resources and demand the EPA do more to strengthen the Clean Water Act, rather than weaken it as the proposed rule would do.  
Sincerely,
John P. Lenczewski
Executive Director
Minnesota Trout Unlimited

Title: Comment submitted by Massachusetts Rivers Alliance, et al.
Comment:
The Massachusetts Rivers Alliance and the additional 12 organizations signed onto the the attached full comment letter are concerned that EPA’s proposed changes to the Section 401 certification process will reduce the quality of our waters by undermining the role of certifying agencies. We are particularly opposed to EPA’s proposal to limit the scope of 401 certification to impacts from discharges from point sources to navigable waters as contrary to both the text and purpose of the Clean Water Act. We urge EPA to not adopt the proposed changes.

The points are explained in further detailed in the attached letter:

I.	We Oppose EPA’s Proposal to Narrow the Scope of 401 Certifications to Discharges from a Point Source to Navigable Waters.

A.	Narrowing the scope of 401 certifications would contradict the plain language of Section 401.

B.	Certifying authorities must be able to consider impacts to waterbodies protected under state law but that are not WOTUS.

C.	Certifying authorities should be able to consider impacts from nonpoint source pollution.

D.	Certifying authorities should be able to consider impacts to non-navigable waters.


II.	EPA’s Proposal Would Undermine the Purpose of Section 401 and Certifying Authorities’ Ability to Protect Water Resources.

III.	EPA’s Proposal Could Weaken Compliance with Public Notice Requirements.

IV.	We Oppose EPA’s Proposal to Stop Treating Tribes as States for the Purpose of 401 Certification.

V.	EPA Should Continue to Allow Modifications to the Grant of Certification Without the Agreement of the Applicant.

Title: Comment submitted by Clean Water for All et al.
Comment:
The Clean Water for All Coalition and these 113 organizations strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule to again improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Section 401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Clean Water Act in 1972 and is specifically designed to empower states and Tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.

The proposed rule will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health, the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them.

We strongly urge you not to move forward with this ill-conceived and unnecessary rule and keep the existing 2023 rule in place.

Title: Comment submitted by Gunpowder RIVERKEEPER®
Comment:
February 17, 2026 

Submission via www.regulations.gov 

Lee Zeldin, Administrator
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. 
Washington, DC 20460 

Re: Environmental Protection Agency, Proposed Rule: “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations,” 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026) Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929.  
   
Dear Administrator Zeldin,

On behalf of Gunpowder RIVERKEEPER, I urge the EPA to withdraw its proposed revisions to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act that would limit the ability of states and Tribal governments to protect water quality. 

As a Waterkeeper organization working daily to safeguard the Gunpowder, we see firsthand how federally permitted projects can threaten drinking water, fisheries, and public health if proper safeguards are not in place. 

Gunpowder Riverkeeper is a nonprofit environmental and public health membership organization charged with protecting, conserving, and restoring the Gunpowder, Little Gunpowder, Bird, Bush and Middle River watersheds.

Gunpowder Riverkeeper has 502 members who reside, work, and recreate in the Gunpowder, Little Gunpowder, Bush, Bird and Middle River watersheds and have aesthetic, environmental, commercial, and property interests related to the water quality and sensitive species like Eastern Brook Trout in the upper watershed, federally endangered Atlantic Sturgeon in the Tidal Basin, and drinking water for 1.5 million residents in the Baltimore Metro Area.

We have attached the full comment letter in opposition to the Proposed Rule Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 for your review.

If approved in its current form, the Proposed Rule will create immediate and irreparable harm to the Gunpowder Riverkeeper’s organizational and supporter’s interests in protecting water quality and sensitive species.

Respectfully submitted,
 
Theaux M. Le Gardeur 
/S/ Theaux M. Le Gardeur 
Executive Director and RIVERKEEPER 
Gunpowder RIVERKEEPER 
1207 Sparks Road 
Sparks-Glencoe, Maryland 21152 
gunpowderriverkeeper@gmail.com

Title: Mass comment campaign sponsored by Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC)
Comment:
Dear EPA Administrator Zeldin,

Please accept these 4,678 public comments from members and activists of the Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC) in reference to Docket # EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929.

I am concerned about your agency’s recent proposal that would restrict the Clean Water Act authority of states and Tribes to help ensure that federally permitted projects do not undermine water quality.

For 50+ years, the Clean Water Act has empowered communities to modify projects that would foul their own drinking water sources, rivers, and wetlands. This proposal would weaken the law to fast-track oil and gas pipelines and other damaging projects, harming states and Tribes without their input in the process.

We cannot give away more of our waters to industry while our communities, health, wildlife, and environment pay the price.

I urge you to reverse course and uphold communities’ rights to have a say in projects that could impact their clean water.

Thank you.


Sincerely,

Margaret Tran Ramadhar
National Digital Advocacy Campaign Manager, NRDC

Title: Mass comment campaign sponsoring organization unknown
Abstract: 600 similar comments were received for this campaign
Comment:
I am reaching out to express my staunch opposition to this proposed rule. While it would streamline and expedite permitting for major energy projects, it would also narrow the scope of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, limiting state and tribal governments’ ability to protect waterways within their borders.

Section 401 has historically given state and tribal governments the authority to challenge large energy projects with long-term impacts on water quality, like oil and gas pipelines. But under the new rule, states and tribes would only be able to consider the immediate water quality impacts of a project when choosing to approve or reject it. They will essentially lose their ability to protect local waterways—even those that support their fisheries and drinking water supplies.

Consider a project like a dam. Even though the water released from it would pass water quality standards, the dam still impacts water flow and fish migration, both of which can have devastating impacts on river ecosystems and local communities in the long term.

As it stands, Section 401 has been instrumental in protecting water quality, and has rarely been “misused” to kill unwanted projects. Rather than gutting it to support fossil fuel projects and strip states and tribes of their ability to challenge them, we should be exploring ways to scale up wind, solar, and battery storage—energy sources that can provide power quickly, reliably, and affordably without jeopardizing the health of our waterways and the communities that rely on them.

Please do not finalize this harmful rule.

Title: Mass comment campaign sponsoring organization unknown
Abstract: 229 similar comments were received for this campaign
Comment:
I am writing to express strong opposition to the U.S. EPA's proposed rule revising the regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. The proposed changes would substantially weaken long-standing state and Tribal authorities that are essential for protecting fish, wildlife, and the aquatic habitats upon which they depend. 

Section 401 has long served as one of the Clean Water Act’s most important tools for ensuring that federally permitted activities do not degrade water quality or impair designated uses, including the protection of fish and aquatic life.  State and Tribal water quality certifications are an essential and sometimes the only meaningful opportunity to address site-specific impacts to fish and wildlife from projects such as dams, pipelines, transmission lines, dredging, and other infrastructure. The proposed rule would significantly constrain that authority, putting rivers and streams at increased risk of pollution or damage.

Healthy rivers and streams are important to support healthy fisheries, supply clean drinking water for downstream communities and to support a multitude of uses including irrigation and municipal use. It is essential that development with impact to these waters be properly evaluated and that any necessary conditions are put in place to ensure our waters are not polluted or destroyed. 

Of particular concern is the proposal’s narrowing of the scope of impacts that states and Tribes may consider as part of the 401-review process. Fish and wildlife are affected not only by the direct discharge, but also by other project impacts to waters including changes to flow regimes, habitat connectivity, temperature and sediment transport. Many aquatic and semi-aquatic species - including migratory fish, amphibians, freshwater mussels, and waterfowl – are highly sensitive to these cumulative and secondary effects. Limiting review to focus only on the discharge rather than the project as a whole fails to reflect ecological realities and undermines effective protection of biological integrity.

The proposed rule would weaken State and tribal authority to require the necessary conditions to mitigate or avoid harm to fish and wildlife, such as seasonal construction restrictions to protect spawning or migration, minimum flow requirements to sustain aquatic habitat, fish passage and screening, just to name a few. These conditions are often critical to preventing irreversible damage, particularly in waters already stressed by climate change, altered hydrology, and habitat fragmentation.

This proposed rule is especially concerning given the recent related proposal from the EPA and Army Corps to exclude all ephemeral streams, the majority of wetlands and an unknown number of intermittent stream miles from the regulatory protections of the Clean Water Act.  Clean water is essential to healthy communities and economies.  Restoring or repairing damaged ecosystems is much more expensive than protecting them in the first place.  We should be working to increase protections for our nations waters, not weaken them.

I urge the EPA to withdraw the proposed rule and retain the existing procedures that allow states and Tribes to protect water quality, aquatic habitat, and the fish, wildlife and communities that rely on them.  Any revisions to the Section 401 regulations should strengthen, not undermine, the Clean Water Act’s core objective: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the nation’s waters.

Thank you for considering my views.

Title: Mass comment campaign sponsoring organization unknown
Abstract: 15 similar comments were received for this campaign
Comment:
I am submitting this comment as a business leader in response to EPA's proposed update to the Water Quality Certification Regulations under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.

My name is Roslyn Ogburn, and I am with GodsNaturalwonder@gmail.com, a business operating in Michigan. Like many businesses, ours depends on clean, reliable water to operate, manage costs, and plan for long-term investment.

Section 401 supports business certainty by ensuring that federally permitted projects account for water quality impacts early in the permitting process, when risks can be addressed at lower cost and with greater predictability. States and Tribes play a critical role in this process by applying locally appropriate standards, data, and technical expertise that reflect site-specific conditions and watershed characteristics. Preserving state rights and Tribal sovereignty is essential: the ability of local authorities to evaluate and condition projects ensures that water quality protections reflect local conditions, practical expertise, and the needs of communities and businesses that depend on clean water. Limiting this authority is a clear infringement on local control.

I am concerned that the proposed rule would narrow the range of impacts subject to review and limit the ability of states and Tribes to conduct meaningful, science-based evaluations grounded in local conditions. Excluding foreseeable impacts from review does not eliminate risk; it defers it. Those deferred risks often resurface later as project delays, operational constraints, costly retrofits, or litigation that is far more disruptive and expensive than early corrective action.

From a business perspective, predictability comes from identifying material risks early—not from discovering problems after financing, contracts, and construction timelines are already set. As proposed, the rule would reduce opportunities for early problem-solving and increase uncertainty for both businesses and public agencies.

I support reasonable streamlining that improves administrative clarity and reduces unnecessary duplication. However, efficiency should not come at the expense of substantive review. Limiting the ability to consider local water quality impacts simply shifts costs downstream and increases the overall economic burden.

Comment:
Dear Administrator Zeldin,

I am concerned by the EPA proposal to weaken Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA) and strongly oppose the proposed changes. 

The Clean Water Act provides states and authorized Tribes with an important tool to help protect water quality. The current proposed changes to Section 401 limit that scope in a detrimental manner that ties the hands of regulators and keeps them from looking beyond the pollution discharged into waterways to holistically evaluate the impact of a project on water quality. States and authorized Tribes need the ability to  determine the scope of review that will protect water quality, not be limited to an ever narrowing scope of federally protected water and solely tasked to review direct water quality impacts of discharge into those waters. The broader water-quality effects caused by a project as a whole are what communities rely on the Clean Water Act to analyze and mitigate.

Water pollution has serious impacts, and any decision that threatens to increase pollution, harm public health, or exacerbate impacts to habitat needs to be analyzed and weighed. Waterways are not solely impacted in the spot where discharge occurs and limiting the scope of the Section 401 analysis would result in a lack of review of project impacts upstream and downstream. With the changes proposed, states and Tribes could lose the ability to use Section 401 to protect many smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters within their borders. This would render state and tribal decision makers powerless to block, or place conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters, even if they are vital to local drinking water supplies, fisheries, recreation, or flood control.

Limiting the scope and timeline for analysis to take place fails to achieve
the CWA goal of ensuring states, territories, and tribes are empowered to protect their water resources and instead only benefits the companies behind the proposed projects. It leaves taxpayers on the hook for additional cleanup of pollution that was unable to be mitigated because it was not analyzed under a discharge only scenario. It opens the door for projects that should not be permitted due to impacts and risks that would become clear during analysis, but are never properly reviewed. 

I call on the EPA to use its authority to uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water, public health, and species habitat in waterways and wetlands.

Title: Mass comment campaign sponsoring organization unknown
Abstract: 143 similar comments were received for this campaign
Comment:
I am writing in opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulation. Section 401 is a cornerstone of the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism framework, providing states and authorized Tribes with the authority to ensure that federally permitted activities comply with water quality standards before pollution occurs. This preventive function is essential to protecting drinking water sources, wetlands, fisheries, and downstream communities from irreversible harm.¹
The proposed rule would significantly narrow how states and Tribes may exercise their Section 401 authority by restricting the scope of review, redefining what constitutes a complete certification request, and limiting the conditions that certifying authorities may impose.² While EPA characterizes these changes as procedural clarifications, their practical effect would be to constrain state and Tribal oversight and accelerate permitting timelines at the expense of water quality protection. This approach conflicts with the Clean Water Act’s express purpose “to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.”³
Section 401 has historically served as one of the most effective tools for protecting wetlands and headwater streams that are critical to downstream water quality, flood mitigation, and ecosystem function. Weakening this authority risks increasing reliance on post-hoc cleanup rather than prevention, shifting long-term environmental and financial burdens onto states, Tribes, and taxpayers. Environmental organizations and legal experts have warned that the proposed changes would reduce meaningful review of projects such as pipelines, hydropower facilities, and other infrastructure with well-documented risks to water resources.⁴
Of particular concern is how this proposal continues a broader pattern of undermining Tribal authority through regulatory action. Tribes are sovereign governments, not stakeholders, and many hold treaty-reserved rights to clean water, fisheries, and aquatic resources. Federal agencies have a legally enforceable trust responsibility to protect these rights. Any rule that limits the ability of Tribes to meaningfully review, condition, or deny certifications for projects affecting their waters or treaty resources undermines binding treaties and long-standing federal obligations.⁵
EPA has previously recognized that robust Tribal participation in water quality certification is essential to honoring Tribal sovereignty and protecting Tribal waters.⁶ Rolling back or narrowing this authority under the guise of efficiency erodes trust, weakens environmental justice protections, and disproportionately impacts Tribal nations and downstream communities that often bear the greatest consequences of water pollution.
For these reasons, EPA should withdraw or substantially revise the proposed rule to preserve strong state and Tribal authority under Clean Water Act Section 401. Protecting clean water requires honoring the statute’s preventive intent, respecting Tribal sovereignty and treaty rights, and ensuring that economic development does not come at the cost of irreversible harm to waters and communities.

Title: Comment submitted by John Weber
Comment:
To Whom It May Concern,

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations (Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929).

I support efforts to make federal permitting more efficient and predictable. At the same time, I am concerned that aspects of the proposed rule risk narrowing Section 401 review in ways that could unintentionally weaken protections for wetlands—systems essential to water quality, flood control, and local economies.

I spend considerable time in both the Great Lakes region and South Florida. In both places, wetlands are not abstract features; they are central to how water moves, how nutrients are filtered, and how communities are protected from flooding and storms. These benefits are often indirect and cumulative, yet they are precisely how water quality is protected or degraded.

Wetlands reduce nutrient and sediment loading before water reaches rivers, lakes, and drinking-water supplies. When wetlands are lost or impaired, communities face higher treatment costs, increased harmful algal blooms, and declining fisheries. These impacts may appear local at first but accumulate across interconnected systems.

Wetlands also function as natural infrastructure. They store water, slow peak flows, and reduce downstream flooding during heavy rainfall and hurricanes. As extreme weather becomes more common, weakening safeguards that prevent wetland loss will likely increase long-term costs for disaster response, insurance, and infrastructure repair—far exceeding the short-term savings of faster permitting.

I also believe it is important to preserve the role Congress intended for states and tribes under Section 401. Local authorities understand their hydrology and watersheds in ways national rules cannot fully capture. Narrowing certification scope risks shifting long-term environmental and economic costs onto states while limiting their ability to prevent harm.

In this context, the Supreme Court’s decision in Sackett v. EPA should not be overextended. While the Court narrowed federal jurisdiction, it did not conclude that wetlands lacking a continuous surface connection are unimportant to water quality, nor did it disturb Congress’s broader purpose to protect the Nation’s waters. Section 401 remains a mechanism through which states may address water-quality impacts from intermittent, seasonal, or indirectly connected wetlands consistent with local hydrology.

EPA can streamline permitting without sacrificing wetland protection. I respectfully suggest:

1-Allow expedited or tiered review for projects with clearly minimal impacts, while preserving full Section 401 review where cumulative or downstream effects may occur.

2-Retain consideration of hydrologic connectivity, including seasonal surface and shallow subsurface flow, with clear standards.

3-Permit certification timelines to pause when applications are incomplete to avoid rushed decisions driven by procedural deadlines.

4-Encourage programmatic or watershed-scale certifications in sensitive regions.

5-Recognize wetlands as natural infrastructure that protects water quality and reduces flood risk.

6-Preserve state and tribal flexibility to tailor implementation to regional wetland systems.

In the Great Lakes, tributary wetlands retain phosphorus before it reaches nearshore waters; incremental losses contribute to basin-wide nutrient loading and harmful algal blooms, particularly in western Lake Erie. In South Florida, the Everglades depends on slow, shallow sheet flow; modest changes to wetland elevation or connectivity can disrupt flows, increase nutrients, and undermine decades of restoration investment. In both regions, hydrologic connectivity is foundational.

For these reasons, I urge EPA to revise the proposed rule to improve permitting efficiency through targeted procedural improvements while preserving the ability of certifying authorities to fully evaluate and protect wetlands and connected waters.

Thank you for considering these comments.

Sincerely,
John Weber

Title: Comment submitted by Pearl McLeod
Comment:
I am deeply concerned about your agency’s proposed rule that eliminates Clean Water Act protections for many U.S. wetlands and streams. Protecting these  water sources is deeply vital to our country's health and economic welfare.

America’s waterways are so interconnected that even weakening federal protections for wetlands anywhere will have a deep impact on clean water across the country. The proposed change would shrink those powers, allowing only TAS tribes to perform evaluations through a separate, more rigorous authorization program. This change would drastically reduce tribes' powers to protect their land and act on their treaty rights.

I stand with thousands of people across the nation who are united in opposition to any attempts to gut clean water protections. Please keep our critical waters protected and do not finalize this new rule.

Title: Comment submitted by Brittany Myers
Comment:
Date: 01/30/2026
To: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 
Attention: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 
RE: Comments on Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (CWA Section 401)

To Whom It May Concern,
I am writing as a concerned citizen Florida/Flagler County to express my strong opposition to the proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 Water Quality Certification regulations. Specifically, I am deeply concerned by the proposal to narrow the "scope of certification" from “activity as a whole" to a "discharge-only" focus. This change significantly undermines the ability of States and Tribes to protect local waters from the cumulative environmental impacts of large-scale projects.
1. Loss of Oversight for Cumulative Effects By limiting the scope of review to the specific point of discharge, the proposed rule ignores the broader reality of how large infrastructure projects—such as pipelines and dams—impact our ecosystems. Water quality does not exist in a vacuum; it is influenced by upland construction, clearing of riparian vegetation, and changes in local hydrology. Narrowing the review prevents states from assessing the cumulative effects of a project’s entire lifecycle, leaving our communities vulnerable to "death by a thousand cuts" where each individual discharge point may seem minor, but the project’s total impact is devastating.
2. Threats to Local Streams and Wetlands The proposed rule creates a dangerous gap in the protection of our most vulnerable water bodies. Wetlands and small headwater streams are the biological "kidneys" of our watersheds. Under the proposed "discharge-only" scope, a state may be forced to certify a project even if the construction of the project itself results in the destruction of non-jurisdictional wetlands or the degradation of streams through non-point source runoff.
•	Specifically, to Flagler County we have the Matanzas River Basin and local wetlands. It is one of the last relatively undisturbed estuaries of Florida’s east coast, with nearly 35% of the basin comprised of critical wetlands. 
•	Cumulative Development Pressure: With major land development projects currently sited in the headwaters of Pellicer, Moultrie, and Moses creeks, our watershed is already facing a projected increase in developed land use to over 25%. Under the proposed rule, the state would be unable to look at the: "activity as a whole" for these massive developments. This means they could be forced to ignore the cumulative loss of wetlands that act as our primary defense against storm surge and flooding—an issue that already causes "Moderate Flooding" levels (over 2.3 ft) at locations like Bings Landing.
•	Wastewater and Infrastructure Strain: Palm Coast is currently grappling with wastewater capacity challenges, specifically at Wastewater Treatment Plant 1, which is nearing its permitted limits. By narrowing the scope of Section 401, the EPA would limit the ability of state regulators to ensure that new federal permits for large-scale projects do not exacerbate our existing infrastructure strain or lead to increased nutrient loading (nitrogen and phosphorus) that fuels harmful algal blooms in our canals and the Intracoastal Waterway.
•	Pollution and Runoff: My community already faces threats from stormwater runoff carrying pesticides, sediments, and bacteria into our waterways. The proposed rule would prevent the state from placing necessary conditions on a project’s construction phase (like upland erosion control) simply because those impacts aren't tied to a specific "point source" pipe. This will inevitably lead to more pollution in our local streams and the degradation of the delicate salt marshes that protect our coastline.

3. Erosion of Cooperative Federalism Section 401 was designed by Congress to ensure that federal licenses do not bypass state water quality standards. This proposal shifts the balance of power away from the local experts and communities who best understand their own watersheds and hands it back to federal agencies that may prioritize project speed over local environmental health.
Conclusion I urge the EPA to maintain the 2023 Rule's "activity as a whole" scope. Protecting our water requires a holistic approach that considers how a project actually functions in the real world, not just how it looks at the end of a single pipe. I ask the Agency to prioritize the health of our local streams, wetlands, and families over regulatory shortcuts for industry.
Sincerely,
Brittany Myers

Title: Comment submitted by Mark Morgan
Comment:
I am writing to you as a Coloradan who cares deeply about the health of our mountain streams and the future of my family's outdoor traditions. I am reaching out to express my strong opposition to the EPA's proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. These changes would strip away the power of our state and local Tribes to protect the very water that sustains our communities, our farms, and our world-class fisheries.

In Colorado, our rivers are our lifeblood. Section 401 has historically been one of our most effective tools for ensuring that large projects like dams, pipelines, and transmission lines do not ruin the water quality we rely on. For decades, it has given our state the right to review these projects and set common-sense conditions to protect fish and wildlife. By weakening this authority, the federal government is effectively telling us that we no longer have a say in protecting our own backyard from the impacts of industrial development.

This proposal is particularly concerning because it narrows the focus to only "direct discharge" of pollution, ignoring the bigger picture of how a project actually affects a river. As someone who spends time on the water, I know that fish aren't just affected by what is dumped into a stream. They are affected by changes in water temperature, flow levels, and the loss of habitat connectivity. If a project dries up a stream bed or blocks a spawning run, the damage is just as real as if a chemical was spilled. We need to be able to consider the whole project, not just a small part of it.

For my family, this isn't just a policy debate. It is about whether or not our kids will still be able to find healthy trout in our mountain streams twenty years from now. If we lose the ability to require seasonal construction restrictions or minimum flow requirements, we risk losing the biological integrity of our waters forever. These safeguards are especially vital now, as our rivers are already stressed by drought and a changing climate.

Protecting our headwaters is a matter of fiscal and environmental responsibility. It is much cheaper to protect a stream today than it is to try to restore a ruined ecosystem tomorrow. This rule, combined with other recent efforts to remove protections for wetlands and smaller streams, represents a massive step backward for Colorado.

I urge you to stand up for our state's right to protect its own resources. Please ask the EPA to withdraw this rule and instead support policies that maintain the chemical, physical, and biological health of our nation's waters. Our rivers and the families who enjoy them deserve nothing less.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 
I submit this comment in response to EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) §401 Water Quality Certification regulations. I am interested in this regulation because I work in environmental compliance, permitting, and regularly operate within multi‑agency regulatory frameworks. My experience includes navigating federal, state, and local environmental regulations and how they apply at my facility. I am generally supportive of the proposed rule’s objective of improving clarity and procedural consistency in the §401 certification process.
This comment focuses on the portions of the proposal that clarify the scope of §401 certification review, standardize application completeness expectations, and define procedural elements such as timelines and certification conditions tied to water quality impacts. These components are especially important in practice because they shape how certification requests are evaluated across jurisdictions, and this can be a recurring challenge in environmental permitting due to the variation in how §401 certification is implemented. 
Differences in how agencies define completeness, scope of review, and allowable certification conditions can create uncertainty, delay, and inconsistent expectations for project advocates. When procedural standards are unclear or highly variable, both regulators and applicants spend additional time resolving threshold issues rather than focusing on substantive water quality protections.
EPA’s proposal to standardize core procedural elements, including completeness criteria and review scope, would improve predictability and transparency while preserving the important role of states and tribes in protecting water quality. By focusing certification review on point‑source discharges into federally regulated waters and on water‑quality‑related impacts, the proposal provides clearer guidance on the intended scope of certification decisions. In complex permitting environments involving multiple agencies, clearer jurisdictional and subject‑matter boundaries improve coordination and reduce confusion.
From a rulemaking perspective, this proposal is not only a technical regulatory update but also an example of how agencies recalibrate the balance between uniform federal standards and state‑level discretion within a cooperative federalism framework.
The proposal identifies implementation inconsistency and procedural uncertainty as current challenges and responds with clearer procedural standards. More defined expectations around completeness, timelines, and certification conditions should support more consistent decisions and more transparent outcomes across jurisdictions. My participation here is offered to assist in improving the final rule. In presenting these comments, I aim to follow sound rulemaking advocacy practices, including reasoned analysis, attention to statutory structure, and respect for the agency’s procedural obligations.
For these reasons, I encourage EPA to adopt the proposed clarifications to scope and procedure in the final rule. I also recommend that the final rule maintain appropriate operational flexibility for state and tribal authorities to address site‑specific water quality conditions when supported by the administrative record.
Clear procedural standards, well‑defined review scope, and transparent decision criteria benefit agencies, regulated entities, and the public. They improve predictability while supporting effective environmental protection. I appreciate EPA’s effort to refine and clarify the §401 framework and respectfully submit these comments for consideration.

Title: Comment submitted by Jeff Bacon
Comment:
I oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations with respect to the scope of waters clause.

The notion that waters beyond the borders of the united states should not be considered ignores the situation of the Great Lakes, namely that one large body of water is shared with Canada. When a body of water is shared in this way, what affects one side affects both sides. A US entity such as Enbridge could choose to run a pipeline through Canadian waters in order to avoid US or State of Michigan or State of New York review. Yet any poor design or planning would still affect the US and those states since it is the same body of water and we cannot pretend an oil spill on the Canadian side will deign to only kill Canadian fish or spoil Canadian shoreline. 

Further, the idea of focusing on "navigable waters" ignores the fact that a navigable body of water might be linked to non-navigable bodies of water within a given ecosystem or drainage basin - but what happens to one body of water again will happen to them all. The existing rules account for this, the proposed changes do not.

Further, it is inappropriate to focus solely on "discharge from a point source". Again, with pipelines. There might be a single point source, there might not even be a "discharge" - at the time of construction. However, a pipeline can break, thus creating a point source discharge where there was not one. Thus the review needs to consider not only where a discharge currently exists, but also where one might occur as a result of the activity under review, which means a review of the entire system. 

There is a temptation to attempt to strictly focus on the pure text of what is in a law from Congress, however all regulations and rulings involve _some_ amount of interpretation. 

The motivation for these rule changes is stated to be to clarify, simplify, and to speed along regulatory approvals. These are laudable goals. However, there is also risk that these goals are pursued solely in the shorter-term economic interest of business, at the expense of the greater public good - notably, the longer-term goals of having clean water for everyone. Clean water is vital to life, much as clean air is, and sometimes it is necessary to impose economic costs on business in the short term to ensure the public good in the longer term.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the USEPA’s proposed rule issued on January 16 that would weaken Section 401 of the Clean Water Act and limit the ability of states and tribes to protect their waterways from pollution caused by federally permitted energy projects.

Section 401 has, for decades, served as a critical safeguard by allowing states and tribes to review and, when necessary, deny permits for projects that threaten local water quality. This provision reflects the reality that state and local governments are best positioned to understand the unique ecological, public health, and economic needs of their watersheds. Curtailing this authority undermines cooperative federalism and strips communities of a vital tool to protect their drinking water, wetlands, rivers, and streams.

The proposed rule would make it easier to construct oil and gas pipelines, coal export terminals, and other large-scale energy infrastructure projects while reducing meaningful oversight of their impacts on local waterways. This change would increase the risk of pollution, habitat destruction, and long-term damage to water resources that millions of people depend on for drinking water, recreation, and livelihoods.

The justification that this rule change is necessary to supply electricity to data centers is insufficient and troubling. Expanding energy infrastructure should not come at the expense of clean water protections or public health. Sustainable energy planning must prioritize environmental safeguards rather than weaken long-standing laws designed to prevent pollution before it occurs.

This proposal follows recent efforts to roll back federal protections for wetlands and streams, further eroding the Clean Water Act’s core purpose. Together, these actions represent a significant step backward in protecting the “waters of the United States,” contrary to the intent of Congress and the public interest.

I urge the USEPA to withdraw this proposed rule and preserve the full authority of states and tribes under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Protecting clean water is not an obstacle to progress—it is a fundamental responsibility and a cornerstone of environmental justice, public health, and long-term economic stability.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Title: Comment submitted by Zenon Kane
Comment:
I am concerned that the proposed rule limits state and tribal authority, narrows the review of water quality impacts to an unrealistic scope, and prioritizes permitting speed over protecting water quality and public health. These changes risk overlooking cumulative pollution, local conditions, and downstream impacts that directly affect communities.
I oppose the proposed updates to the Water Quality Certification Regulations because they weaken important safeguards that protect water resources people rely on every day for drinking, fishing, farming, and recreation. By restricting what states and tribes are allowed to consider during certification, the proposal may fail to account for how pollution actually behaves in the environment, including runoff, sedimentation, and pollutants that accumulate over time or travel downstream.
From an environmental health perspective, water quality cannot be separated from human health. Even small increases in pollution can contribute to long-term health risks, especially for children, older adults, and communities already facing higher environmental burdens. Wetlands, headwaters, and smaller waterways play a critical role in filtering pollutants and protecting downstream water supplies, yet these areas are more likely to be overlooked under a narrower review process.
The proposal also places strict limits on timelines and standardizes information requirements in a way that may reduce meaningful review. While efficiency is important, rushing decisions or restricting the ability of certifying authorities to request additional information may lead to incomplete evaluations and missed risks. Local and tribal agencies are often best positioned to understand site-specific conditions, historical pollution concerns, and community health impacts, and their expertise should be preserved rather than constrained.
Additionally, limiting state and tribal authority undercuts the cooperative federalism framework that is central to the Clean Water Act. States and tribes have long served as a frontline defense in protecting water quality, and reducing their role could lead to weaker protections and increased public health risks over time.
While timely infrastructure development is important, it should not come at the expense of clean water, public health, or community trust. I urge EPA to reconsider these proposed changes and retain a more flexible, comprehensive approach that allows states and tribes to fully evaluate potential water quality and health impacts before certifications are granted.

Sincerely,
Zenon Kane, MD
Occupation and Environmental Medicine Resident Physician

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)’s proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certifications. For well over 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has been a critical tool to allow states and the federal government to cooperatively manage our shared water resources—and ensure that local voices are not disregarded in the process. This proposed rulemaking—which is being carried out under an order to make it easier for oil and gas infrastructure to be built, not to better safeguard our waters– is contrary to the language of the Clean Water Act and would greatly restrict states’ and Tribes’ long-standing authority to protect waters within their borders.

Water Quality Certification is one of the most important and effective tools available to states and Tribes to ensure federally-licensed projects are protective of water quality for drinking and recreation, and aquatic habitat for fish and wildlife. The proposed rule would greatly undermine state and Tribal authority to protect their waters by restricting the type of protective conditions states and Tribes can put on federally licensed or permitted activities that threaten state or Tribal waters. 

Additionally, the proposed rule would restrict the timeline and information for state and Tribal review. States and Tribes must have adequate time and information to review and process complex applications and the ability to impose conditions on permits that will protect water quality, especially given the differences in types of resources and water quality standards between states. 

In 2023, the EPA appropriately reasserted the strong role that states and Tribes should have in protecting their waters. This strong role - which has been the norm for most of the last 55 years - does not need to be weakened. I urge you to withdraw this damaging proposal immediately. I instead ask you to uphold the longstanding state and Tribal authority to protect waters within their boundaries.

Title: Comment submitted by Michael Meredith
Comment:
On behalf of the National Wildlife Federation and affiliate partners from Missouri, along with our millions of members and supporters, we write in opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposal to again revise regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act), a tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted or licensed projects. This proposed rule largely recycles an ill-conceived rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, and data centers are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services they provide to both people and wildlife. The 2020 rule was subsequently replaced in 2023 by a rule that restored the strong proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act while making some needed updates. We urge the EPA to keep the 2023 rule in place.
This proposal flies in the face of established science, and could potentially endanger our most precious natural resource, our drinking water.
The proposed rule is unnecessary and harmful while undermining state and Tribal sovereignty. It would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court precedent by limiting the scope of certification from the activity to the discharge from a point source of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. For instance, it could eliminate or undermine state and Tribes from being able to put in place protective measures so that rivers and streams have adequate flow, habitat is protected, fish passage can occur, and that public access to designated uses like fishing and recreation is maintained.
It undercuts the ability of states to ensure they have the information they need to assess how various projects may impact their waters. It improperly gives the applicant an effective veto over modifications to protective conditions if a project changes. And it appears to remove the ability of states and Tribes to protect non-federal waters with 401 certification – a critical rollback given that EPA is considering a rule that would leave less than 20 percent of historically covered wetlands and only a fraction of historically covered headwater streams still federally jurisdictional. Finally, it limits the ability of states to protect other water quality-related requirements of state law by narrowing the definition of these requirements to applicable and appropriate state or water-quality related requirements for discharges only. This would make it harder for states to protect things like flow and habitat.
The 2023 rule adequately addressed any concerns regarding uncertainty around timing or process while clarifying that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. It did this while restoring the broad and longstanding ability of states and Tribes to protect their waters, as envisioned by the Act. This proposal amounts to little more than a needless weakening of that ability for no reason. The Section 401 process rarely results in the denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming number of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.
Furthermore, consider recent significant changes to the scope of Clean Water Act protections generally. Section 401 is only triggered when a federal permit or license involving a discharge to waters of the United States is at issue. Most federal permits and licenses that trigger Section 401 are Section 404 discharge permits under the Clean Water Act itself. In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a decision in Sackett v. EPA that dramatically reduced the number of waters covered by the federal Clean Water Act. Adding to this, the EPA is now finalizing a rule that would even further limit the number of waters protected by the Act. If the proposed EPA rule governing waters of the United States is issued as proposed, it would result in an estimated 81% of historically federally protected wetlands lacking Clean Water Act protection. Protections for millions of miles of streams are also likely to be lost. As such, if both this proposal and the waters of the United States rule are finalized, there will be far fewer opportunities and weaker options for states to protect their waters under Section 401. This threatens waters, wildlife, state and Tribal sovereignty, plus the people and communities that rely on these protections.
We strongly urge you to adhere to the requirements and charge of the Clean Water Act and not move forward with this proposed rule, instead keeping the existing 2023 rule in place.
Sincerely,
Michael Meredith

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality.

The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources.

Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections.

Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations.

I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship.

Title: Comment submitted by Jim Steitz
Comment:
***The Clean Water Act makes plain that states may veto Section 401 permits for infrastructure projects that cause gratuitous harm to aquatic ecosystems in that state, correctly reflected in the 1971 rule. The purpose of this clause is vividly demonstrated today. The authors of the Clean Water Act anticipated that one day, the EPA leadership offices would be occupied by scientifically and morally illiterate men, ambitious for power and favor, who say EPA's mission is to unleash American industry. They would purge EPA of its responsibility to protect our streams and wetlands from economic interests, and instead join industry in shuffling along this destruction, disdainful and oblivious to the destruction of biodiversity, hydrological systems, and human health. This has now come to pass. They anticipated that one day, vapid, hand-waving appeals to nebulous concepts like ‘vital infrastructure,' or ‘national interest,' or this Administration's fetish, ‘energy dominance,' could be wielded to discard Americans' love for the very things that make life worth living, and make America a worthwhile place to live. 

In my former home state of Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality vetoed the so-called ‘Jordan Cove Liquified Natural Gas' Project in 2019 using its section 401 authority. This was a correct choice, and the people of Oregon and America are better off for it. EPA would define out of existence the capacity of Oregon to choose whether it wishes to be a sacrifice zone. This cautionary scenario is repeatable any time that a company with no care either for the people and life of a place, or for the future, draw a line on a map showing all to be bulldozed, erasing everything beautiful, precious, and valuable in the way. Section 401 is a tool for humans with values not measured in dollars or in barrels or cubic feet to assert their right to a quality of life, and to a healthy local ecosystem.

America has lost the majority of its wetlands already. The extinction crisis is especially pronounced in aquatic ecosystems. Simultaneously, extremes of flood and drought are magnified by climate change, whether this Administration believes it or not. Our remaining wetlands, aquifers, and tributary streams are more precious than ever in buffering these extremes. This Section 401 guardrail against combined, federal-corporate voracious power is more vital than ever. If EPA hands industry the tools to steamroll the love of Americans for our last aquatic ecosystems, the Trump Administration will leave our children with great ‘energy dominance,' but a bleak, barren, meaningless world stripped of natural life, and a traumatic, harried struggle for survival between droughts and floods.*** 

I am writing to urge EPA to withdraw its proposed revisions to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. I believe this proposal would drastically limit the ability of states and Tribal governments to protect water quality.

Federally permitted projects can threaten drinking water, fisheries, and public health if proper safeguards are not in place.

Section 401 embodies cooperative federalism and ensures that local expertise and community voices are not overridden by federal permitting decisions. Weakening this authority would disproportionately harm frontline and underserved communities and make it harder to prevent pollution before it occurs.

The proposed rule undermines decades of effective clean water protections and prioritizes industry convenience over the public interest. I urge EPA to reject this proposal and fully uphold Section 401 as Congress intended.

Thank you.

Hello,

This new rule will limit the ability of states to block the construction of oil, and gas pipelines, coal export terminals, and other energy projects that in all likely hood will pollute local waterways. Thank you,
Monica Dechering

Title: Comment submitted by Neil Bass
Comment:
As someone who has worked on both sides of CWA regulations, I know that the rules and regulations can seem cumbersome but let's remember what brought about the Clean Water Act in the first place.  Without rules and regulations our waters were full of offal and chemicals.  So much so that in Missouri some residents recount the, " overpowering smells of blood, piss, and decaying flesh."  In Cleveland, Ohio the Cuyahoga River was so full of flamable chemicals that it often caught fire.  Obviously, we Americans are not capable of preserving the environment over our own profit.  

New definitions of Waters of the United States, as currently proposed, will lead to a decrease in regulated waters and thus to increased pollution.  This increased pollution will directly effect downstream waters, which are regulated, and make them less fishable and swimmable, two standards of the Clean Water Act.  This lowered water quality will also impact the health of all Americans who come into contact with it, whether physical contact or through treatment of the waters to make them potable.  
Proposal's to limit the 401 enforcement abilities of the states and tribes will also have a negative impact on those waters removed from requlation.  Aside from the negative impacts to water quality, the following negative health impacts to citizens and the degradation of natural systems, it is also and affront to states rights.  The Clean Water Act was written to allow states to enact rules and regulations more strict than federal laws.
There should be no degradation of the Clean Water Act if you care about the health of this countries natural resources or it's human population.
