Title: Comment submitted by Jim Steitz
Comment:
The Clean Water Act makes plain that states may veto Section 401 permits for infrastructure projects that cause gratuitous harm to aquatic ecosystems in that state, correctly reflected in the 1971 rule. The purpose of this clause is vividly demonstrated today. The authors of the Clean Water Act anticipated that one day, the EPA leadership offices would be occupied by scientifically and morally illiterate men, ambitious for power and favor, who say EPA’s mission is to unleash American industry. They would purge EPA of its responsibility to protect our streams and wetlands from economic interests, and instead join industry in shuffling along this destruction, disdainful and oblivious to the destruction of biodiversity, hydrological systems, and human health. This has now come to pass. They anticipated that one day, vapid, hand-waving appeals to nebulous concepts like ‘vital infrastructure,’ or ‘national interest,’ or this Administration’s fetish, ‘energy dominance,’ could be wielded to discard Americans’ love for the very things that make life worth living, and make America a worthwhile place to live. 

In my former home state of Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality vetoed the so-called ‘Jordan Cove Liquified Natural Gas’ Project in 2019 using its section 401 authority. This was a correct choice, and the people of Oregon and America are better off for it. EPA would define out of existence the capacity of Oregon to choose whether it wishes to be a sacrifice zone. This cautionary scenario is repeatable any time that a company with no care either for the people and life of a place, or for the future, draw a line on a map showing all to be bulldozed, erasing everything beautiful, precious, and valuable in the way. Section 401 is a tool for humans with values not measured in dollars or in barrels or cubic feet to assert their right to a quality of life, and to a healthy local ecosystem.

America has lost the majority of its wetlands already. The extinction crisis is especially pronounced in aquatic ecosystems. Simultaneously, extremes of flood and drought are magnified by climate change, whether this Administration believes it or not. Our remaining wetlands, aquifers, and tributary streams are more precious than ever in buffering these extremes. This Section 401 guardrail against combined, federal-corporate voracious power is more vital than ever. If EPA hands industry the tools to steamroll the love of Americans for our last aquatic ecosystems, the Trump Administration will leave our children with great ‘energy dominance,’ but a bleak, barren, meaningless world stripped of natural life, and a traumatic, harried struggle for survival between droughts and floods.

Title: Comment submitted by Emily Baker
Comment:
Attn: Administrator Lee Zeldin, and Lauren Kasparek, Oceans, Wetlands and Communities Division, Office of Water
Re: Docket # EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

Dear Sir and Madam:

My name is Emily, and I write as a concerned Michigan citizen. Living in the midst of a watershed to the world's largest source of fresh water, I am understandably concerned about planned changes to the Clean Water Act's certification registrations. I will refer to sections and pages of FRL-6976.2-01-OW from January 15 as needed. On that note, I second the statement from the Attorney Generals that this comment period should be extended for another month to grant a meaningful chance for comment. Below, I will list particular areas for concern.

First, the original definition involved "Activity" that could lead to polluting discharges (IV-B, par. 3, p. 2012). I worry that changing the definition to "discharge" alone (V-C-2, p. 2023-4, and V-C-2-v, p. 2026-7) might lead to allowing more harm, since much water pollution also comes from non-point sources such as urban runoff and rural fertilizers, and delayed point-source discharges that arise from processes, such as minerals eroded during construction.

I also worry about the narrowing of Waters of the U.S. to "navigable waters" alone, since many pollutants leach in via smaller streams, snowmelt, groundwater, and other sources from the land-based watershed.

Another concern is that amending the scope of who can address the application by changing "Neighboring Jurisdictional Leader/Regional Administrator" to simply "Administrator" (V-F, pp. 2031-2) would mean that any administrator could address this. I would prefer that regional administrators, who are close to the problem site and can more easily understand the situation, address regional problems, over those more remote with less vested interest in the site.

I understand the desire to streamline processes in the name of economic efficiency (VI-A, p. 2037), and belief that Executive Order 14192 is the easiest way to it (VII-B, P. 2038). However, the costs to local livelihoods and the long-term loss of materials for business might outweigh any short-term gains. While amending the act itself might not directly impact human health (VII-H, p. 2040), what these changes permit most assuredly could.

Many in our state and neighboring ones have a vested interest in protecting the Great Lakes and their adjoining ecosystems and communities. They tie into tourism, fishing, shipping, recreation, brewing, local traditions, and so much more. It is thus imperative that we leave the Clean Water Act as it is, even strengthen its effectiveness, so that we can continue to protect our waters for generations, and continue to thrive.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Title: Comment submitted by Walther Soeldner
Comment:
Dear Environmental Protection Agency: 

On behalf of myself and my family, I urge the EPA to reconsider its recent update of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act limiting the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and ultimately, clean, drinking water. 

We live in a region which depends on clean, natural waterways and water bodies, i.e., rivers and streams, lakes, ocean and inland seawater bodies. We are blessed with an abundance of them, and our state tribal governments have protected them over many years--for tribes over centuries. Local and regional athorities are best situated to offer protection because they know these waters best and they depend daily on their health.

Federal and state cooperation is foundational to America's system of government, and Section 401 has been critical to putting the public's interest above the wants of polluters. After recent rollbacks of federal clean water protections, state, tribal and local input has become even more necessary.

The Spokane River, the Skagit River, and above all the Columbia have depended upon and benefited from the wise historic protection from tribal nations and more recently from Washington State oversight.

The EPA's proposed rule weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protections and the ability of states and tribes to review and possibly block projects that may threaten public health and degrade the waterways and wetlands within their borders. I urge the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401.


Sincerely,

Title: Comment submitted by Erin Baumann
Comment:
Dear Administrator Zeldin, 

I’m writing as a concerned citizen to communicate my opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency's proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. This proposed rule would limit both state and Tribal governments ability to limit projects that threaten our valuable water resources– waterways, wetlands, and drinking water. 

The proposed rule seeks to undermine environmental protection and limit the rights of states and Tribes to protect their water resources. The current procedures set in the 2023 Rule allow for flexibility for state and Tribal governments to assess risks to their communities. The 2023 Rule certification process serves to prevent irreversible, costly damage to our waterways. This process would be reversed if the proposed rule is not amended. 

The Clean Water Act is so important because it seeks to protect one of the most valuable resources in our country. Water’s ability to flow and move underground, while vital for our survival, complicates regulation and protection. Someone or something will always be downstream of other’s actions. This proposed rule, while seeking to shorten and streamline the certification process for projects, decreases the ability to consider environmental and human health impacts on these proposed projects. Protecting our nation’s water resources should not be rushed.

Additionally, I am respectfully requesting that the comment period be extended beyond the current 30-day comment period. The monumentally important Clean Water Act and its proposed rules should be considered significant rulemaking and the public comment period should be at a minimum 60 days if not longer. As was stated in Section A of this Federal Register document, “the Agency welcomes comments on whether the proposed definition is necessary and addresses concerns”. 30 days is hardly sufficient time for all the interested parties to prepare and submit comments. 

I strongly urge EPA to not adopt the proposed rule changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Thank you for the opportunity to share my comment and concerns for the citizens of this country.

Best, 
A Concerned Citizen

Title: Comment submitted by Sierra Club Florida Chapter
Comment:
Public Comment Opposing Proposed Revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 Regulations — Florida Waters
Docket: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

I oppose EPA’s January 13, 2026 proposal to revise the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.

Florida demonstrates why Section 401 must remain strong. Our state sits on porous limestone karst geology where pollution travels rapidly through groundwater into springs, rivers, wetlands, estuaries, and drinking water aquifers. Contamination rarely stays at a discharge point — it moves through connected waters and affects communities far from the original source. Even small upstream discharges can degrade world-renowned Florida springs that depend on clean groundwater.

Section 401 allows states and Tribes to evaluate the full impacts of federally permitted projects and require conditions necessary to protect water quality. The proposed rule would improperly narrow that review to a limited discharge analysis and prevent consideration of broader and cumulative impacts. That approach ignores real-world hydrology and undermines the authority Congress intentionally preserved for states.

In Florida, upstream decisions directly affect downstream drinking water, fisheries, recreation economies, and public health — especially for rural and environmental justice communities. Limiting review shifts risk from project developers to residents and local governments.

EPA should withdraw the proposal and preserve a broad interpretation of Section 401 that allows meaningful review of entire projects and protects downstream waters, springs, and communities.

Respectfully submitted.

Cliff Thaell, Vice Chair, Sierra Club Florida Chapter

Title: Comment submitted by Deborah Socolar
Comment:
As a public health professional, I know how crucial it is for our policies to stress prevention and the precautionary principle. There is enormous evidence that your deregulatory approach will bring avoidable illness, suffering, and death. In addition, the damage to health will result in damage to the economy. 

To Administrator Zeldin,

I'm appalled at the EPA plan to undermine Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). I very strongly oppose the changes that the EPA now proposes. 

Under the Clean Water Act, states and tribes have a vital tool to help protect water quality fir their people. Your proposed changes to Section 401 would recklessly limit that scope. Those changes would block regulators from looking beyond the pollution discharged into waterways to evaluate the overall water quality impact of a project. 

States and authorized Tribes must be enabled to  determine the scope of review needed to protect water quality. They must not be restricted to a shrunken range of federally-protected waters and permitted only to examine direct effects of discharges into those waters. 

The broader water-quality effects caused by a project as a whole are what communities rely on the Clean Water Act to analyze and mitigate.

Far beyond the spot where discharge occurs, water quality can be damaged. Limiting the scope of any Section 401 analysis would block crucial reviews of the impacts upstream and downstream of planned projects. 

Under the changes that the EPA has proposed, states and Tribes could lose their ability to use Section 401 to protect many smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters. That would leave state and tribal decision-makers unable to block, or put conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters -- even if those conditions are vital to the safety of local drinking water supplies, recreation, flood control, or fisheries.

Restricting the scope and timeline of analyses would undermine the CWA's goal of ensuring that states, territories, and Tribes are enabled to protect their water resources. It would instead only benefit the corporations advancing project proposals. Taxpayers would wind up burdened by the costs of added cleanup of pollution that couldn't be mitigated because it didn't get analyzed due to the discharge only constraints. Such a restriction would mean approvals for dangerous projects that should not be permitted. Absent proper review, regulators and the public would very sadly fail to learn of harmful impacts and risks that would, under existing policies, be revealed during analyses that we currently benefit from.

The proposed change is unconscionable.
I urge the EPA to instead uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water and species habitat in waterways and wetlands, and protect the health of the public.

Title: Comment submitted by Marie Noel
Comment:
I write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to again
improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act). Section
401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources
from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative
federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Act in 1972 and is specifically designed
to empower states and Tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to
states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.
 
The proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state
and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, datacenters, and other
projects are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services they provide people and
communities. The harm done by the 2020 rule was substantially corrected in 2023 by a revised rule that
restored the proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act and the long-standing regulatory
structure that stood in place since 1971. In doing so, it also updated the rule to clarify that state and
Tribal reviews were appropriately limited to water related requirements and that there was a clear and
timely process for applicants to follow.
 
The proposed rule is  an unnecessary retread of the rejected 2020 rule. Of foremost
concern, it would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court
precedent confirming that meaning by limiting the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to
the “discharge from a point source” of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and
Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. For instance, it
could eliminate or undermine state and Tribes from being able to put in place protective measures so
that rivers and streams have adequate flow, habitat is protected, and designated uses like fishing and
recreation enjoyed by the public are not disturbed or destroyed.
It is hard to see this proposal as anything thing other than an unwarranted assault on clean water and
state and Tribal rights. Any concerns, which we feel were misplaced at the time, about uncertainty
around timing or process were addressed with the changes in the 2023 rule. The 2023 rule also makes it
clear that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. The
 combined result of these two proposed rules will be far fewer opportunities for state and Tribes to
protect their waters under Section 401 and far weaker options for them to do so in the limited number
of times it is triggered. This will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic
resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health,
the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative
consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them.
We strongly urge you not to move forward with this ill-conceived and unnecessary rule and keep the
existing 2023 rule in place.
Sincerely,
Marie Noel

Title: Comment submitted by B. Petersen
Comment:
For more than fifty years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has given states and Tribes real power to protect their water. It allows them to review major federal projects like pipelines, dams, mines, and power plants before permits are issued. When projects threaten drinking water, wetlands, or fisheries, communities can step in and demand changes or stop them outright.

This rule strips states and Tribes of meaningful review power, forces decisions through on an industry timetable, and clears obstacles for polluters that communities spent decades building.

Section 401 has been used to block or reshape fossil fuel pipelines, mining operations, and industrial facilities that would have contaminated water supplies or destroyed ecosystems. Weakening it hands the advantage to corporations that see clean water protections as a barrier to profit.

Frontline communities will now pay the price. Tribes lose one of the only tools they have to defend sacred waters and cultural sites. Rural towns like mine lose leverage to protect wells and rivers. Downstream communities are left dealing with contamination while corporations walk away with the profits.

Industry supporters openly praise this proposal because it makes pollution cheaper. Shorter reviews mean less scrutiny. Narrower standards mean fewer protections. Corporations get certainty and speed, while communities absorb the health risks, cleanup costs, and long-term damage.

This fight is about who controls our water. Local governments and Tribes understand their waterways, their ecosystems, and their people. Corporate lobbyists in Washington understand balance sheets. Section 401 has always been about keeping those priorities straight.

This proposal did not come out of nowhere. Pipeline companies, energy developers, data center operators, and industrial polluters have spent years lobbying to narrow Section 401. They want faster approvals, fewer conditions, and fewer chances for states or Tribes to say no.

We must not allow corporate polluters to rewrite clean water protections.

Title: Comment submitted by Kay Slama
Comment:
I write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to again improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act). Section 401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Act in 1972 and is specifically designed to empower states and Tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.
 
The proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, datacenters, and other projects are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services they provide people and communities. The harm done by the 2020 rule was substantially corrected in 2023 by a revised rule that restored the proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act and the long-standing regulatory structure that stood in place since 1971. In doing so, it also updated the rule to clarify that state and Tribal reviews were appropriately limited to water related requirements and that there was a clear and timely process for applicants to follow.
 
The proposed rule is by and large an unnecessary retread of the rejected 2020 rule. Of foremost concern, it would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court precedent confirming that meaning by limiting the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to the “discharge from a point source” of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. For instance, it could eliminate or undermine state and Tribes from being able to put in place protective measures so that rivers and streams have adequate flow, habitat is protected, and designated uses like fishing and recreation enjoyed by the public are not disturbed or destroyed.

It is hard to see this proposal as anything thing other than an unwarranted assault on clean water and state and Tribal rights. Any concerns, which we feel were misplaced at the time, about uncertainty around timing or process were addressed with the changes in the 2023 rule. The 2023 rule also makes it clear that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. Moreover, the Section 401 process rarely results in the denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming number of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.[JD2] 
As such, this proposed rule is not seeking to address any plausible problem. It also must be seen in relation to recent changes to the scope of Clean Water Act protections generally. Section 401 is only triggered when a federal permit or license involving a discharge to “waters of the United States” is at issue. In fact, most federal permits and licenses that trigger Section 401 are Section 404 discharge permits under the Clean Water Act itself. In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a decision in Sackett v. sEPA that dramatically reduced the number of waters covered by the federal Clean Water Act. Subsequently, this EPA is finalizing a rule that would even further limit the number of waters protected by the Act. If the proposed EPA rule governing “waters of the United States” is issued as proposed, it would result in an estimated 81% of historically federally protected wetlands lacking Clean Water Act protection[JD3] . Massive losses in protections under the Act for streams is also at issue. With these rollbacks, many projects will no longer need CWA permits, meaning that the trigger for Section 401 review for many projects will disappear altogether.   
The combined result of these two proposed rules will be far fewer opportunities for state and Tribes to protect their waters under Section 401 and far weaker options for them to do so in the limited number of times it is triggered. This will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health, the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them.
I strongly urge you not to move forward with this ill-conceived and unnecessary rule and keep the existing 2023 rule in place.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I appreciate the opportunity to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water.

I have a personal stake in this bill as I have spent years as a scout master teaching the youth values of land and water stewardship, something this bill is indirect opposition to. I am born and raised in Minnesota and I’ve seen how important our clean water is to everyone here. It is at the center of our tourism, and is a critical part of the culture. I have brought hundreds of kids upstate to experience the wonders of the boundary waters in trips that have taught them values of hard work, perseverance, and responsibility. These lands are so critical to so many young people‘s journeys and this bill would destroy these natural wonders.

The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. 

The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically:

1.) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations.

2.) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park.

3.) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water.

4.) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 

5.) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? 

The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. These activities are not just for fun, there are how we survive and central to our tourism industry that brings in millions of dollars a year. Not only with these social repercussions be huge, the economic ones would be devastating long term. 

Thank you for considering my comments,

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
The new rule, as proposed, would limit the abilities of states and tribes to review and consider broader water-quality effects caused by proposed projects when weighing certification. This would weaken the abilities of potentially affected parties and stakeholders to provide oversight of projects that may have water-quality effects not limited strictly to discharge.

The proposed rule would prevent states from protecting vital water resources such as smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters within their borders, leaving state regulators powerless to block, or place conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters, even if they are vital to local drinking water supplies, fisheries, recreation or flood control.

The new rule seeks to subvert the intent of the Clean Water Act by relying on overly strict definitions to remove the ability of states and tribes to steward water resources in the face of federally permitted projects. At a time when the Southwest is in the worst drought in over 1200 years, this is no time to roll back protections and tie the hands of the governments closest to, and most reliant on, these increasingly scarce and therefore valuable resources.

Please maintain the current rule as-is.

3.) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water.

4.) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 

5.) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? 

The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish.

Thank you for considering my comments,

Title: Comment submitted by Melissa Frady
Comment:
To Whom it may concern in regards to Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929:

I am a resident of Park County Montana, I live just north of Yellowstone National Park as well as the Absaroka Beartooth Wilderness. The Yellowstone River flows through Park County and my town of Livingston, Montana. The Yellowstone River and the many tributaries that feed it are a life source for my community. These waters (including groundwater and seasonal wetlands) supports the habitat of wildlife and plant species that I care about and recreate in. Having an intact ecosystem is important to me. These waters support local agriculture and a tourism economy that helps my community to thrive.

Statutory requirement 401(a)(1) requires meaningful opportunity for public comment. Codifying a comment period of no more than 30 days for EPA certifications (40 CFR 121.17(a)) contradicts 401(a)(1).  30 days is insufficient for meaningful review, analysis, and response for complex projects and/or associated technical documents. This is particularly true in rural and tribal communities where capacity for stakeholders to provide substantiative comments is limited by time and resources. 

I oppose codifying a comment period of no more than 30 days for EPA certifications. The EPA should retain flexibility to extend the period for public comment based on project complexity.

Limiting the scope of Section 401 certifications to “discharges” vs “activities” ignores non-point source pollution and does not consider the entire hydrology of the system in which the certification pertains to, such as construction runoff, erosion, impervious surfaces and sediment transport. 

For example: I reside less than 1/4 of a mile of a rail yard that is an active superfund cleanup following decades of contamination. As proposed in these changes, our aquifer would not be reviewable and my communities health would be compromised.

I oppose the proposed EPA change to define “discharge” for purposes of CWA section 401, at 40 CFR 121.1(c), as “a discharge from a point source into waters of the United States.” Contaminants in upstream, intermittent streams and wetlands directly impacts downstream water quality and drinking water. The proposed changes compromise the health and wellbeing of all by compromising water quality. 

Thank you for considering my comments,
Melissa Stringham Frady

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I am writing to oppose EPA’s proposed changes to Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401. These changes would weaken a core safeguard Congress deliberately included to ensure that federally permitted projects do not degrade water quality or undermine State and Tribal water quality standards. Section 401 is essential to the CWA’s purpose: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters (33 U.S.C. §1251(a)).

EPA states that the purpose of this proposed rule is to address “regulatory uncertainty and implementation challenges,” increase “transparency, efficiency, and predictability,” and align regulations with the scope of the Clean Water Act. I respectfully disagree that the proposed revisions will achieve these goals. Instead, the proposal appears to use the language of process improvement to justify a substantive narrowing of Section 401 authority that Congress intentionally granted to States and authorized Tribes.

EPA’s justification relies heavily on claims that stakeholders raised “questions” about the scope of certification under the 2023 Rule. However, disagreement from regulated entities about the breadth of Section 401 is not evidence of statutory ambiguity—it is often evidence that Section 401 is functioning as Congress intended: as a meaningful check to ensure federally permitted activities comply with water quality standards and other appropriate requirements of State and Tribal law. Section 401 was designed because federal permitting programs historically issued licenses and permits “without any assurance that the [water quality] standards will be met or even considered.” Narrowing certification scope in the name of “certainty” risks returning to the problem Congress sought to solve.

Furthermore, Section 401 is a preventative safeguard. The conditions and monitoring requirements that States and Tribes include are not administrative burdens; they are often the primary enforceable mechanisms ensuring compliance with water quality standards. If the proposed rule reduces the scope of conditions or narrows what can be considered, then “efficiency” is achieved only by shifting environmental risk to the public and downstream communities.

EPA states that the proposed rule will ensure States and Tribes “understand and adhere to their section 401 role.” This framing implies that States and Tribes have been acting outside their authority, when in fact Section 401 explicitly authorizes certifications to include “any effluent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure” compliance with the Clean Water Act and “any other appropriate requirement” of State or Tribal law (33 U.S.C. §1341(d)). Congress intentionally gave States and Tribes a strong role in protecting water quality within their borders, consistent with the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism structure. A rule that reinterprets this authority narrowly is a policy choice to reduce State and Tribal power.

The proposed revisions target core provisions, not minor procedural issues. They go to the heart of whether Section 401 remains an effective safeguard. If the scope of certification is narrowed, then conditions and denials become harder, even when water quality standards are at risk.

EPA’s cost-benefit framing is incomplete and biased toward short-term applicant savings as EPA states that it prepared an economic analysis to evaluate “cost savings and benefits” relative to the 2023 Rule. However, the costs of weakening Section 401 are not primarily borne by applicants—they are borne by the public through degraded water quality, increased downstream treatment costs, loss of aquatic habitat, increased flooding and sedimentation impacts, and long-term remediation obligations.

In addition, weakening Section 401 may increase conflict and litigation, undermining the predictability the Agency claims it is seeking. If State and Tribal authority is curtailed, affected jurisdictions and communities will have fewer tools to resolve water quality concerns early, increasing the likelihood of delays later through disputes and court challenges.

For these reasons, I urge EPA to withdraw the proposed changes and maintain a strong, workable Section 401 framework that protects water quality and respects State and Tribal authority.

Respectfully,

A citizen from Missouri

Title: Comment submitted by Katherine Davis
Comment: The EPA has failed to provide evidence to justify several proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. The document uses the recent SCOTUS overturning of Chevron as the excuse for revisiting Section 401, but that is misleading as it does not address the alleged burden of regulation. Rather, proposed changes seem to be yet another giveaway to developers by the Trump Administration, as the only argument in their favor appears to be fast-tracking projects. Furthermore, this particular bit is most concerning: "the Agency is proposing to remove the text currently located at 40 CFR 121.5(c) which allows State and Tribal certifying authorities to define additional contents in a request for certification, consistent with the Agency's rulemaking authority." Perhaps if the EPA demonstrated, over the past year, that it is acting in good faith, in the spirit of the creation of the EPA, which is to protect the environment and not concern itself with the earnings of shareholders, then the consolidation of power here would be less alarming. As it stands, stripping States and Tribes of further authority over waters that affect their citizens is in line with EPA's attempts to re-define WOTUS, deregulate chemicals because 'we don't know what they do,' and remove the independent research body within the organization that studied the effects of said chemicals/chemical pollution. Because the EPA has shown a blatant disregard for public health ever since Trump was sworn into office, there is no trust here. Tribes and States are not abusing their authority by participating in the process of approving/rejecting projects; rather, they are protecting their citizens' right to a good quality of life through safe access to clean water. When so much is at stake, both in the public health sense and the environment-at-large, with a project that can drastically affect waterways, there should be more regulation and research, not less. And its acceptance or rejection should certainly not be in the hands of a single (especially now) federal entity.

Title: Comment submitted by Abigail Johnson
Comment:
I have several concerns over the proposed changes in Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929. As a citizen of the United States of America I take my water quality very seriously as water is integral to human life. When reading the proposed rule changes in the docket, I came across several concerning items: 1.  limiting the comment period to “case by case basis”; 2. A repeal of treatment in a similar manner as a state or TAS for tribes.

The glibness of enacting a case by case basis for a comment period is an insult to the people.  The American people deserve to have consistent regulations regarding items, such as water, that are necessary for life. If the comment period is “generally 30 days” then just make the standard 30 days.

My larger concern when reading the docket was the disregard and open antagonism towards tribes. The docket states the following, “if they are interested in pursuing TAS for CWA” they will be redirected to 40 CFR 131.8. If the purpose of this docket is to save time why make the tribal requirements more difficult to navigate? Additionally, this docket is also discussing repealing tribal TAS for participation as a neighboring jurisdiction. This does not strike me as an agency trying to increase efficiency but rather an agency trying to cut out a specific group of people for a very specific purpose. Therefore, I looked for news articles discussing this topic and found something very interesting in the AP: “Congress has said that when federally backed projects like oil pipelines or nickel mines affect water quality, fishing and drinking water, states and tribes get to review them and reject proposals that will do too much damage, according to Moneen Nasmith, director of national climate, fossil fuel infrastructure at the nonprofit Earthjustice. “EPA’s claims that states and tribes are overreaching are baseless,” she said. “This proposed rule is trying to solve for a problem that does not exist.”” (https://apnews.com/article/water-trump-epa-section-401-data-centers-42716f21a37cadf624e2d7dee05fa3c3).  This docket is clear in its desire to remove or make participation challenging for tribal nations. I have taken it upon myself to include the following excerpt: “The [CWA] Act aims to improve water quality through regulatory programs that Tribal Nations can implement over their reservation waters.  Tribes have used the CWA to protect water quality for ceremonies, fisheries, and other Tribal uses as an act of Tribal governance and sovereignty.” (https://narf.org/intro-clean-water-act/).  As that quote makes clear from the Native American Rights Fund, clean water is integral to every single American life, and the CWA has been utilized appropriately by tribal nations to secure clean water. 
The docket section regarding TAS I believe under sells, the tribal popularity of the TAS system. Towards the end of the docket, it states the following: “In the July 2025 Federal Register notice, the Agency asked stakeholders for any data and information on their experiences with the 2023 Rule, including the provisions regarding TAS solely for section 401. 90 FR 29829. A few Tribes and Tribal associations expressed support for the TAS provisions, noting they provide a tool for Tribes with limited resources to protect their water quality[.]” I therefore went looking for information regarding this meeting and located “the summary report of tribal consultation on the US EPA: implementation challenges associated with the clean water act section 401.” This EPA document states the following: “Tribes noted that section 401 of the Clean Water Act is an important tool that empowers Tribes to protect their aquatic resources from potential adverse impacts of federally licensed or permitted projects. In their input, the Tribes unanimously support the definitions and procedures promulgated under the 2023 Rule, asserting the 2023 Rule had successfully facilitated Tribal water quality protections without creating implementation challenges or regulatory uncertainty. The Tribes urged the EPA to retain the current regulations and uphold its trust responsibility to Tribal nations. Several Tribes expressed their appreciation for the opportunity to share their experience with the EPA on the implementation of the 2023 Rule; however, several Tribes emphasized that face-to-face meetings between Tribal and EPA officials would help the EPA fully understand how revisions to Clean Water Act section 401 could impact treaty rights, Tribal communities, and Tribal resources.” (https://downloads.regulations.gov/EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929-0008/attachment_2.pdf). Unanimous support is what the 2023 rule has for tribal nations. I am extremely disappointed in the EPA for this docket and the proposed rule changes within it. Wanting safe and clean water for food, drinking and recreation is a human right.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
To the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency,
I am writing to oppose EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401I Water
Quality Certification regulations.
Section 401 is one of the most important tools States and Tribes have to ensure that federally
permitted projects comply with water quality standards and other protective requirements of state
law. Narrowing Section 401 review to a discharge-only framework would undermine long-standing
safeguards and increase pollution risks to downstream communities.
In Pennsylvania, Section 401 certification is routinely coordinated with reviews under the
Commonwealth’s Stream and Wetland Regulatory Program and Chapter 105 permitting.
Narrowing the scope of Section 401 review would constrain the Pennsylvania Department of
Environmental Protection’s ability to address erosion and sedimentation, wetland loss, flow
alterations, habitat degradation, and cumulative impacts, particularly for projects such as
pipeline crossings, dredge-and-fill activities, industrial redevelopment, and hydropower licensing.
Weakening this authority would exacerbate existing water quality impairments and threaten
drinking water supplies.
I urge EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and retain the 2023 Section 401 regulations.
Thank you for considering my comment.
Sincerely,
Display Properties (Name, Label, Tooltip: pageCount, Page Count, Number of pages In the content file

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
In 1972 Congress passed the Clean Water Act (CWA) with strong bipartisan votes to protect
America’s water resources and safeguard our nation’s public health. Yet, six decades later, the
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers (USACE)1
have proposed a new Waters of the United States (WOTUS) draft rule that threatens to undo the
significant progress our nation has made to restore the quality of our nation’s waters. The
proposed rule is legally unnecessary, scientifically unsound, and will harm public and
environmental health by allowing more harmful chemicals into our waterways. We urge
you to abandon this rulemaking and refocus your efforts on making Americans healthier.
EPA’s own analysis estimates that only 19 percent of the nation’s existing nontidal wetlands
would fall under WOTUS jurisdiction if the 2025 rule is codified as proposed, while other
studies find this number could range from 9 percent to 0 percent. The potential impact on other
headwaters, tributaries, and ephemeral water bodies remains to be evaluated.
By excluding all waters but those with continuous surface connections and which abut water
bodies, the proposed rule would improperly exclude from federal jurisdiction many discharges
that are functionally equivalent to discharges into jurisdictional waters. The ecology of these
important water bodies is inextricably tied to the water quality of traditionally navigable waters.
These water bodies are the capillaries and kidneys of the nation’s watersheds; when they are
polluted or filled in, harm flows downstream in the form of higher nutrient, sediment, and toxics
loads, all of which cumulatively degrade water quality throughout watersheds. This means more
flooding, more harmful algal blooms, and less filtration of pollutants. The new definition would
also absolve upstream polluters from the obligation to adopt responsible prevention measures,
and would instead shift the burden of managing pollution, flood, and drought onto the shoulders
of those who work and live downstream.
The administration’s proposed rule would cause EPA and USACE to fail to meet
Congress’s mandate, and Administrator Zeldin’s stated primary objective: to maintain
clean water for all Americans.
We all drink and use water. Municipal water utilities and their ratepayers—the American
people—will disproportionately bear the economic burden of remediating the poorer quality
water this rule will cause. Moreover, flood risk management and disaster response services will
become increasingly overburdened from the compounding impacts of cumulative upstream
watershed degradation. The proposed rule considers benefits to developers yet makes no
attempt to estimate or compare those theoretical benefits to the other costs to society from
the degradation that will occur to drinking water sources and aquatic ecosystems across
the country.
Further, this new rule jeopardizes wetlands and wildlife habitat that many Americans enjoy for
outdoor recreation and wildlife watching, which is very important to me.
EPA stated in its announcement that the proposed rule “support[s] the role of states and tribes as
primary regulators managing their own land and water resources.” Yet states are not helped by
the federal government’s abdication of its statutory responsibilities. Because of EPA’s
abandonment of responsibility to protect clean water, more of the onus will fall on the
states to enforce state-level protections of clean water—protections that can vary greatly
state-by-state. States will be forced to pick up the slack, which runs the risk of straining
state budgets and environmental enforcement resources.
As proposed, the 2025 draft WOTUS rule ignores science, removes vast swaths of aquatic areas
from federal jurisdiction, fails to protect water quality, and passes the costs on to the American
taxpayer. It does not simplify the ability of the agency to identify jurisdictional waters. There are
reasonable policies we could pursue to simplify permitting and create union jobs in this country,
but this proposed rule does not represent a viable path forward.
I urge you to immediately abandon this unnecessary and statutorily
unfaithful rulemaking that will harm public health and to instead
refocus your efforts on protecting the American people’s right to
clean and safe water.
I know that you’ll make the right decision, which is to abandon this
new proposed rule. Thank you

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I cannot imagine why we would rush to reduce oversight of water quality. We are talking about the water we all drink! Not to mention the health/wellbeing of our beautiful state's ecosystems. This proposal treats state and Tribal protections as silly inconveniences standing in the way of industry timeliness. There is no industry project so essential that we should cut corners on oversight. Who does this benefit? Certainly not the residents of Washington State. 

The changes proposed are dressed up as "clarity" but ignores basic science by limiting review to "point source discharges" and barring states from considering broader project impacts on rivers and wetlands. There are experts at the state and Tribal levels who have the knowledge and experience to accurately assess and evaluate the far-reaching possible effects for proposed industrial projects. The Supreme Court rejected this narrow reading in PUD No. 1 (1994).

Further, it strips the state of the ability to request necessary information and makes it harder for Tribes to certify their own programs. This hands power back to the very entities being regulated. These systems are in place to PROTECT us from  these entities. Individuals and businesses  with sufficient capital have historically always taken advantage of natural resources if there aren't legal systems in place to stop them. 

I would like to finish this by saying that clean water is not a nuisance to be managed so a pipeline can be built on schedule. Speed is not the only virtue.

Please withdraw this proposal. It has not been written for the good of the people, it has been written to limit our ability to protect ourselves and our incredible state. Absolutely not.

Title: Comment submitted by Robin Muller
Comment:
EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
The Proposed Rule would eliminate regulation facilitating Tribal certification authority for treatment as a state. The Rule would erase all existing regulatory language that supports neighboring/downstream Tribes in requiring conditions or denial of Federal permits that would cause violations of Reservation water quality requirements.
The Proposed Rule would prevent any State or Tribe from setting conditions on Federal “general permits” that apply across the United States. No conditions could be set to prevent degradation and no Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters or Outstanding Resource Value waters could be protected.
The Proposed Rule would tighten a noose around the scope of certification to prevent conditions on or denial of permits, even if they would pollute and degrade lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or contaminated seepage through groundwater into surface water.
The Proposed Rule would force involuntary waiver of certification if the State or Tribal process was not completed within one-year, even if the extra time was needed due to public process, lack of sufficient information to make a decision, or even acts of God.
The Proposed Rule would allow project applicants to exploit the new forced waiver Rule by requesting certification of its “draft” application and incomplete water-related data. The polluter could then do a bait-and-switch and disclose the real plan and its impacts on water after the one-year time limit on certification had ended.
The Proposed Rule would centralize review of State and Tribal certification in the Administrator or its designee. That political appointee would have the power to determine that an entire category of discharge would never have an “affect” on downstream States or Tribes

Title: Comment submitted by Alexis McCarter
Comment: The undersigned oppose the proposed EPA rule aimed at updating and clarifying the requirements for water quality certification under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. While the intention to streamline the certification process may seem beneficial, this rule threatens to undermine critical environmental protections, federal oversight, and the rights of states and tribes to safeguard their waters. It appears to restrict the capacity of states and tribes to fully exercise their authority to review discharges into waters of the United States, potentially leading to weaker protections against pollution and harm to aquatic ecosystems. By prioritizing faster and more predictable permitting processes, the rule risks diminishing environmental safeguards, which could result in increased pollution, adverse public health impacts, and long-term ecological damage. Additionally, the language of the proposal creates ambiguities that may lead to legal disputes and delays, discouraging responsible development while compromising water quality standards. Although respecting state and tribal authority is important, federal oversight under the Clean Water Act serves as a vital safeguard to ensure consistent and adequate protections nationwide. The proposed rule, as drafted, may weaken this federal role and undermine the comprehensive framework designed to protect water resources for all Americans. Furthermore, the process by which this rule has been developed appears to lack sufficient public participation and environmental review, raising concerns about transparency and accountability. Protecting our water resources requires a balanced approach that supports sustainable development while maintaining strong environmental safeguards. This proposed rule tilts too far toward facilitating permits at the expense of water quality protections, and we strongly urge the EPA to reconsider and revise the proposal to uphold the integrity of the Clean Water Act for current and future generations.

Title: Comment submitted by Adelle Welch
Comment:
To Whom It May Concern,
My name is Adelle Welch, and I am a resident of Livingston, MT. I have an undergraduate degree in Earth and Climate Science and currently teach preschool in Livingston. I am writing to comment on the proposed change to Section 401 Request for Certification. Specifically I strongly oppose removing the text located at 40 CFR 121.5(c) that allows State and Tribal certifying authorities to define additional contents in a request for certification, and the proposal to add a definition for “discharge” at 40 CFR 121.5(c) that reduces the definition of discharge to “a point source into waters of the United States”. 
In Livingston, my family and I rely heavily on water from Shields River, a snowmelt driven river fed by many ephemeral streams, and groundwater for our everyday use, irrigation, and to support our animals. Additionally, my community depends on the same river, groundwater, and the Yellowstone River which is fed in a similar manner to the Shields, for our outdoor tourism (fly fishing especially) and agriculture industries, as well as everyday needs like drinking water. Water is a scarce and hotly contested resource. Currently, a low snow year is already presenting anticipatory anxieties for people throughout the county who depend on snow melt to replenish aquifers, and provide water for the summer (our dry season). 
Since much of the water my community depends on, i.e. groundwater, ephemeral streams, wetlands or other waters with no continuous surface connection, are no longer defined as a water of the United States (2023 “Revised Definition of Waters of the United States”) defining “discharge” as from a point source into waters of the United States further removes protection around groundwater as pollution to groundwater happens not just through point sources but through pervious surfaces. 
My family and I live adjacent to a small pond that provides habitat and nesting grounds for many migrating birds, and water for farmland irrigation. This pond is connected to the Shields River underground and in low water years has dried up. It currently has no federal protection since there is no continuous surface connection. If there were to be a project near this pond which safely discharges pollution from a point source into the Shields River (a legal water of the U.S.), the pond and the Shields River would still be vulnerable to any untreated discharge into the pond or through pervious surfaces due to the connectivity of the system. 
An attempt to clarify language, in this context, will provide a loophole for pollution to reach vital waters in communities across the US. The interconnectivity of water systems must be taken into account when approving projects for certification and “discharge from a point source” leaves other pathways for water pollution, be it into “waters of the United States” or other vital water resources. 
Additionally, I oppose the proposal to remove the text at 40 CFR 121.5(c) which “allows State and Tribal certifying authorities to define additional contents in a request for certification”. By making the federal regulations the only standard for Clean water and pollution, the EPA is denying states rights and tribes rights to protect water resources like groundwater that are outside of the scope of the Clean Water Act.

Thank you for your time and for reading this comment.

Sincerely,

Adelle Welch

    • The Proposed Rule would force involuntary waiver of certification if the State or Tribal process was not completed within one-year, even if the extra time was needed due to public process, lack of sufficient information to make a decision, or even acts of God.

    • The Proposed Rule would allow project applicants to exploit the new forced waiver Rule by requesting certification of its “draft” application and incomplete water-related data. The polluter could then do a bait-and-switch and disclose the real plan and its impacts on water after the one-year time limit on certification had ended.

    The Proposed Rule would centralize review of State and Tribal certification in the Administrator or its designee. That political appointee would have the power to determine that an entire category of discharge would never have an “affect” on downstream States or Tribes.

Title: Comment submitted by Don Carlson
Comment:
I really care about our planet and i don't want our beautiful places to disappear

Dear Administrator Zeldin,

I am concerned by the EPA proposal to weaken Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA) and strongly oppose the proposed changes. 

For a long time, I thought pollution was just trash scattered on the ground. But seeing it in real life,  and truly understanding its impact, made me realize it is far more serious.
One warm day in Honolulu, my dad and I were walking toward the beach. The city felt alive. People played music, danced traditional Hawaiian dances, carved wood, and laughed together. The sweet smell of taro bread, grilled chicken, fresh pineapple, and cooked fish filled the air. We were having a whale of a time.
But when we reached the beach, everything changed.                            
A horrible, trash-filled smell hit us like we had stepped into an old dumpster. A rusty drainage canal stretched from the city straight into the ocean, pouring filthy water and debris into the waves. Bottles, sewage, and garbage floated on the surface, spreading through the once-beautiful blue water. Watching something so ugly invade something so beautiful was heartbreaking.
The beach felt dull and silent. Plastic bottles and metal scraps littered the stiff sand. The waves splashed dirty foam back onto the shore. Even the seagulls were quiet. The smell of dead fish hung heavily in the air.
In that moment, I realized how fragile nature truly is, and how easily human carelessness can destroy it. Just minutes before, the city had been full of life and joy. Now I was standing in a place that felt abandoned and damaged.
If we dont act now, even the most beautiful places on Earth could disappear.
Our oceans and beaches are drowning in pollution. Every piece of trash left behind harms wildlife, poisons marine life, and destroys natural beauty. I care about stopping pollution because I dont want future generations to grow up without ever experiencing clean beaches, clear water, and thriving ocean life.
If we work together, we can protect the beauty of our planet before its too late.

I call on the EPA to use its authority to uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water, public health, and species habitat in waterways and wetlands.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
As someone who is about to graduate with an undergraduate degree in environmental science I can see several issues with these proposed regulations. The most obvious issue is the inconsistency in the changes. The new regulations say they want to give states and tribes more power in regulating their own waterways, however, the section 401 changes actually give states less power in determining what projects will harm their state’s water. This seems designed to give the federal government the ability to bypass any states which wish to implement more strict regulations, which undermines the purpose of giving the states more choice in the regulations. 
    Furthermore, the idea of giving states more power over their regulations is a flawed idea to begin with, given that the waterways in one state affect waterways in another. This means that pollution resulting from one state deregulating, may end up in the waterways of a state which wishes to keep their water clean. Having inconsistent regulation policy from state to state will cause disproportionate negative outcomes for states who wish to keep strict regulations.
     Reclassifying wetlands, thus protecting significantly less of them, will also be absolutely disastrous for America. Wetlands are absolutely vital in providing ecosystem services such as maintaining biodiversity and water quality and providing flood control. In recent decades we have seen a significant loss of biodiversity leading to entire ecosystems being in danger. The destruction of more wetlands will only make this problem worse. There has also been worsening extreme weather events recently, leading to more flooding. Wetlands are key in controlling floods. While deregulation may make some companies money in the short term, it will cost communities and the government more in the long term.

Title: Comment submitted by Kirk Davidson
Comment:
I am writing this as a citizen who is concerned by the EPA proposal to weaken Section 401 on the CWA.
I am in strong opposition to the proposal identified as EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929-0001. This proposal will increase pollution risks, create regulatory uncertainty (perpetuating bad actors), undermine public health and economic stability, and contract scientific evidence. 
The Clean Water Act was enacted with a clear, bipartisan mandate: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. For over five decades, that framework has protected drinking water sources, wetlands, streams, and aquatic ecosystems that millions of Americans depend on. Weakening its implementation—whether through narrowed definitions, reduced oversight, diminished enforcement, or relaxed standards—directly undermines that statutory purpose.
Water pollution has serious impacts, and any decision that threatens to increase pollution, harm public health, or exacerbate impacts to habitat needs to be analyzed and weighed. Waterways are not solely impacted in the spot where discharge occurs and limiting the scope of the Section 401 analysis would result in a lack of review of project impacts upstream and downstream. With the changes proposed, states and Tribes could lose the ability to use Section 401 to protect many smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters within their borders. This would render state and tribal decision makers powerless to block, or place conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters, even if they are vital to local drinking water supplies, fisheries, recreation, or flood control.
Limiting the scope and timeline for analysis to take place fails to achieve the CWA goal of ensuring states, territories, and tribes are empowered to protect their water resources and instead only benefits the companies behind the proposed projects. It leaves taxpayers on the hook for additional cleanup of pollution that was unable to be mitigated because it was not analyzed under a ‘discharge only’ scenario. It opens the door for projects that should not be permitted due to impacts and risks that would become clear during analysis but are never properly reviewed. 
The Environmental Protection Agency was created to safeguard human health and the environment. Retreating from that mission erodes public trust and places short-term regulatory convenience over long-term national resilience.
I respectfully urge the Agency to withdraw or substantially revise this proposal. Any modification to Clean Water Act implementation must strengthen, not weaken, protections for the waters that sustain our communities, ecosystems, and economy.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes

Dear Administrator Zeldin,

I appreciate the opportunity to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water.

The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. 

The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically:

1.) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations.

2.) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park.

3.) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water.

4.) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 

5.) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? 

The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish.

Thank you for considering my comments,

Title: Comment submitted by Mari Jensen
Comment:
RE: Clean Water Act Section 401 Proposed Rule: Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

Thank you for the opportunity to provide comment on the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA’s) proposed rule Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule) pertaining to implementation of Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401.

I oppose the proposed rule. 

This proposed rule takes away the right of my state, the State of Arizona, to protect my water resources – and does the same for all other states and tribes. Doing so clearly contravenes what Congress intended. 

The Clean Water Act states: “It is the policy of the Congress to recognize, preserve, and
protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution, to plan the development and use (including restoration, preservation, and enhancement) of land and
water resources … “

Section 401 of the Clean Water Act gives states and tribes power to set water standards. Section 401 gives states and tribes the power to impose conditions on Federal permits that impair water standards set by states and tribes. 

My recommendation: reject the current proposed change and retain the current scope of review established in the 2023 Rule.

Sincerely yours,

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
To Whom It May Concern:

I am writing to formally oppose the proposed revisions to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.

Section 401 has long served as a critical safeguard that allows states and authorized Tribes to review federally permitted projects to ensure compliance with water quality standards. This cooperative federalism structure reflects Congress’s clear intent to preserve state authority in protecting local waters and the public health of their communities.

The proposed revisions would significantly narrow the scope of review under Section 401 and limit the ability of states and Tribes to evaluate the full range of impacts associated with federally licensed projects. Such limitations would undermine the very purpose of the Clean Water Act: “to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.”

Public Health Concerns

Water quality protections are directly linked to public health outcomes. Reduced oversight of discharge-related impacts increases the risk of contamination from heavy metals, PFAS (“forever chemicals”), petroleum byproducts, and other industrial pollutants. Communities downstream from energy, mining, or infrastructure projects often bear disproportionate health burdens, including increased risks of cancer, neurological disorders, reproductive harm, and respiratory disease.

Section 401 has historically allowed states to impose conditions that prevent these harms before they occur. Weakening this authority would shift the burden from prevention to remediation—an approach that is both more costly and less effective.

Environmental Consequences

The narrowing of review authority would limit the consideration of cumulative and project-wide impacts to wetlands, groundwater connectivity, fisheries, and watershed integrity. Degradation of wetlands and aquatic ecosystems reduces natural flood control, impairs biodiversity, and weakens climate resilience.

Section 401 has been instrumental in protecting salmon habitats, coastal estuaries, and drinking water sources across multiple states. Curtailing state review risks long-term ecological damage that cannot easily be reversed.

Long-Term Corporate and Economic Impacts

While the proposed revisions are framed as a means of “streamlining” permitting, reducing environmental oversight often results in greater regulatory and financial uncertainty in the long run. Projects approved under weakened review standards are more likely to face:

- Increased litigation from states, Tribes, and affected communities
- Reputational damage and investor scrutiny related to environmental, social, and governance (ESG) concerns
- Cleanup liabilities and remediation costs
- Insurance and risk-pricing increases
- Supply chain disruptions tied to environmental degradation

Corporations ultimately benefit from clear, durable, and locally informed permitting standards. Weakening Section 401 may accelerate approvals in the short term, but it creates instability and risk exposure that can negatively affect corporations, shareholders, and long-term economic growth.

Federalism and Tribal Sovereignty

Section 401 reflects a careful balance between federal authority and state and Tribal sovereignty. Limiting the scope of review erodes that balance and diminishes the ability of sovereign governments to protect treaty rights, drinking water resources, and local ecosystems.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the Agency to withdraw or substantially revise the proposed changes and preserve the existing scope and authority under Section 401.

Protecting water quality is not an obstacle to economic development—it is foundational to sustainable prosperity, public health, and intergenerational environmental stewardship.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Title: Comment submitted by Nina Schoch
Comment:
Re: Opposition to Proposed Rule Amending Clean Water Act Section 401 Submitted February 17, 2026 

Dear Administrator Zeldin:

I am writing to strongly oppose U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule, published on 1/13/26, to amend regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). The Clean Water Act has been essential in improving water quality across our nation, and section 401 is a vital tool to protect water quality and aquatic ecosystems from pollution. 

Growing up in the 1960’s and 1970’s, I remember when waterways across America were severely polluted, impacting drinking water, as well as human, wildlife, and ecosystem health. And, having lived in New York’s six-million acre Adirondack Park for several decades, I have seen how polluted waters, including acid deposition, mercury contamination, have affected the health of human, fish, and wildlife populations. 

Thanks to the critical water quality safeguards implemented under the CWA, particularly those under section 401, and other regulations, we have significantly improved the quality of American waters in my lifetime, greatly benefiting human and wildlife health. Clean waters are crucially important for human health, livelihoods, and the quality of life in and beyond the Adirondacks.

I am deeply concerned that the proposed rule would substantially weaken State and Tribal authority to protect our waters, and reverse important protections for public and environmental health. Section 401 is one of the most effective safeguards for American waters, as it maintains a cooperative framework in protecting water quality by empowering States and Tribes with the ability to review and provide input into federal federally permitted or licensed projects, such as dams and industries, to ensure compliance with water quality standards on local and regional levels. 

The proposed rule is particularly concerning, given the ongoing reduction in CWA protections for wetlands and streams across the United States. Maintaining the current Section 401 is a critical remaining mechanism for States and Tribes to safeguard water quality in their areas. The proposed rule also reduces the scope of State and Tribal review of a federal permit from the full activity to only the “discharge from a point source,” which would undermine their ability to protect water quality in their communities. Additionally, the proposed rule would restrict access of States and Tribes to needed information to evaluate the permitting of a project, weakens their authority, and changes the definition of water quality requirements, resulting in reducing essential protections to maintain the health of the environment. 

Again, I strongly oppose the proposed rule to amend Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, as it will reduce the rights of States and Tribes to protect their own waters and jeopardize long-time efforts to improve the health of our waters, leading to changes in water flows, hindering restoration efforts, and increased pollution. 

I fervently urge the Environmental Protection Agency to withdraw this proposed rule and retain the 2023 Section 401 regulations, which provide a balance of regulatory clarity with the Clean Water Act’s fundamental goal of restoring and maintaining the health and biological integrity of our Nation’s aquatic ecosystems. 

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this critically important issue affecting the waterways of our nation.

Sincerely, 
~N. Schoch, DVM, MS
Wildlife Conservation Consulting, LLC
