Title: Comment submitted by Armanda Roco
Comment:
I strongly oppose EPA’s 2026 proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 regulations. The rule would sharply narrow state and Tribal authority by limiting certifications to only point‑source discharges into waters of the United States, removing their long‑standing ability to evaluate the broader impacts of an entire project. 
For decades, states and Tribes have relied on Section 401’s broader “activity‑wide” review to ensure that federal projects comply with applicable water quality requirements and do not endanger public health or critical water resources. This authority is essential because many harmful project impacts—such as habitat disruption, cumulative watershed effects, and operational risks—extend beyond a single discharge.

The proposed rule would also reduce state and Tribal discretion by limiting their ability to address non‑discharging aspects of major infrastructure projects, benefiting developers at the expense of communities. Such restrictions undermine the intent of Section 401, which Congress designed as a direct grant of power to states and Tribes to protect water quality within their borders.

Weakening Section 401 in this way jeopardizes the health and safety of residents who rely on state and Tribal agencies to prevent environmentally harmful or ethically questionable projects from advancing without proper scrutiny. I urge EPA to withdraw this proposal and maintain the broader review authority restored in the 2023 rule.

Title: Comment submitted by Coalition to Dismantle the Doctrine of Discovery
Comment:
Dear Administrator Zeldin:

We write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to again improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act). Section 401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted development. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.

Our organization, the Coalition to Dismantle the Doctrine of Discovery, is made up of churches, religious groups, and individuals who support Indigenous sovereignty because of our faith. We are concerned that the proposed changes to the Clean Water Act will have clear and important adverse effects on Tribal Nations’ ability to protect their waters, as the Native American Rights Fund (NARF) has reported. We are also deeply concerned because the EPA has reported that in their consultation process, Tribes that provided input unanimously opposed revisions to the scope of certification and related definitions. 

We believe that all communities deserve safe, reliable water free from contamination. Section 401 is a vital safeguard that allows states and Tribal governments to protect their waters from harmful federally permitted projects. Weakening this authority would put drinking water sources, rivers, wetlands, and downstream communities at risk while making it easier for polluting projects to move forward without accountability. 

This proposal comes immediately after EPA’s rollback of federal water protections under the Waters of the United States. Together, these actions undermine the Clean Water Act’s purpose and threaten water quality, public health, and Indigenous sovereignty. 

We urge the EPA to withdraw this proposal and preserve strong Section 401 authority.

Sincerely,
The Coalition to Dismantle the Doctrine of Discovery

Title: Comment submitted by Susan Baumgartner
Comment:
Public Comment on Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Submitted by: Susan Baumgartner, Waukesha, Wisconsin
Date: February 17, 2026

I am a Wisconsin resident submitting this comment in strong opposition to EPA's proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.

Wisconsin is a state defined by its water with numerous types of enormous volumes. And yet we are seeing firsthand what we can easily lose without strong regulation.  Our communities — including many Tribal nations whose treaty rights, subsistence practices, and sovereign interests are inseparable from these waterways — depend on clean water not just economically, but culturally and spiritually. I support their ways of managing our resources. The Clean Water Act as is does a fairly decent job of supporting them as well. We need it and more. 

Section 401 exists for exactly this context. It ensures that when a federally permitted project — a pipeline, dam, mine, or data center — threatens local water quality, states and Tribes have the authority to say so and act on it. This proposed rule would gut that authority by narrowing certification review to direct discharge impacts only, ignoring the broader water quality consequences that large infrastructure projects routinely cause.

For Wisconsin's Tribal nations, this is particularly serious. Their ability to exercise Section 401 authority is tied directly to treaty-protected rights to fish, gather, and use waterways that their communities have depended on for generations. Weakening that authority isn't streamlining — it's overriding sovereign protections that federal law is supposed to honor.

EPA should withdraw this proposal. It subordinates the water quality judgments of the people who know these waters best to the convenience of federal permitting timelines, and that is the opposite of what the Clean Water Act was built to do.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I write this comment in strong opposition to the proposal, as it does not align with the
values the EPA claims to be upholding. Under this revision of the Clean Water Act
section 401, the state’s authority to ensure clean water ways would be greatly reduced
rather than strengthened. Congress granted authority to states in section 401, to issue a
permit or license on activities that could result in discharge into WOTUS in 1971. By
changing the language of this section, states no longer hold much authority to ensure
the quality of water within each state's jurisdiction. Only allowing for point-source
discharge certification authority misses a majority of pollution that could enter the water
ways. According to the EPA’s own basic information regarding point sources, “States
report that nonpoint source pollution is the leading remaining cause of water quality
problems” (US EPA 2022). It is counterproductive to remove a state's ability to limit
nonpoint source discharge into WOTUS, while claiming to be following the EPA mission
to “Protect human health and the environment”.
This proposal is only meant for furthering infrastructure and other developments while
negating the health of the environment and potential impacts the developments could
have on surrounding ecosystems. Public health and environmental health should be at
the forefront of regulations put in place by the EPA, in order to ensure all parts of
society. Instead this proposal’s protection is covering only the select few who benefit
from quick approvals with little environmental and societal considerations.

Title: Comment submitted by M. Frances Eargle
Comment:
-	The EPA has provided for an unusually short 30 day comment period. Many environmental groups have requested additional time to more fully comment on this rule but the EPA chose not to extend the public comment period. 

-	Section 401 of the Clean Water Act is a major regulatory tool to restore state control of waters and protecting water quality. 

-	These changes outlined by EPA in the proposed rule-making takes the opposite approach, restricting and preempting state and tribal authority to protect their waters. 

-	The proposed rule would establish requirements and standards for state and tribal water quality certification under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act that inappropriately restricts the scope and timing of this important avenue for states and tribes to protect their waters. 

-	These proposed limitations are inappropriate and are inconsistent with the statute. Under the statute, Section 510, the states have the right to establish requirements which include procedural and substantive standards controlling water pollution. Section 313(a), waives sovereign immunity, making federal agencies subject to state authorities and permit requirements. This action proposed by EPA is unconstitutional and tramples on states' rights. It preempts Supreme Court decisions which have repeatedly defended states and tribes authority to protect water quality (PUD vs. EPA ) . It also violates the tenants of federalism.

-	Taken together, under the CWA federal agencies are subject to state water pollution and water quality requirements whether “substantive or procedural.” Section 401 is written broadly for authorizing states and tribes, among other things, to include conditions in the certification related to “any requirement of state law” and should be construed and applied in a manner that enables states to apply their standards as fully as the statute allows. 

-	I recommend EPA continuing to use the existing Section 401 regulations.

Title: Comment submitted by Martin and Sharon McGladdery
Comment:
EPA Wants to Weaken Water Protections for Line 5 and Other Pipelines

A new pro-industry attack on the states from the Trump administration could directly impact the outcome of Michigan's Clean Water Act, Part 401, permit decision for the proposed Enbridge Line 5 oil tunnel, particularly if the decision is further delayed.

The Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) new rule was proposed last Tuesday and seeks to limit the ability of states and tribes to protect waters from oil pipelines, including Line 5. Under current law, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act gives states and tribes the authority to decide whether to certify issues around water quality, and as a result limits the ability of federal agencies to issue permits. If the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy (EGLE) issues a Section 401 permit decision on the tunnel before the final rule is published, the denial would be evaluated under the existing rule, although it could still give Enbridge a new argument if the decision was challenged in court. The same holds true if the state issues a permit with conditions. The state's 401 permit is key to the future of the tunnel and is required before the Army Corps can issue a final federal permit. Right now the EPA says it hopes the new rule will be finalized this spring. 

EGLE has set February 28 as the newest deadline for a 401 permit decision, which was initially supposed to happen by the end of January.

Title: Comment submitted by Erin K. (no surname provided)
Comment:
For Arizona water rights owners, the concern is fairly simple; how a waterbody is classified can determine whether they get any real protection during federal permitting. Many Arizona users rely on washes, streams, or connected waters that may or may not be treated as federally protected depending on how they are labeled on a map. If a water source tied to an existing right is classified out, the state may have little ability to review or condition upstream discharges. The right still exists, but there are fewer options to protect it as new projects are approved.

This proposal also shifts more responsibility onto local water users to protect themselves. By narrowing the scope of review, impacts such as reduced water availability or cumulative effects may no longer be considered at all. When this happens, water rights owners are left to pursue separate state remedies, which takes time and money. For smaller agricultural or municipal users, that added burden is real, and it means long standing rights may become harder to defend.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:

The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources.

Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections.

Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations.

I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship.

Title: Comment submitted by Molly Pluenneke

I urge you to reject these proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 regulations. Restricting the necessary scope of review authority fundamentally threatens water quality and effective monitoring through local governance.

Section 401 certifications are used by States and authorized tribes to evaluate cumulative impacts on water quality, including changes in flow, habitat, wetland function, and water-quality conditions within watersheds. 

These factors fundamentally shape ecosystem health, meaning a project’s impact on water quality cannot be adequately evaluated if its review focuses only on point-source discharges. Additionally, the proposed restriction diminishes the role of states and tribes, who serve as place-based stewards with vital local and Indigenous water knowledge of water systems.

Effective water quality protection requires cooperative governance. Narrowing state and tribal authority under Section 401 shifts power away from local decision-makers and limits their ability to respond to site-specific and cumulative environmental impacts.

In addition, I urge the Agency to extend the public comment period for this proposed rule to the traditional 60-day timeframe. Given the importance of Section 401 certification to state, tribal, and local water resource governance, and the potentially devastating ecological impact of its revision, additional time is needed to understand the proposal's implications.

I urge the Agency to reject the proposed revisions and preserve robust Section 401 review authority so that states and tribes can continue to protect water quality in ways that reflect real ecological systems.

Title: Comment submitted by Caleb Merendino
Comment:
Honorable Administrator Zeldin,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed changes to Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certifications. For more than five decades, Section 401 has been essential to cooperative federalism, allowing states, Tribes, and the federal government to jointly steward shared water resources while ensuring that local expertise and priorities are respected. The current proposal—advanced in response to an effort to expedite oil and gas infrastructure rather than to strengthen water protections—runs counter to both the intent and the plain language of the Clean Water Act and would significantly erode long-standing state and Tribal authority.

Water Quality Certification remains one of the most effective mechanisms available for states and Tribes to ensure that federally licensed or permitted projects do not compromise water quality for drinking, recreation, or ecological health. By limiting the scope of conditions that states and Tribes may impose, the proposed rule would substantially weaken their ability to prevent or mitigate harm from activities that pose real risks to local waters.

The proposal would also impose unreasonable constraints on the review process by narrowing timelines and limiting access to critical information. States and Tribes must be afforded sufficient time and complete application materials to evaluate complex projects, particularly given the wide variation in water bodies, environmental conditions, and regulatory standards across jurisdictions. Anything less undermines meaningful review and effective protection.

As recently as 2023, the EPA reaffirmed the central role of states and Tribes in safeguarding water quality—an approach that has been foundational to Clean Water Act implementation for the past 55 years. There is no justification for reversing course now. I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposal and instead maintain and respect the established authority of states and Tribes to protect the waters within their borders.

Respectfully,

Caleb Merendino

The purpose of this clause is vividly demonstrated today. The authors of the Clean Water Act anticipated that one day, the EPA leadership offices would be occupied by scientifically and morally illiterate men, ambitious for power and favor, who say EPA's mission is to unleash American industry. They would purge EPA of its responsibility to protect our streams and wetlands from economic interests, and instead join industry in shuffling along this destruction, disdainful and oblivious to the destruction of biodiversity, hydrological systems, and human health. This has now come to pass. They anticipated that one day, vapid, hand-waving appeals to nebulous concepts like ‘vital infrastructure,' or ‘national interest,' or this Administration's fetish, ‘energy dominance,' could be wielded to discard Americans' love for the very things that make life worth living, and make America a worthwhile place to live. 

In my former home state of Oregon, the Department of Environmental Quality vetoed the so-called ‘Jordan Cove Liquified Natural Gas' Project in 2019 using its section 401 authority. This was a correct choice, and the people of Oregon and America are better off for it. EPA would define out of existence the capacity of Oregon to choose whether it wishes to be a sacrifice zone. This cautionary scenario is repeatable any time that a company with no care either for the people and life of a place, or for the future, draw a line on a map showing all to be bulldozed, erasing everything beautiful, precious, and valuable in the way. Section 401 is a tool for humans with values not measured in dollars or in barrels or cubic feet to assert their right to a quality of life, and to a healthy local ecosystem.

America has lost the majority of its wetlands already. The extinction crisis is especially pronounced in aquatic ecosystems. Simultaneously, extremes of flood and drought are magnified by climate change, whether this Administration believes it or not. Our remaining wetlands, aquifers, and tributary streams are more precious than ever in buffering these extremes. This Section 401 guardrail against combined, federal-corporate voracious power is more vital than ever. If EPA hands industry the tools to steamroll the love of Americans for our last aquatic ecosystems, the Trump Administration will leave our children with great ‘energy dominance,' but a bleak, barren, meaningless world stripped of natural life, and a traumatic, harried struggle for survival between droughts and floods.

Title: Comment submitted by M. Richey

My key concerns about the negative impacts of this proposed rulemaking are:
1. Threats to Drinking Water: Unchecked industrial pollution threatens critical water sources, affecting the health of millions of Americans.
2. Prioritizes permitting speed over environmental protection.
3. Loss of Local Control: Weakening the Clean Water Act Section 401 means states and tribes lose their power to protect their waters from potentially harmful projects.
4. Climate and Environmental Harm: Limiting state and tribal authority benefits polluting industries at the expense of our families and communities, our local economies, ecosystems, and efforts to combat climate change.
5. Limits public input and transparency.

This proposed revision should not be approved.

Title: Comment submitted by Barbara Sabaj
Comment:
I write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to again improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act). Section 401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Act in 1972 and is specifically designed to empower states and Tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.  The proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, datacenters, and other projects are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services they provide people and communities. In 2023 by a revised rule that restored the proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act and the long-standing regulatory structure that stood in place since 1971. In doing so, it also updated the rule to clarify that state and Tribal reviews were appropriately limited to water related requirements and that there was a clear and timely process for applicants to follow.
 Of foremost concern, it would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court precedent confirming that meaning by limiting the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to the “discharge from a point source” of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. 
 While this change is the most concerning provision of the proposed rule, there are several others that also unnecessarily and improperly seek to restrict the ability of states and Tribes to protect their waters. 
The 2023 rule also makes it clear that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. Moreover, the Section 401 process rarely results in the denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming number of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.[JD2] 
 It also must be seen in relation to recent changes to the scope of Clean Water Act protections generally. Section 401 is only triggered when a federal permit or license involving a discharge to “waters of the United States” is at issue. In fact, most federal permits and licenses that trigger Section 401 are Section 404 discharge permits under the Clean Water Act itself. In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a decision in Sackett v. sEPA that dramatically reduced the number of waters covered by the federal Clean Water Act. Subsequently, this EPA is finalizing a rule that would even further limit the number of waters protected by the Act. If the proposed EPA rule governing “waters of the United States” is issued as proposed, it would result in an estimated 81% of historically federally protected wetlands lacking Clean Water Act protection[JD3] . Massive losses in protections under the Act for streams is also at issue. With these rollbacks, many projects will no longer need CWA permits, meaning that the trigger for Section 401 review for many projects will disappear altogether.   
The combined result of these two proposed rules will be far fewer opportunities for state and Tribes to protect their waters under Section 401 and far weaker options for them to do so in the limited number of times it is triggered. This will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health, the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them.
I strongly urge you not to move forward with this ill-conceived and unnecessary rule and keep the existing 2023 rule in place. Sincerely, Barbara Sabaj

Title: Comment submitted by Garrett Robinson
Comment:
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Clean Water Act Section 401)
To whom it may concern,

My name is Garrett D. Robinson. I live in Stafford County, Virginia, in the Chesapeake Bay watershed. I am a retired Marine Corps Master Gunnery Sergeant, and I work with the Armed Forces Initiative of Backcountry Hunters & Anglers, which helps veterans find purpose through conservation and time outdoors. Last summer, I had the opportunity to support hands-on Chesapeake Bay conservation work with fellow veterans. That experience reinforced something simple: clean water is not abstract. It is local, it is personal, and it is a public trust responsibility.

I am submitting this comment to urge EPA to retain strong, practical Clean Water Act Section 401 authority for states and eligible tribes, and to avoid changes that would narrow certification review to an overly rigid, discharge-only framework that fails to capture real-world water quality risks from complex projects.

Section 401 is one of the few tools that ensures federally permitted activities actually comply with water quality standards where the impacts occur. In the Chesapeake Bay watershed, that matters every day. Bay restoration depends on protecting tributaries and downstream waters from sediment, nutrients, temperature impacts, habitat loss, and degradation that often come from how a project is built and operated, not just from a narrowly defined discharge point.

1) Do not narrow the scope of review in a way that prevents states and tribes from addressing real water quality impacts
The proposed rule indicates it would narrow review from an activity-based approach to a focus on point source discharges. In practice, many of the most damaging water quality impacts are connected to the overall activity and its foreseeable consequences. Construction-phase sedimentation, stormwater runoff, erosion, dewatering, altered flows, thermal impacts, and long-term monitoring and adaptive management needs can determine whether water quality standards are actually met.
Virginia and authorized tribes should be able to evaluate and condition certifications to ensure compliance with water quality standards in a way that matches on-the-ground realities, including when impacts are cumulative or occur over time. I respectfully urge EPA to maintain a framework that does not handcuff states and tribes from requiring conditions that directly protect designated uses and water quality standards.

2) Preserve workable timelines that support meaningful public notice and avoid “waiver by process”
I support efficient permitting, but efficiency must not come at the expense of public participation and workable state and tribal administration. The proposal removes automatic extensions tied to public notice procedures and force majeure events. That risks creating situations where certification is effectively waived, not because water quality standards are met, but because the process becomes unworkable under real-world conditions.
EPA should ensure states and tribes can complete legally required public notice processes and respond to unavoidable disruptions without the penalty of losing certification authority by default.

3) Protect and strengthen tribal participation and capacity
The Chesapeake Bay and its tributaries are waters of cultural, subsistence, and economic importance, and tribes must have a meaningful role where projects may affect their waters. I urge EPA to avoid changes that reduce tribal participation or make it harder for tribes to exercise Section 401 authority. Any final rule should support tribal capacity and remove barriers to participation, including as neighboring jurisdictions where applicable.
4) Keep Section 401 focused on water quality protection, not just permitting speed
As a veteran, I care about mission, discipline, and results. The mission here should be straightforward: ensure projects permitted by the federal government comply with water quality standards and protect the waters that communities depend on. Section 401 is a guardrail that helps make that mission real. Please do not weaken it in a way that shifts risk onto downstream communities and the waters that define places like the Chesapeake Bay.
For these reasons, I urge EPA to revise the proposal to maintain robust state and tribal Section 401 authority, preserve workable timing and public notice flexibility, and ensure certification can meaningfully address water quality impacts from the full project activity where necessary to meet water quality standards.

Thank you for considering my comment.

Respectfully,
Garrett D. Robinson
Stafford County, Virginia

Title: Comment submitted by Mid-Atlantic Chapter of Backcountry Hunters and Anglers
Comment:
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Clean Water Act Section 401)
To whom it may concern,
On behalf of the Mid-Atlantic Chapter of Backcountry Hunters & Anglers (BHA), we submit this comment regarding EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certification regulations.
Backcountry Hunters & Anglers is a national conservation organization representing hunters, anglers, and outdoor recreationists who rely on clean water and healthy habitat. The Mid-Atlantic Chapter serves members across Delaware, Maryland, Washington, D.C., and Virginia. Our chapter’s mission is rooted in science-based wildlife management and stewardship of public lands and waters. For our members, clean water is not an abstract policy concept. It is the foundation for fishable streams, swimmable rivers, functioning wetlands, and the public trust resources that sustain outdoor traditions and local economies.
We urge EPA to finalize a rule that preserves robust and workable Section 401 authority for states and eligible tribes and avoids changes that would narrow certification review in ways that reduce real-world protection of water quality.
1) Maintain Section 401’s practical scope to address water quality impacts from the full activity when necessary
Section 401 has long served as a commonsense safeguard, ensuring that federally permitted projects comply with state and tribal water quality standards where impacts occur. In the Mid-Atlantic region and especially across the Chesapeake Bay watershed, water quality harms frequently arise from the overall activity, including construction-phase sedimentation, stormwater runoff, erosion, altered flows, temperature impacts, wetland loss, and long-term operation and maintenance issues. These impacts can determine whether designated uses, including aquatic life and recreation, are protected.
EPA should avoid finalizing a rule that effectively limits certification to an overly rigid, discharge-only framework. States and eligible tribes must retain the ability to evaluate and condition certifications to ensure water quality standards are met in practice, including through conditions tied directly to protecting water quality, monitoring, mitigation, and adaptive management where warranted.
2) Preserve meaningful public notice and workable timelines, and prevent “waiver by process”
Public notice and public participation are essential to legitimate and durable water quality decisions. The final rule should ensure that certification timelines allow states and tribes to conduct required public notice, review complex records, and respond to material issues raised by the public without risking inadvertent waiver.
We also urge EPA to retain reasonable flexibility for unavoidable disruptions and force majeure circumstances. A regulatory structure that increases the risk of waiver due to process or timing constraints undermines the purpose of Section 401 and shifts risk onto downstream communities and waters.
3) Respect tribal sovereignty and strengthen tribal participation and capacity
Tribal nations have a vital role in protecting water quality. EPA should ensure the final rule supports tribal sovereignty and capacity to implement Section 401, and it should remove barriers that make it harder for tribes to engage meaningfully in certification decisions that affect their waters and resources.
4) Keep Section 401 focused on clean water outcomes that support fish, wildlife, and public access
Hunters and anglers depend on intact riparian habitat, coldwater and warmwater fisheries, healthy wetlands, and stable flows. When water quality standards are weakened in practice, opportunities for fishing and other outdoor recreation decline, and restoration costs rise. Defending strong Section 401 implementation is aligned with the Clean Water Act’s core objective to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.
For these reasons, the Mid-Atlantic Chapter of Backcountry Hunters & Anglers urges EPA to revise the proposal as needed to maintain robust, workable state and tribal Section 401 authority, preserve meaningful public participation and realistic timelines, and ensure certifications can address the water quality impacts of the full activity where necessary to meet water quality standards.
Respectfully submitted,
Mid-Atlantic Chapter, Backcountry Hunters & Anglers
Submitted by: Garrett D. Robinson, Chapter Chair
Stafford, Virginia

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Lee Zeldin
Administrator
EPA Docket Center, Water Docket
Mail Code 28221T
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW,
Washington, DC 20460
Dear Administrator Zeldin:
I write to strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule to again improperly weaken regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Section 401 represents an important tool used by states and Tribes to protect state and Tribal water resources from the impacts of federally permitted development. Section 401 is a linchpin of the cooperative federalism structure envisioned by Congress when it passed the Act in 1972 and is specifically designed to empower states and tribes. The proposed rule would dramatically undercut the role provided to states and Tribes by the Act in protecting their waters.

The proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state and tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, data centers, and other projects are protective of state and tribal waters and the important services they provide people and communities. The harm done by the 2020 rule was substantially corrected in 2023 by a revised rule that restored the proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act and the long-standing regulatory structure that stood in place since 1971. In doing so, it also updated the rule to clarify that state and Tribal reviews were appropriately limited to water related requirements and that there was a clear and timely process for applicants to follow.

The proposed rule is by and large an unnecessary retread of the rejected 2020 rule. Of foremost concern, it would violate the Act by rejecting its clear meaning and overturning Supreme Court precedent confirming that meaning by limiting the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to the “discharge from a point source” of a project. This would dramatically limit the ability of states and Tribes to look at the totality of potential impacts and harms to waters from a project. For instance, it could eliminate or undermine state and Tribes from being able to put in place protective measures so that rivers and streams have adequate flow, habitat is protected, and designated uses like fishing and recreation enjoyed by the public are not disturbed or destroyed.
While this change is the most concerning provision of the proposed rule, there are several others that also unnecessarily and improperly seek to restrict the ability of states and Tribes to protect their waters. For instance:
· It undercuts the ability of states and Tribes to ensure that they have the information they need in time to assess how various projects may impact their waters.
· It gives the applicant an effective veto of modifications to protective conditions if a project changes, giving the applicant an improper role in regulating their own project and an incentive to modify a project to get out from under conditions they may not want to comply with.
· It places inflexible time restrictions on states and Tribes, potentially forcing them to make a hasty decision and prompting increased certification denials.
· It removes the ability of Tribes to apply for treatment as a state for Section 401 certification only, making it harder and more costly for Tribes to protect their own waters.
· It removes a key tool for states and Tribes to protect non-federal waters. This is a critical rollback given that EPA is also considering a rule that would leave less than 20 percent of wetlands and millions of miles protected by the Clean Water Act.[JD1]
· It redefines “water quality requirements” to limit the ability of states and Tribes to protect “other water quality-related requirements of state law” by narrowing the definition of these requirements to applicable and appropriate state or tribal water-quality related requirement for discharges. This would arguably undercut critical protections such as minimum flow needed for river health, recreation, and the needs of many key species.

It is hard to see this proposal as anything thing other than an unwarranted assault on clean water and state and Tribal rights. Any concerns, which we feel were misplaced at the time, about uncertainty around timing or process were addressed with the changes in the 2023 rule. The 2023 rule also makes it clear that certification, conditions, and denial must regard water quality requirements. Moreover, the Section 401 process rarely results in the denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming number of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.[JD2]

Sincerely,

A Concerned Citizen

Title: Comment submitted by Vincent Lampone
Comment:
I oppose EPA’s proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 certification (Docket ID EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929) because key elements conflict with the administrative record, reliance interests, and the statute’s cooperative-federalism structure.

EPA states the proposal is needed to improve efficiency and predictability and prevent delays. However, the agency’s own prior economic analysis found available data insufficient to quantify national rates of certification decisions, waivers, or timelines. Proceeding with a rule justified primarily by delay concerns without reliable nationwide evidence risks arbitrary-and-capricious decision-making under the Administrative Procedure Act. Courts have repeatedly vacated rules where agencies relied on unsupported assumptions about regulatory burdens.

The proposal would also newly restrict state review of nationwide and general permits, even though more than 90 percent of Army Corps permits are issued through those permit types. Eliminating review authority over the majority of permits would materially alter longstanding federal-state practice and raises serious reliance-interest concerns that the agency must address explicitly.

Because the rule rests on unquantified premises and would substantially change existing regulatory expectations, EPA should withdraw the proposal or extend the comment period and produce a data-supported analysis before proceeding.

Title: Comment submitted by Jeff Pollari
Comment:
Subject: Protect Our Waters: Opposition to the Proposed 2026 EPA Rule Changes
To the EPA Review Board,

I am writing today not just as a concerned citizen, but as someone who believes that clean water is a fundamental right, not a luxury. The proposed 2026 rule changes to the Clean Water Act feel less like "streamlining" and more like a deliberate attempt to silence the people most affected by pollution: our States and Tribal nations.

The Clean Water Act was designed to empower locals, not sideline them. These proposed changes are a step backward for several critical reasons:

Erasing Tribal Sovereignty: These rules would effectively strip Tribes of their right to protect their own Reservation waters and object to permits that impact them. We should be honoring Tribal stewardship, not writing them out of the regulations.

Handcuffing Minnesota: In my home state, we rely on our current authority to protect world-class treasures like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. These rules would stop us from setting the necessary conditions to keep these waters "Exceptional."

A "Gift" to Polluters: Narrowing the scope of what we can review and forcing a "one-year waiver" is a recipe for disaster. If a company hides the truth about their environmental impact or if a natural disaster slows down a study, the EPA shouldn't reward them by letting the clock run out.

Unchecked Power: Giving the EPA Administrator the power to unilaterally decide that entire categories of waste—like mining or pipeline discharge—can bypass review is dangerous and unreasonable.

My research says The Clean Water Act was largely spurred into existence after the Cuyahoga River in Ohio literally caught fire for the 13th time in 1969. We have come too far since then to start dismantling the very tools that prevent us from returning to those days.

Also, here in Minnesota, we have roughly 11,842 lakes—that’s a lot of shoreline to protect, and we can’t do it if the EPA ties our hands.

The current rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting my family and my community than this proposed update ever could. I urge you to withdraw this proposal and keep the authority where it belongs: with the States and Tribes who actually live with the water.

Thank you for your time and for actually listening to the people on the ground.

Sincerely,
-Jeff Pollari

(this comment is on Docket ID # EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929)

Title: Comment submitted by Amy Georgeson
Comment:
Dear Administrator and EPA Staff,

I am submitting this comment as a concerned citizen who has watched water quality protections steadily erode while communities are told that streamlining regulations will somehow produce better outcomes.

My primary concern is that narrowing the scope of Section 401 review to only the immediate discharge point, combined with compressed timelines and restricted conditioning authority, could result in the approval of projects whose downstream and cumulative water quality impacts are insufficiently evaluated. This creates environmental, financial, and legal risk that ultimately undermines the Clean Water Act’s objective.

Our current regulatory system is already struggling to prevent contaminated waterways, harmful algal blooms, failing infrastructure, and downstream drinking water impacts. Given that reality, I am deeply concerned that this proposed update to Section 401 water quality certification regulations risks further weakening one of the few tools states and Tribes have to prevent pollution before it occurs.

Section 401 exists for a reason. It ensures that federally permitted projects cannot move forward unless they comply with state and tribal water quality standards. That authority is not bureaucratic excess, it is a critical safeguard rooted in cooperative federalism. Any rule that narrows the scope of review, restricts the ability to consider cumulative or downstream impacts directly tied to discharges, or compresses timelines without guaranteeing adequate information effectively reduces meaningful oversight.

We cannot afford regulatory backsliding.

If certifying authorities are prevented from fully evaluating impacts to designated uses, anti-degradation requirements, and narrative water quality standards, then certification becomes procedural rather than protective. Efficiency is not a substitute for environmental integrity. Faster approvals do not make polluted water safer.

There is also significant legal and financial risk in weakening oversight. A rule that limits statutory authority too aggressively may invite litigation under the Administrative Procedure Act. Projects approved under a narrowed interpretation could face court challenges, vacated permits, citizen suits, or costly retrofits after construction. That is not regulatory certainty, it is regulatory instability.

More importantly, communities bear the cost when water protections fail. Contaminated drinking water, degraded fisheries, recreational losses, and long-term cleanup costs are not theoretical harms. They are lived realities across the country. Preventing damage at the certification stage is far more responsible than attempting to fix it after the fact.

If EPA’s goal is predictability, then the Agency should strengthen clarity without constraining substantive authority. States and Tribes must retain the ability to request sufficient information, impose necessary conditions, and ensure compliance with all applicable water quality standards. Any final rule should explicitly affirm that it does not diminish existing Clean Water Act protections or anti-degradation policies.

I urge EPA to prioritize the Clean Water Act’s core objective: restoring and maintaining the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. Streamlining must not come at the expense of clean water.

Thank you for considering my comments.

Title: Comment submitted by Brennan Kaye
Comment:
I submit this comment in opposition to the proposed changes to the Clean Water Act framework.

I am an ecological land steward and native plant practitioner working in central Pennsylvania, primarily within riparian corridors, floodplains, and headwater systems in the Susquehanna River watershed. My work includes invasive species removal, native plant restoration, erosion control, and long-term watershed stabilization on private and semi-public lands. I work directly in the field with soils, streambanks, and stormwater flow patterns. The integrity of small tributaries and seasonal waters is not abstract to me; it is visible in real time through sediment load, bank collapse, algal growth, and downstream turbidity.

First, any rule that narrows Clean Water Act jurisdiction must be reconciled with the statutes core objective: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nations waters (33 U.S.C. 1251(a)). In practice, water quality is cumulative and hydrologically connected. Headwater streams, ephemeral channels, wetlands, and floodplain depressions function as nutrient filters, sediment traps, and groundwater recharge zones. When those systems are weakened or excluded from protection, downstream waters degrade measurably. This is not theoretical; it is observable at the watershed scale.

Second, limiting Section 401 certification authority constrains the ability of states and Tribes to apply locally informed water quality standards to federally permitted projects. In Pennsylvania, many drinking water sources rely on intact upstream tributaries and forested riparian buffers. When review authority is narrowed, local governments lose one of the few meaningful tools available to address cumulative impacts from pipelines, fill projects, channelization, and other disturbance. Any such limitation must be clearly justified under the cooperative federalism structure embedded in the Act.

Third, under the Administrative Procedure Act, the agency must provide a reasoned explanation supported by scientific evidence demonstrating that the proposed changes will not result in increased pollutant loading, sedimentation, nutrient runoff, or impairment of downstream drinking water sources. From a field perspective, disturbance to small and intermittent waters has outsized downstream effects, particularly in agricultural and rapidly developing landscapes. A rulemaking that does not adequately analyze cumulative watershed impacts risks being arbitrary and capricious under Motor Vehicle Manufacturers Association v. State Farm.

Fourth, ecological function does not conform neatly to jurisdictional categories. Seasonal and ephemeral flows in Pennsylvania routinely carry nutrients and sediments into larger tributaries during storm events. Floodplain wetlands attenuate peak flows and reduce erosion pressure. Narrowing protection for these systems may reduce regulatory paperwork in the short term but will increase long-term remediation costs, infrastructure strain, and public health risk.

If the agency intends to depart from prior interpretations that recognized hydrological connectivity and cumulative watershed impacts, it must provide a reasoned explanation for that change and address reliance interests built around existing regulatory frameworks.

Based on direct, ongoing field experience within the Susquehanna watershed, weakening protections for upstream waters or constraining state and Tribal certification authority is inconsistent with the statutory objective of maintaining water integrity. Unless the agency can demonstrate, with peer-reviewed evidence and watershed-scale modeling, that the proposed revisions will not degrade downstream water quality, the proposal should not be finalized.

Thank you for the opportunity to submit this comment.

Title: Comment submitted by Michael Rocco
Comment:
I am writing as an Oregonian with deep respect for Tribal Nations, a strong understanding of the delicate balance of our ecosystems, and more than a decade of professional experience working as an environmental steward for a Fortune 500 company. Throughout my career, I have partnered closely with NGOs and non-profits across the globe, and that work has only reinforced a core belief: long-term environmental protection depends on empowering local and Tribal voices, not sidelining them.

For these reasons, I strongly oppose this proposed rule.

While it may streamline and expedite permitting for major energy projects, it would do so by dangerously narrowing the scope of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act—undermining the ability of state and Tribal governments to protect the waterways within their borders.

Section 401 has long served as a vital safeguard, allowing states and Tribes to assess not only immediate water quality impacts, but also the long-term and cumulative effects of major infrastructure projects such as oil and gas pipelines. Under the proposed rule, that authority would be reduced to a narrow, short-term lens, effectively stripping states and Tribes of meaningful oversight—even when projects threaten fisheries, drinking water supplies, and culturally significant waterways.

Here in Oregon, we understand that water systems are living systems. A project like a dam may technically meet water quality standards at the point of discharge, yet still disrupt river flow, fish migration, and entire ecosystems. These impacts reverberate far beyond chemistry metrics, affecting Tribal treaty rights, local economies, and the long-term health of watersheds that communities depend on.

Section 401 has not been a blunt instrument to block projects arbitrarily. On the contrary, it has been used judiciously to ensure that development proceeds responsibly and with accountability. Weakening it in service of fossil fuel infrastructure not only ignores decades of environmental policy progress—it disregards the knowledge and stewardship of Tribal Nations and local governments best positioned to protect these resources.

Rather than dismantling one of the Clean Water Act’s most effective tools, we should be focusing on scaling up wind, solar, and battery storage—energy solutions that can be deployed efficiently, affordably, and without compromising the health of our waterways or the communities that rely on them.

I urge you not to finalize this harmful rule.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
To: Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
Attention: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Subject: Comments on the Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929)

I. Introduction and Standing
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule changes to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 regulations. As a resident of Virginia with a professional background in environmental monitoring, I am deeply concerned that the proposed narrowing of the scope of certification from the "activity as a whole" to specific "discharges" will irreversibly harm the biological and physical integrity of our nation’s watersheds.

II. Judicial Arguments: Departure from Established Precedent
The proposed rule seeks to restrict the ability of state and tribal certifying authorities to review a project’s holistic impact. This interpretation contradicts nearly thirty years of judicial precedent established by the U.S. Supreme Court.
 * PUD No. 1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology (1994): The Court held that Section 401(d) authorizes states to impose conditions on the "activity as a whole" to ensure compliance with water quality standards. The current proposal’s attempt to decouple "discharge" from the "activity" creates a regulatory loophole that prevents states from protecting their waters from non-point source stressors.
 * S.D. Warren Co. v. Maine Board of Environmental Protection (2006): The Court affirmed that even activities without a chemical "discharge" (such as hydroelectric dams) are subject to Section 401 because they result in "discharges" in the broader sense of the term. By narrowing this definition, the EPA is attempting to override the Supreme Court's functional interpretation of the Clean Water Act.

III. Scientific Analysis: Conflict with Established Consensus
The scientific community has long recognized that water quality cannot be protected by managing isolated "points" of discharge alone. The proposed rule ignores the fundamental principles of watershed connectivity and biological integrity.
 * Biological Integrity (Karr, 1991): Peer-reviewed research, such as Biological Integrity: A Long-Neglected Aspect of Water Resource Management, demonstrates that the health of a water body is determined by its physical and biological structure, not just chemical concentrations. By barring states from considering the "activity as a whole," the EPA is permitting physical habitat destruction that may not involve a chemical discharge but results in the "biological death" of the stream.
 * Cumulative Impacts (Ambrose & Meentemeyer, 2025): Recent longitudinal studies confirm that project-wide stressors—such as thermal pollution from riparian clearing or flow alteration—have systemic downstream effects. The proposed rule’s restriction on the "reasonable period of time" for review and the scope of information states can request (Section 121.6) prevents scientists from conducting the multi-seasonal assessments required to predict these cumulative failures.

IV. Conclusion and Recommendation
The EPA’s proposal prioritizes administrative convenience over the statutory mandate of the Clean Water Act: "to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters." The proposed rule is scientifically unsound, legally fragile, and environmentally reckless.
I strongly urge the EPA to withdraw the proposed revisions and maintain the 2023 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, which appropriately recognizes the "activity as a whole" standard necessary for effective environmental protection.

Title: Comment submitted by Amphibian Refuge
Comment:

Title: Comment submitted by Tyson Kurtz
Comment:
Reducing restrictions on the protections for clean water, which is a shared resource, should not even be considered. Project that have the potential to propose adverse effects to water quality, not covered under section 404 programs, need to be thoroughly reviewed, prior to approval and construction. The US Army Corps of Engineers, delegated authority of Section 404 implementation, has had recent major reductions in staff. I have had working relationships with Corps' representations for many years now and in the past year, the folks responsible for reviewing permit applications (to impacts Waters of the US) are so over-worked, it seems impossible that they can thoroughly review projects and their potential impacts. Luckily, there is typically a second reviewing agency for projects that may impact Waters of the US, through the 401 programs. Having multiple reviewers, looking at projects from slightly different angles (direct fill in waters vs water quality concerns) it better for all of us, to make sure our waters (again, a shared resources) are adequately protected. 

There are many ways besides direct point source discharges (or fill - section 404) that can have measurable and detrimental effects on water quality. As mentioned in the public notice, thermal impacts can be hugely determinantal. Aquatic scientists should be consulted on these matters. Just because there is still water flowing in a stream, doesn't mean water quality won't being so adversely affected, that the biology in a stream isn't altered so much that it affects the "food chain", that could ultimately result in impaired water ways. Understanding what could actually pose adverse effects to water quality needs to be assessed, with sufficient timelines, by people with knowledge of how waters can be impacted, directly and indirectly, to make sure we all continue to have clean water. 

Reducing the timelines for 401 review and limiting the extent of power the 401 programs have, is going to result in more adverse effects to water quality. This proposal is only to save extra money for developers (of any funding source) and have less "red tape", to adversely impacts our shared waterways. To have nationwide standard on 401 implementation that will adequately address water quality concerns for a project proposed on a flat piece of land in the arid dessert with highly seasonal streams and a mountainous site in a temperate rainforest, the rule would have to be insanely complex to provide adequate protections. Although it would seem that the intent is to have one national 401 standard that barely offers protects or considerations to cmplexities of aquatic resources across different ecoregions.

Title: Comment submitted by Michael Lighthiser
Comment:
The U.S. EPA's proposed rule revising the regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act is a bad idea. It would significantly weaken essential long-standing state and Tribal authorities that are needed for protecting fish and wildlife. The reasons for healthy rivers and streams are many. Development that puts these waters at risk must be properly evaluated with necessary conditions put in place to protect them.

Section 401 has a long history ensuring that federally permitted activities do not impair water quality or designated uses. States and Tribes know their water bodies best and should have the authority to address site-specific impacts to fish and wildlife that would be caused by projects. However, instead of empowering States and Tribes, the proposed rule would significantly limit their authority, putting our aquatic systems at risk. States and Tribes need to be able to not only consider direct discharge, but also other project impacts such as flow regimes and temperature, connectivity of habitat, and sediment transport. Many animal species are highly sensitive to these cumulative and secondary effects, so review should not be limited to only the discharge.

I strongly urge the EPA to withdraw the proposed rule. Instead of weakening rules, the EPA should strengthen them to empower states and Tribes to protect water quality and aquatic habitat.

Thank you for this opportunity to provide input.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
The Trump EPA’s 2026 Proposed Rule on certification is inconsistent with and unlawful under the Clean Water Act. It will undermine the purpose of the Act to eliminate the discharge of pollutants and to achieve water quality levels that are fishable and swimmable, and drag us back to the days when rivers burned.

The Proposed Rule would also eliminate a regulation facilitating Tribal certification authority for treatment as a state. The Rule would erase all existing regulatory language that supports neighboring/downstream Tribes in requiring conditions or denial of Federal permits that would cause violations of Reservation water quality requirements.
The Proposed Rule would prevent any State or Tribe from setting conditions on Federal “general permits” that apply across the United States. No conditions could be set to prevent degradation, and no Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters or Outstanding Resource Value waters could be protected.
The Proposed Rule would tighten a noose around the scope of certification to prevent conditions on or denial of permits, even if they would pollute and degrade lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or contaminated seepage through groundwater into surface water.
The Proposed Rule would force involuntary waiver of certification if the State or Tribal process was not completed within one-year, even if the extra time was needed due to public process, lack of sufficient information to make a decision, or even acts of God.
The Proposed Rule would allow project applicants to exploit the new forced waiver Rule by requesting certification of their “draft” application and incomplete water-related data. The polluter could then do a bait-and-switch and disclose the real plan and its impacts on water after the one-year time limit on certification had ended.
The Proposed Rule would centralize review of State and Tribal certification in the Administrator or its designee. That political appointee would have the power to determine that an entire category of discharge would never have an “effect” on downstream States or Tribes.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Public Comment to the EPA 
on Proposed Revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

To Whom It May Concern:

I am submitting this comment as a deeply concerned member of the Sierra Club and as an advocate for the protection of Minnesota’s and the USA’s waters, wetlands, and downstream communities. I strongly oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. These changes would dramatically weaken one of the most essential tools states and Tribal Nations have to safeguard water quality, public health, and ecological integrity.

1. The Proposed Rule Strips States and Tribes of Their Core Authority

Section 401 has always served as a critical checkpoint: before a federal permit is issued, states and authorized Tribes must certify that a project will comply with water quality standards. This includes the ability to:
•	Approve a project
•	Approve with conditions
•	Deny certification
•	Or waive certification
The proposed rule would severely limit this authority by narrowing the scope of what states and Tribes can consider. The rule would force certifying authorities to ignore the full range of project related pollution risks and downstream impacts that are essential to responsible water governance.

This is not a minor procedural change—it is a fundamental weakening of cooperative federalism.

2. Minnesota Provides Clear Examples of the Harm

This rule would cause consequences for Minnesota that are immediate and alarming:
•	The Minnesota Pollution Control Agency (MPCA) would be unable to protect the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness (BWCA) as an Outstanding Resource Value Water. This is one of the most pristine freshwater ecosystems in the world, and it deserves the highest level of protection.
•	The Fond du Lac Band would lose the ability to require that projects affecting the St. Louis River—such as PolyMet’s sulfide mining proposal—meet Tribal water quality standards for mercury and specific conductance. These standards are grounded in science and cultural survival. Weakening Tribal authority is both environmentally reckless and a violation of Tribal sovereignty.

These are only a few examples that illustrate exactly why Section 401 exists and why its full strength must be preserved.

3. The Rule Undermines Downstream Protections and Public Health

Water does not respect political boundaries. Pollution from one project can travel miles downstream, affecting communities, fisheries, drinking water supplies, and ecosystems far beyond the project footprint.
The proposed rule would prevent states and Tribes from evaluating:
•	Cumulative impacts
•	Downstream water quality threats
•	Broader ecological risks
•	Climate related vulnerabilities
•	Interactions with existing impairments

This is not just shortsighted—it is dangerous. Section 401 was designed precisely to ensure that local knowledge and local standards guide decisions that affect local waters.

4. The Rule Conflicts with the Intent of the Clean Water Act

Congress created Section 401 to give states and Tribes a meaningful role in protecting water quality. The proposed revisions invert that intent by:
•	Narrowing the scope of review
•	Shortening timelines
•	Limiting the ability to impose conditions
•	Restricting the types of impacts that can be considered
This is a step backward at a time when water quality challenges—from nutrient pollution to PFAS to mining waste—are becoming more complex, not less.

5. EPA Should Strengthen, Not Weaken, Section 401

Instead of restricting state and Tribal authority, EPA should:
•	Affirm the broad scope of review historically recognized under Section 401
•	Support Tribal Nations’ inherent rights to protect their waters
•	Ensure that downstream communities are fully protected
•	Provide resources and technical support to certifying authorities
•	Encourage robust public participation in water quality decisions
A strong Section 401 is essential for environmental justice, climate resilience, and the long-term health of our waters.

Conclusion

I urge the EPA to withdraw the proposed revisions and instead uphold the full authority of states and Tribal Nations under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. Our waters, our communities, and our future depend on strong, science based, locally informed protections.

Thank you for considering these comments.

Sincerely, 
A concerned Sierra Club member and Minnesota resident

Dear Administrator,

Every American, regardless of their political affiliation or views, deserves to have clean water. Having the ability to trust the cleanliness and safety of our water has been something fought for over the decades and needs to continue to be a priority. There are so many negatives impacts that can be linked to contamination of water sources. We need the government to advocate for and the EPA to strengthen water protections. 

I am concerned by the EPA proposal to weaken Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA) and strongly oppose the proposed changes. 

Water pollution has serious impacts, and any decision that threatens to increase pollution, harm public health, or exacerbate impacts to habitat needs to be analyzed and weighed. Waterways are not solely impacted in the spot where discharge occurs and limiting the scope of the Section 401 analysis would result in a lack of review of project impacts upstream and downstream. With the changes proposed, states and Tribes could lose the ability to use Section 401 to protect many smaller streams, wetlands and headwaters within their borders. This would render state and tribal decision makers powerless to block, or place conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters, even if they are vital to local drinking water supplies, fisheries, recreation, or flood control.

Limiting the scope and timeline for analysis to take place fails to achieve
the CWA goal of ensuring states, territories, and tribes are empowered to protect their water resources and instead only benefits the companies behind the proposed projects. It leaves taxpayers on the hook for additional cleanup of pollution that was unable to be mitigated because it was not analyzed under a discharge only scenario. It opens the door for projects that should not be permitted due to impacts and risks that would become clear during analysis, but are never properly reviewed. 

I call on the EPA to use its authority to uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water, public health, and species habitat in waterways and wetlands. 

Do what is right for ALL PEOPLE, not just the select few.

The Clean Water Act provides states and authorized Tribes with an important tool to help protect water quality. States and authorized Tribes need the ability to  determine the scope of review that will protect water quality, not be limited to an ever narrowing scope of federally protected water and solely tasked to review direct water quality impacts of discharge into those waters. This would render state and tribal decision makers powerless to block, or place conditions on, federally permitted projects that could damage those waters, even if they are vital to local drinking water supplies, fisheries, recreation, or flood control.

Limiting the scope and timeline for analysis to take place fails to achieve
the CWA goal of ensuring states, territories, and tribes are empowered to protect their water resources and instead only benefits the companies behind the proposed projects. It leaves taxpayers on the hook for additional cleanup of pollution that was unable to be mitigated because it was not analyzed under a discharge only scenario. It opens the door for projects that should not be permitted due to impacts and risks that would become clear during analysis, but are never properly reviewed. 

Without hesitation and with sincere concern, I call on the EPA to use its authority to uphold and strengthen the Clean Water Act to protect drinking water, public health, and species habitat in waterways and wetlands.

Title: Comment submitted by Jamie Higgins
Comment:
As retired US Army Corps of Engineers (USACE) employee, I am are concerned with EPA’s proposal to eliminate the need for the USACE to acquire a water quality certification for planning projects requiring Congressional authorization.  Since the inception of the Clean Water Act of 1977, USACE has sought water quality certifications for Congressional authorized projects and has an established history of working with certifying authorities to ensure the Congressional authorized projects will not degrade state water quality standards. 
USACE 33 CFR 335.2 states that “…the Corps does not issue itself a CWA permit to authorize Corps discharges of dredged material or fill material into U.S. waters, but does apply the 404(b)(1) guidelines and other substantive requirements of the CWA and other environmental laws.”  Further in 33 CFR 336.1(a)(1) states, 
“The CWA requires the Corps to seek state water quality certification for discharges of dredged or fill material into waters of the U.S.”. This is consistent with Section 313 of the Clean Water Act that states, 
“Each Federal agency having jurisdiction over any facility or engaged in activity resulting, or which may result, in the discharge or runoff of pollutants is subject to, and must comply with, all Federal, State, interstate, and local requirements and administrative authorities for the control and abatement of water pollution, including reasonable service charges. This includes adhering to any reporting, recordkeeping, and/or permitting requirements.”  
Additionally, Congress made it abundantly clear in Clean Water Act 1977 Senate Report on page 67 stating, 
“This section clarifies section 313 to provide that all Federal facilities must comply with all substantive and procedural requirements of Federal, State or local water pollution control laws. It also eliminates the exception that Federal agencies from State certification of activities under the section 401.
This section also amends section 404 to insure that dredge and fill activities of the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers are carried out in compliance with State, local or interstate substantive or procedural requirements.”
Congress provides further clarification by stating, “The act has been amended to indicate unequivocally that all Federal facilities and activities are subject to all of the provisions of State and local pollution laws.”  It is important to note that Congress stated “ALL” federal facilities and activities without explicitly stating USACE Congressionally authorized projects were exempt. 
Should USACE think that a certifying authority has issued a legally unsupported or overly burdensome water quality certification, Section 404(r) of the Clean Water Act provides a mechanism for USACE to elevate and resolve potential issues.  The fact USACE rarely uses this provision is a testament to the collaborative relationship USACE has with state environmental agencies or certifying authorities. Most issues regarding water quality certifications are resolved long before the certifying authority issues a water quality certification. 
Besides the obvious intent of Congress and adequate mechanisms for resolution in the Clean Water Act, the elimination of USACE obtaining water quality certifications for USACE planning projects would make USACE more vulnerable to litigation and potentially cause schedule delays and cost overruns further delaying project delivery.  It could also erode public and stakeholder trust, jeopardize relationships with state environmental agencies and strain partnerships with Non-federal Sponsors.  The USACE has had an extremely sound foundation of partnering with state and federal agencies in building the largest, most complex and important water resource projects in the nation and the world. This proposed rule has the potential to fracture that important foundation and jeopardize the construction of life saving projects.

Title: Comment submitted by Rachel Ruff
Comment:
I am submitting this comment in opposition to the proposed changes to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929).

Water quality protections are fundamentally tied to human health, ecological stability, and long-term economic sustainability. Because clean water is essential to sustaining human life, any regulatory change that narrows review authority or alters existing protections should be approached with caution and broad public engagement.

I am concerned that the proposed rule represents a significant shift in how water quality impacts may be evaluated, particularly if it limits the ability of states and tribes to consider the full range of effects that projects may have on water resources. Decisions affecting water systems often have long-term and cumulative consequences that extend beyond immediate discharge impacts, and regulatory frameworks should reflect that reality.

Before implementing changes of this scale, I believe further outreach, stakeholder engagement, and public education should occur to ensure that affected communities fully understand the implications of the proposal and have meaningful opportunities to participate in the decision-making process. Water quality policy should be shaped through transparent and inclusive processes, especially when the potential consequences affect public health and environmental sustainability.

For these reasons, I urge the EPA to reconsider the proposed rule and conduct additional outreach and review prior to finalizing any changes.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Title: Comment submitted by Lisa Williams
Comment:
Dear Administrator Zeldin,

As an environmental professional, I strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)’s proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certifications. States and Tribes bring local expertise and data that can be critically important to making accurate findings, especially when EPA staffing levels, especially for ecological risk assessors, are as low as they are now. 
 
For well over 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has been a critical tool to allow states and the federal government to cooperatively manage our shared water resources—and ensure that local voices are not disregarded in the process. This proposed rulemaking is contrary to the language of the Clean Water Act and would greatly restrict states’ and Tribes’ long-standing authority to protect waters within their borders.

Water Quality Certification is one of the most important and effective tools available to states and Tribes to ensure federally-licensed projects are protective of water quality for drinking and recreation, and aquatic habitat for fish and wildlife. The proposed rule would greatly undermine state and Tribal authority to protect their waters by restricting the type of protective conditions states and Tribes can put on federally licensed or permitted activities that threaten state or Tribal waters. 

Additionally, the proposed rule would restrict the timeline and information for state and Tribal review. States and Tribes must have adequate time and information to review and process complex applications and the ability to impose conditions on permits that will protect water quality, especially given the differences in types of resources, designated uses, and water quality standards among states.
 
In 2023, the EPA appropriately reasserted the strong role that states and Tribes should have in protecting their waters. This strong role - which has been the norm for most of the last 55 years - does not need to be weakened. I urge you to withdraw this damaging proposal immediately. I instead ask you to uphold the longstanding state and Tribal authority to protect waters within their boundaries.

Sincerely,
Lisa L. Williams, Ph.D.
Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry
