Title: Comment submitted by Steven Ring
Comment:
February 17, 2026

Lee Zeldin, Administrator
Water Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Mail Code 28221T
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW,
Washington, DC  20460

Dear Administrator Zeldin,

I am writing to oppose the EPA’s proposed changes to the rules that define how Section 401 of the Clean Water Act will be administered. I urge you to reconsider the recent update of these rules. 

Water pollution is a negative economic externality i.e. a cost imposed by an activity of one party onto others. Those costs may be incurred from health issues caused by the pollution; filtration or treatment costs; costs to drill new wells; or costs to one’s livelihood because a water resource has been degraded by the pollution. There may also be many indirect ecosystem costs related to animals, birds, and insects.

Responsible developers do not want to cause harm to their neighbors. Society (and government) have determined that regulations to minimize externalities are essential to a prosperous and healthy economy and social structure.

The current Section 401 rules have been in effect for many years. They support a coordination between state, tribal, and federal interests. For most projects, there are general permits and quick certification. For complex projects, there is a need to understand the potential pathways that could lead to an activity that imposes substantial costs on others. These projects require a thorough understanding of the ecological context in which an activity is proposed to take place. Therefore, it may be likely to take time. Restricting certification time and state and tribal input into this decision making will lead to increased cost being borne by others.

Federal and state cooperation is foundational to America's system of government, and Section 401 has been critical to ensuring responsible development activities that consider the impact and costs on individuals, other businesses, and the public. 

For example, under the proposed rule, the Minnesota Pollution Control Agency could not protect the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness (BWCAW) as an Outstanding Resource Value Water. Activities that would degrade the BWCAW are irresponsible – the cost is too high.


The current rule is well understood and strikes a good balance between allowing development activities and protecting our water resources. The proposed rule would simply push any destructive consequences and costs onto other individuals and the public. This may be seen as avoiding responsibility.

I urge the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401.

Sincerely, 

Steven Ring

Title: Comment submitted by Deanna Strohm
Comment:
I am writing to oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. 

Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has long provided states and authorized Tribes a critical role in protecting water quality within their jurisdictions by allowing them to review and certify federally permitted projects that may discharge into waters. The statute intentionally creates a cooperative federalism framework: federal agencies issue permits, while states and Tribes ensure local water quality standards are protected. The EPA itself recognizes Section 401 as “an important tool to help protect the water quality of federally regulated waters within their borders.”

The proposed rule undermines this framework by narrowing the authority of certifying agencies and prioritizing administrative efficiency over environmental protection. The consequences of weakening state and Tribal review are significant. 

With this proposed rule, we would lose the local expertise of state and tribal agencies which are working most closely within these systems. States and Tribes possess the most detailed understanding of their own hydrology, fisheries, wetlands, and drinking water sources. Federal agencies cannot reasonably evaluate cumulative, watershed-scale impacts across diverse local ecosystems. Section 401 exists precisely because Congress recognized local knowledge is essential to protecting water quality. Limiting certification scope effectively removes the ability of states and Tribes to evaluate real-world impacts such as: Habitat fragmentation from dams and culverts, wetland loss affecting flood protection, temperature and sediment changes harmful to aquatic life, drinking water contamination risks. 
The Clean Water Act was designed to protect water quality at the watershed level and explicitly preserved state authority to implement stronger protections. Weakening Section 401 reverses decades of cooperative federalism and shifts the balance away from resource protection toward project approval.

The proposed changes therefore do not merely “clarify” procedure, they materially reduce the ability of states and Tribes to prevent water quality degradation, contrary to Congressional intent.

I request that EPA withdraw the proposed rule to maintain robust state and Tribal authority under Clean Water Act Section 401.

Protecting water quality requires meaningful local review. Section 401 is one of the most effective tools communities have to safeguard drinking water, fisheries, wetlands, and public health. Weakening it will increase conflict, litigation, and environmental damage while reducing public trust in permitting decisions.

Thank you for considering this comment.

Comment:
waters.
 
- The proposed rule would establish requirements and standards for state and tribal water quality certification under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act that inappropriately restricts the scope and timing of this important avenue for states and tribes to protect their waters.
 
- These proposed limitations are inappropriate and are inconsistent with the statute. Under the statute, Section 510, the states have the right to establish requirements which include procedural and substantive standards controlling water pollution. Section 313(a), waives sovereign immunity, making federal agencies subject to state authorities and permit requirements.
 
- Taken together, under the CWA federal agencies are subject to state water pollution and water quality requirements whether “substantive or procedural.” Section 401 is written broadly for authorizing states and tribes, among other things, to include conditions in the certification related to “any requirement of state law” and should be construed and applied in a manner that enables states to apply their standards as fully as the statute allows.
 
- I recommend EPA continuing to use the existing Section 401 regulations.

Title: Comment submitted by Jason Locke
Comment:
To the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency,

The changes would destroy Long-Term Water Protection. Water is the foundation of life on Earth, without it everything and everyone will die. 
The rule arbitrarily limits states to reviewing only the specific "discharge" from a pipe, rather than the impact of the whole project (such as a pipeline or mine). In the short term, this might speed up permits for industry. But in the long term, it is a disaster.

Why it fails: It ignores how projects actually destroy water quality—by destroying wetlands that filter pollution, increasing runoff, or altering stream flows. By forcing states to ignore these long-term, cumulative impacts, the EPA is guaranteeing that our rivers and drinking water sources will degrade over time. We are sacrificing permanent water health for temporary construction speed.

By prohibiting states from asking for more time or more information from applicants, the rule forces rushed decisions. In the short term, this benefits project developers. In the long term, it ensures that when complex projects are proposed, the state will have to deny the permit outright rather than work with the applicant to find solutions. This creates an adversarial system that hurts everyone and leads to litigation rather than cooperation.

It Ignores the reality of pollution!
The EPA argues that states should only look at the "discharge" because that is what the law regulates. However, this ignores that pollution happens throughout a project's lifecycle. If a company clear-cuts forests and builds roads for a pipeline, the resulting sedimentation and runoff pollutes water just as surely as a pipe does. By banning states from looking at the "activity as a whole," this rule gives polluters a license to trash the landscape around our waterways.

This rule trades our clean water for the illusion of efficiency. It weakens the  structure of cooperative federalism that has protected American waters for 50 years. I urge the EPA to withdraw this harmful rulemaking immediately.

Title: Comment submitted by Suzanne Ball
Comment:
I am writing to express strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed 2026 revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certification regulations. The proposed rule would significantly weaken the ability of states and federally recognized Tribes to protect their waters—and therefore the health, safety, and welfare of their residents—by severely narrowing the scope of Section 401 reviews.

Under the current framework, states and Tribes have clear authority to evaluate whether an entire activity, not just a single discharge, will comply with applicable water quality requirements. This broader authority has been recognized for years as essential to ensuring that federally permitted projects do not harm local ecosystems or public health. Existing regulations allow certifying authorities to review whether an “activity” complies with “applicable water quality requirements,” giving them the necessary tools to prevent harmful environmental and community impacts. 

However, EPA’s newly proposed 2026 rule would dramatically narrow this review authority and limit state and Tribal certifications only to discharges “from a point source into waters of the United States,” eliminating consideration of broader project impacts.  Such a narrowing directly undermines the intent of Section 401, which Congress designed as a direct grant of power to states and Tribes to safeguard water quality within their borders. 

By restricting Section 401 reviews to a narrow set of discharge‑related factors, the proposed rule prevents states and Tribes from addressing critical non‑discharging aspects of major infrastructure projects—such as pipeline routes, hydropower operations, habitat alteration, and cumulative impacts—that can materially harm water resources and public health. Analysts have noted that the proposal would limit the discretion of state agencies and narrow states’ ability to regulate non-discharging aspects of projects, ultimately benefiting developers at the expense of community health protections. 

This rollback poses serious risks, especially for vulnerable communities that rely on state and Tribal agencies to advocate for their health, water security, and environmental well‑being. Weakening Section 401 will make it harder for local governments to stand against projects that may be ethically questionable, environmentally destructive, or dangerous for public health. States and Tribes must retain the authority to deny or condition certifications when projects threaten drinking water sources, fisheries, culturally significant resources, and the long‑term sustainability of watersheds.

For these reasons, I strongly urge EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and instead preserve the broader, activity‑wide review authority restored in the 2023 rulemaking. States and Tribes are indispensable co‑regulators in protecting our nation’s waters, and their ability to defend the health and interests of their citizens must not be diminished.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Title: Comment submitted by One Mississippi
Comment:
Dear Administrator Zeldin,

We are writing you today on behalf of the Mighty Mississippi River. The Mississippi River supplies drinking water, supports agriculture, facilitates transport, and enriches our cultural heritage. It’s essential that we honor the rights of states, tribes, and local communities to review and, if necessary, halt projects that could endanger their water quality. The well-being of this precious waterway, vital to millions, deserves our strongest protection now more than ever. 

One Mississippi is an expanding national initiative that includes more than 20,000 individuals and over 77 organizations dedicated to safeguarding the future of the Mississippi River.

On behalf of One Mississippi, we request EPA reconsider its recent update to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, which limits the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and potentially block them if they pose a threat to waterways, wetlands, and, ultimately, clean drinking water.

The Mississippi River is a crucial component of the United States' ecological and cultural landscape, serving as a critical artery for economic activities, a vital habitat for diverse wildlife, and a key resource for recreational opportunities. The challenges of water quality and pollution require significant increases in support for communities and businesses reliant on this waterway, rather than reductions in protection. It is essential to address the array of threats facing the river and its dependent ecosystems to ensure the Mississippi River remains resilient and functioning. Protections, including the Clean Water Act, must be strengthened rather than weakened, as they are integral to enhancing the river’s health.

Extending over 10 states and encompassing 123 counties, the Mississippi River flows from its headwaters in Minnesota to its mouth in Louisiana. It transports more water than any other river in the United States and serves as the primary drinking water source for over 20 million people.  The people of these communities deserve input, say, and control over what happens to their water. 

The collaboration between federal and state governments is essential to our democracy. Section 401 has played a vital role in prioritizing the public's health over polluters' interests. In light of recent federal rollbacks on clean water protections, the contributions from state, tribal, and local communities are more crucial than ever!

We strongly encourage the EPA to reconsider the proposed revisions to Section 401 and not limit states and tribal governments' authority to safeguard access to clean water. This decision is crucial, and it's important that we protect our environment and uphold our standards! 

Thank you for your consideration.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Thank you, EPA, for accepting my comments.  Do I think they will change anything?  Absolutely not.  The current Trump administration has shown they will do anything to destroy the Clean Water Act, so millionaires and billionaires can develop and do whatever they want. So, I'm not hopeful anything good will come out of this.  
1.  I request an extension of the public comment period.  30 days is not enough for something this substantial.  
2.  The public meetings the EPA put on were a joke!  EPA employees were not allowed to answer any questions.  Also, you had a mining lobbyist heading the "meetings".  Really? What a joke!  I don't know how anyone can take this seriously.  
3.  As proposed, the rule would limit certifying authorities to only being able to review point source discharges into waters of the US.  Currently, certifying authorities are allowed to use the "activity" approach and account for nonpoint source impacts and impacts to state waters.  How can this be taken seriously?  What a joke!!!!  How are certifying authorities going to review projects for compliance with water quality requirements if they can't even account for all impacts to water quality?  Under the proposed rule, if someone wanted to build a dam, certifying authorities would only be able to look at the point source discharges of the dam!  Anything that is deemed "nonpoint" cannot be accounted for, despite previous Supreme Court rulings.  Water levels, dissolved oxygen levels downstream, water temperatures upstream and downstream...none of this can be accounted for.  This will result in certifying authorities issuing water quality certifications for projects that do NOT COMPLY with existing, EPA-approved water quality criteria.  What if a company wants to build a massive plant with discharges to both waters of the US and state waterbodies?  Certifying authorities may be issuing a water quality certification for a project that will discharge into state waters that doesn't comply with water quality criteria or TMDLs!  What happens when a project that has received a water quality certification is built and ready to discharge, but their is not enough wasteload allocation left for them to discharge into state waters?  What happens?!  Do they just get to sue the certifying authority and get to discharge?  What if the applicant wants to build a ditch that leads directly to waters of the US, but it isn't classified as waters of the US itself.  The applicant has decided they will discharge extremely radioactive wastewater into this ditch.  Oh well!  Certifying authorities don't get to account for this point source discharge to waters of the US in their review.  This is severely limiting and essentially removing certifying authorities from the water quality certification and federal permitting process.  
4.  Under the proposed rule, certifying authorities, the federal agency, and the applicant have to agree to a modification.  How does this make sense?  If an applicant changes their project, but they don't agree to the modification, oh well I guess the certifying authorities and federal agencies are out of luck!  Applicants can just do what they want!  Only certifying authorities and the federal permitting agency should be able to decide when a modification can happen.  
5.  Under the proposed rule, the reasonable period of time starts when the applicant submits the water quality certification application.  What if the application has incorrect information?  Oh well, the certifying authority has to wait however long for the applicant to submit the correct information.  Certifying authorities should be allowed to determine when an application is complete and when the reasonable period of time starts.  
6.  The EPA has asked if certifying authorities should only be able to use numeric water quality criteria in water quality certification decisions.  Um, NO!!!!!  States have EPA-approved narrative/general water quality criteria that is used all the time!  
7.  The EPA has asked if they should remove general federal permits from CWA 401.  Absolutely not!  If this is the case, certifying authorities would never see general permits and the federal agency would be allowed to change them however they want.  These projects would end up not complying with existing water quality criteria.  
In short, this proposed rule is absolutely ridiculous.  It is not cooperative federalism.  This is a power grab that is removing states from the federal permitting process.  The rule should remain as is.  There is nothing wrong with the 2023 401 rule.  This is opening up certifying authorities to litigation, both from the public and future presidential administrations.

I respectfully submit this comment in strong opposition to the proposed Updated Definition of “Waters of the United States” by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the United States Army Corps of Engineers. The rule conflicts with the underlying purpose of the Clean Water Act (CWA) to “restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters”¹ and would substantially diminish federal protections for streams, wetlands, and other waters that are vital to public health, ecological integrity, flood resilience, and drinking-water security.
Under the proposed definition, federal jurisdiction would be limited primarily to relatively permanent waters or wetlands with a continuous surface connection to those waters. This narrow test disregards the substantial body of scientific evidence showing that intermittent streams, seasonal wetlands, and groundwater‐connected systems play indispensable roles in filtering pollutants, reducing floodwaters, recharging groundwater, supporting biodiversity, and sustaining downstream aquatic life. The proposal mirrors the standard set forth in Sackett v. EPA, which rejected the “significant nexus” framework in favor of a rigid surface‐connection test.²
The consequences are profound. A 2025 GIS-modeling study by the Natural Resources Defense Council (NRDC) estimates that under the least restrictive interpretation, 19 million acres of wetlands may fall outside federal protection — and under a more restrictive interpretation, up to 70 million acres or roughly 84% of previously protected wetlands could lose safeguards.³–⁴ The Environmental Defense Fund (EDF) reports similar risks, estimating that up to 90 million acres of non-tidal wetlands may now be at risk under the new standard.⁵ These losses carry real-world ramifications for flood control, water purification, habitat protection, and climate resilience.
Moreover, regulatory protection is shifting to the states—many of which lack the statutory authority, program capacity, or political will to fill the gap. An analysis by the Environmental Law Institute finds that only about half of U.S. states maintain comprehensive standalone wetland protection programs, meaning many newly-non-WOTUS waters go unregulated.⁶ The American Bar Association (ABA) confirms that states are “struggling to fill the gap” in aquatic resource protections.⁷ This regulatory fragmentation weakens protections and creates a patchwork system where polluters may exploit discrepancies across jurisdictions.
At the local level, states like Illinois already face massive exposure: a recent study showed that up to 563,000 acres of Illinois wetlands might be entirely unprotected due to the rule change.⁸ The implications for water quality, public health, and flood risk are especially acute for downstream communities and under-resourced regions.
Finally, narrowing federal jurisdiction introduces substantial legal uncertainty, opening the door to increased litigation over jurisdictional determinations, prolonged permitting processes, and uneven enforcement that advantaged well-resourced polluters can exploit.
In sum, the proposed definition of Waters of the United States contradicts the fundamental goals of the Clean Water Act. It trades proven science and public-interest protection for narrower jurisdiction and regulatory ease. I urge EPA and the Corps not to finalize any rule that excludes wetlands or streams lacking a continuous surface connection. Instead, a science-based, robust definition must be adopted — one that protects all waters that materially affect downstream integrity, public health, and environmental justice.
Thank you for your time and careful consideration.
Footnotes / References:
Clean Water Act, 33 U.S.C. § 1251 (“restore and maintain … integrity of the Nation’s waters”).
Sackett v. EPA, 598 U.S. ___ (2023).
NRDC. Mapping Destruction: Using GIS Modeling to Show the Disastrous Impacts of Sackett v. EPA on America’s Wetlands. 2025.
NRDC press release, “New Report Reveals Massive Loss of Wetland Protections After Court Decision.” March 24 2025.
EDF. “Science publishes EDF analysis warning up to 90 million acres of wetlands may now be at risk.” Sept 26 2024.

ABA Section of Environment, Energy, & Resources. “States Struggle to Fill the Gap in Aquatic Resource Protections After Sackett.” 2024.
Study: University of Illinois, “72 % of Illinois Wetlands No Longer Protected Under the CWA.” 2025.

Title: Comment submitted by Bruce Hilbach-Barger
Comment:
Dear EPA

I urge the EPA to reconsider its recent update of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act limiting the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and clean, drinking water.

I have been professionally involved in regulated water quality testing, compliance and improvement since 1979 and completed 4 years of post-secondary education in industrial chemistry. I have worked in and studied forestry and forest products, agriculture, and community scale economic development including projects addressing environmental remediation, energy development and road building. A family member was poisoned in utero by non point source pollution regulatory failure, resulting in years of health and development challenges and a lifetime of physical effects. Three family members have been diagnosed (one fatally) with a type of cancer known to be related to a different environmental toxin; in that case economic considerations were put foremost in a so-called emergency. I am a board member of a community water quality agency and as such am an elected official of the State of California.

The proposed change would decrease "regulatory uncertainty" by increasing water quality uncertainty. This trade-off violates the intent of the Clean Water Act section 401 and common sense. First, the success of regulatory efforts to provide clean water in a particular place depends directly on the ability communities, states and tribes of that place to assess the real dangers of proposed actions. This proposed rule merely trades decreased effectiveness for decreased cost to applicants. Second, cooperation between EPA and local agencies including tribes can increase effectiveness in providing clean water. This rule reduces federal commitment to cooperation, replacing direct representation with hidden political processes at the federal level. Third, the goal of this proposed rule change is to provide financial certainty for permit applicants which is not a valid goal for any governmental action in a free market or of the Clean Water Act. All investment involves financial risk. Genuinely profitable actions require careful design and execution not abandonment of costs in the public space. This proposed rule reduces the ability of the EPA to consider cumulative effects, downstream effects and effects over time, violating the intent of the Clean Water Act by reducing the ability of the EPA to assure actual human access to clean water and reducing availability of clean water for other living beings and natural processes that support human life.
"Decreasing regulatory uncertainty" is an illogical goal. Common sense and careful thought make clear that "regulatory certainty" only occurs at two levels of regulation: one that requires all applications to be approved or one that requires no applications to be approved. Regulatory stability, effectiveness and responsiveness are goals that can be pursued and measured to achieve both a supportive and effective regulatory structure for clean water. The likely outcome of this rule change is only to increase false appearances of financial profits, subsidized by direct human harm and indirect human harm through damage to life and ecosystems that support us.
There are reasonable alternatives to the proposed rule change. The EPA and congress could design rule updates that pair maximum regulatory effectiveness with a minimum regulatory burden to achieve that effectiveness. Maximum achievable effectiveness of rules would create a regulatory environment such that environmental failures do not undermine flexibility or require emergency actions. Regulatory timelines could be rationalized (not merely shortened) to stabilize the risks of permit applications. EPA could provide greater appropriate support and guidance to applicants through many means. Under no circumstances is it appropriate for EPA to trade health for a reduction in normal business risks associated with investments.

As a board member of my local water district I am actively involved in a cooperative effort between state, local and tribal agencies to improve the water quality that underpins the economy and ecology of my community. Direct cooperation with US EPA has been a part of my other efforts to improve water quality in the entire river watershed and would improve many other projects I am familiar with. To reduce the ability of EPA to consult with and receive support from our local entities when permits are being considered will limit all of our effectiveness.

The EPA's proposed rule weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protection and the ability of states and tribes to review and possibly block projects that may threaten public health and degrade the waterways and wetlands within their borders. I urge the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401.

Sincerely,
Bruce

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
Ms. Lauren Kasparek 
Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division 
Environmental Protection Agency, Office of Water 
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW 
Washington, DC 20460 

Subject: Comments RE: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 

Dear Ms. Kasparek, 

I am writing as a concerned environmentalist and public citizen regarding the Federal Register Notice, “Updating Water Quality Certification Regulations,” Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929. 

On January 13, 2026, the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) announced a proposed rule to update and clarify the procedural requirements under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). The CWA, particularly Section 401, has strong ramifications for water quality protection in the United States. Section 401 enables states, territories, and authorized Tribes to protect their water quality from adverse impacts of construction or operation of federally permitted projects. Specifically, a federal agency may not issue a license or permit to conduct any activity that may result in any discharge into a water of the United States, unless the appropriate state, territory, or authorized Tribe issues a CWA Section 401 water quality certification or waives certification. 

Under the first Trump Administration, in 2020, the EPA finalized a rule that weakened Section 401 requirements, and the Biden Administration in 2023 finalized a rule revising and replacing the 2020 rule. EPA’s 2023 rule realigned the scope of Section 401 certification with decades of established practice and restores and strengthens the role of states, territories, and authorized Tribes. Under this most recent proposed rule, states and authorized Tribes would be restricted to only reviewing the direct water-quality impacts of a project’s discharges into federally protected waters, meaning they would no longer have the authority to consider broader water-quality effects, thus weakening oversight of projects as a whole. 

I urge EPA to consider maintaining the current procedural scope of Section 401 to better align with the goals of the CWA and to ensure states and Tribes have the ability to protect the waters within their borders. Any changes to the scope of CWA authority should strengthen, rather than weaken, water quality protections, especially in light of weakened WOTUS protections in the outcome of Sackett v. EPA. An increase of pollutants in our surface waters and fewer healthy wetlands and streams also means that fish and wildlife that rely on these waters for habitat will suffer as well. According to the EPA, more than one-third of the United States' threatened and endangered species live only in wetlands, and nearly half use wetlands at some point in their lives. 

Sustainable water management is also vital in our fight against climate change. According to the United Nations Environment Programme, wetlands serve as highly effective carbon sinks that absorb and store CO2, helping to reduce greenhouse gas emissions. They also serve as a buffer against extreme weather events and absorb excessive precipitation. 

I appreciate the opportunity to provide input, and I urge EPA to give serious consideration to its mission to protect public health and the environment when finalizing this rule.

Comment:
Comment regarding: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

To whom is may concern, 
      The proposed revisions to Section 401 of the Water Quality Certification Regulations are directly going to reduce state authority on this matter. The proposed changes to alter how extension times to grant certifications are made, is going to greatly reduce the time for state authorities to gather resources and information needed to make proper decisions. The proposed changes to Section 401's wording also reduces the spectrum of protection The State can provide in order to keep our waters clean. The proposed wording not only reduces what can be considered protected under this legislation, but also reduces the power granted to The State in order to make decisions regarding the waters they govern. The proposed changes of wording limit The State's broad authority, which could make it easier for certain parties to circumvent or abuse certification regulations. 
       I also find the proposed changes to limit "water quality requirements" to only numeric value to be another way to limit a state's authority on granting a certification. This, plus the proposal of requiring that denied certifications have specific measures of how their proposed project violates the CWA, are measure that could allow certification seekers to oppose state rulings through loopholes. It also limits what is considered pollution and how pollution is recorded, which could allow for a greater number of pollutants to enter our waters. 
        The changes proposed to Section 401 are not only unnecessary, but harmful. They directly lower the protection currently in place to keep our waters clean through strategic changes to wording. They limit the power of The State, by hindering or denying a state from their right in deciding what projects can occur in an area they will be directly impacted. These proposed changes empower the rights of companies while limiting environmental protections, which can cause further pollutants to enter our waters, endangering wildlife and American citizens alike. Do not pass this proposed rule.

Title: Comment submitted by Lynda Heideman
Comment:
RE: Docket ID EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations

I strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act, Section 401, Water Quality Certifications. For more than 50 years, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act has been a critical tool that enables states and the federal government to cooperatively manage our shared water resources, that ensures local voices are regarded in the process, and that safeguards processes resulting in stronger, more durable projects. The proposed rulemaking is contrary to the language of the Clean Water Act and would greatly restrict states’ and Tribes’ long-standing authority to protect waters within their borders.

SCOPE OF CERTIFICATION AUTHORITY. The proposed rule narrows the scope of states’ and Tribes’ certification authority to water quality-related conditions that may be applied only to the discharge, not to the project as a whole.

Water quality certification is one of the most important and effective tools available to states and Tribes to ensure federally licensed projects are protective of water quality for drinking and recreation and as aquatic habitat for fish and wildlife. The proposed rule would greatly undermine state and Tribal authority to protect their waters by restricting the type of protective conditions states and Tribes can put on federally licensed or permitted activities that threaten state or Tribal waters.

States and Tribes must be able to…
> Review oil and gas infrastructure, data centers, hydropower projects, roads, etc. that could violate their water-quality standards.
> Consider spill risks, threats to cultural sites, and impacts on fish and wildlife when weighing in on federal projects. 
> Evaluate infrastructure that could cause higher water temperatures and habitat degradation that would be detrimental to fish and wildlife, as well as a loss of recreation and clean, flowing water for communities. 
> Protect the waters within their borders, which has been their long-standing authority.

TIMING AND INFORMATION REQUIREMENTS. The proposed rule establishes strict timeline and information requirements for states and Tribes to consider a request for certification. States and Tribes must have adequate time and information to review and process complex applications and the ability to impose conditions on permits that will protect water quality, especially given the differences in types of resources and water-quality standards among states.

IN CONCLUSION. In 2023, the EPA appropriately reasserted the strong role that states and Tribes should have in protecting their waters. That  role—which has been the norm for most of the last 55 years—must not be weakened. I urge you to withdraw the proposed rule and ask that you uphold the longstanding authority of states and Tribes to protect waters within their boundaries.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Title: Comment submitted by Jeannie Ambrose
Comment:
EPA Proposed Rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations
Section 401 of the Clean Water Act
Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Public Comment
February 17, 2026

I am opposed to the proposed procedural changes to section 401 of the Clean Water Act governing the certification of water quality. The proposed streamlining of the WQ certification process for federally permitted major projects limits the authority of the state(s) and tribal power to protect its local waterways from the detrimental impacts of water pollution. The ability to protect aquatic ecosystems is essential. Therefore, a point source discharge into federally protected waters should not be the only factor that should be considered in the review process. The total impact of project activities, such as, those potentially occurring during the construction phase, may cause significant–even irreparable–harm to water quality. And, in those very extreme cases, cannot be mitigated. 

A careful review of the application for large scale projects may very well result in permit delays/time extensions. Yes, delays of any kind in the procedural permitting review can be touted as bad for business. But not having all the necessary information to evaluate the content of a permit is even more egregious: bad for environmental sustainability. Surely, there are better ways to expediate the 401 permits and still retain the regulatory status of states and tribal nations [even those seeking TAS status] to meaningfully review 401 WQ permits. Both entities should be allowed to keep their legal regulatory authority and given the time to carefully assess and provide input on how best to minimize any potential ecological impacts. Some large-scale federal projects should be denied–but very, very few are. 

Thank you for the opportunity to submit public comments on the EPA’s Proposed Rule on Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.

Title: Comment submitted by Keith Liuzzi
Comment:
Docket ID# EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
The proposed rules restrict state and tribal sovereignty when addressing protection of our waterways and wetlands.

I appreciate the opportunity to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water.

The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. 

The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically:


1.) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations.

2.) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park.

3.) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water.

4.) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 

5.) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? 

The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish.

Thank you for considering my comments

Title: Comment submitted by Bruce Hilbach-Barger
Comment:
Dear EPA

I urge the EPA to reconsider its recent update of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act limiting the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and clean, drinking water.

I have been professionally involved in regulated water quality testing, compliance and improvement since 1979 and completed 4 years of post-secondary education in industrial chemistry. I have worked in and studied forestry and forest products, agriculture, and community scale economic development including projects addressing environmental remediation, energy development and road building. A family member was poisoned in utero by non point source pollution regulatory failure, resulting in years of health and development challenges and a lifetime of physical effects. Three family members have been diagnosed (one fatally) with a type of cancer known to be related to a different environmental toxin; in that case economic considerations were put foremost in a so-called emergency. I am a board member of a community water quality agency and as such am an elected official of the State of California.

The proposed change would decrease "regulatory uncertainty" by increasing water quality uncertainty. This trade-off violates the intent of the Clean Water Act section 401 and common sense. First, the success of regulatory efforts to provide clean water in a particular place depends directly on the ability communities, states and tribes of that place to assess the real dangers of proposed actions. This proposed rule merely trades decreased effectiveness for decreased cost to applicants. Second, cooperation between EPA and local agencies including tribes can increase effectiveness in providing clean water. This rule reduces federal commitment to cooperation, replacing direct representation with hidden political processes at the federal level. Third, the goal of this proposed rule change is to provide financial certainty for permit applicants which is not a valid goal for any governmental action in a free market or of the Clean Water Act. All investment involves financial risk. Genuinely profitable actions require careful design and execution not abandonment of costs in the public space. This proposed rule reduces the ability of the EPA to consider cumulative effects, downstream effects and effects over time, violating the intent of the Clean Water Act by reducing the ability of the EPA to assure actual human access to clean water and reducing availability of clean water for other living beings and natural processes that support human life.
"Decreasing regulatory uncertainty" is an illogical goal. Common sense and careful thought make clear that "regulatory certainty" only occurs at two levels of regulation: one that requires all applications to be approved or one that requires no applications to be approved. Regulatory stability, effectiveness and responsiveness are goals that can be pursued and measured to achieve both a supportive and effective regulatory structure for clean water. The likely outcome of this rule change is only to increase false appearances of financial profits, subsidized by direct human harm and indirect human harm through damage to life and ecosystems that support us.
There are reasonable alternatives to the proposed rule change. The EPA and congress could design rule updates that pair maximum regulatory effectiveness with a minimum regulatory burden to achieve that effectiveness. Maximum achievable effectiveness of rules would create a regulatory environment such that environmental failures do not undermine flexibility or require emergency actions. Regulatory timelines could be rationalized (not merely shortened) to stabilize the risks of permit applications. EPA could provide greater appropriate support and guidance to applicants through many means. Under no circumstances is it appropriate for EPA to trade health for a reduction in normal business risks associated with investments.

As a board member of my local water district I am actively involved in a cooperative effort between state, local and tribal agencies to improve the water quality that underpins the economy and ecology of my community. Direct cooperation with US EPA has been a part of my other efforts to improve water quality in the entire river watershed and would improve many other projects I am familiar with. To reduce the ability of EPA to consult with and receive support from our local entities when permits are being considered will limit all of our effectiveness.

The EPA's proposed rule weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protection and the ability of states and tribes to review and possibly block projects that may threaten public health and degrade the waterways and wetlands within their borders. I urge the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401.

Sincerely,

Title: Comment submitted by Rebekah Dubois
Comment:
Regarding Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 to all concerned persons,
 
I am writing to urge that the EPA withdraws its proposed revisions to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. It is my sincerest belief that this proposal would drastically limit the ability of states and tribal governments to protect our water quality from potential harm due to industrial, commercial, and other types of activity.

Federally permitted projects can and may threaten the United States' drinking water, fisheries, and eventually, public health if the proper safeguards are not in place.

Section 401 represents cooperative federalism and ensures that local professional expertise and community voices are not ignored or overridden by federal decisions. Weakening this authority would disproportionately harm communities that are directly exposed to affected waterways and underserved communities affected by existing oversight issues. Changing the existing writing of the Clean Water Act to reflect these revisions would make it more difficult, time-consuming, and ultimately more wasteful to federal and state resources should pollution affect water resources.

The proposed rule undermines decades of effective clean water protections. The proposals reflect and prioritize industry convenience and commercial expedience over public and environmental health.

I sincerely urge the EPA to reject this proposal and fully uphold Section 401 as a means to protect our water resources.

Thank you for your time and consideration. In the future, may renewed interest in water conservation continue to drive improvements to the Clean Water Act.

Sincerely,

A concerned friend and individual of the United States

Title: Comment submitted by Cal Curtice
Comment:
To the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency:

On behalf of the New York State Council of Trout Unlimited—representing thousands of dedicated coldwater conservationists and anglers across the Empire State—I am writing to express our unwavering opposition to the proposed rule revising Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. These changes represent a direct assault on the state and Tribal authorities that serve as the last line of defense for New York’s iconic trout streams and the fragile aquatic ecosystems they support.

The Vital Role of State Authority
In New York, Section 401 is not just a bureaucratic procedure; it is a critical safeguard. From the legendary tailwaters of the Delaware River to the pristine headwaters of the Adirondacks, state-led water quality certifications ensure that federally permitted projects—such as hydropower dams, pipelines, and large-scale infrastructure—do not degrade our coldwater fisheries. By narrowing the scope of these reviews, the EPA is effectively stripping New York of its ability to protect its own natural heritage.

Ecological Integrity vs. Narrow Definitions
The proposal’s focus on "direct discharge" rather than the total project impact ignores the fundamental science of stream ecology. Our coldwater species, particularly our state fish, the Brook Trout, do not just rely on clean water; they rely on:

Thermal Consistency: Temperature spikes from clearing riparian buffers can be fatal.

Flow Regimes: Altered hydrology destroys spawning beds (redds).

Habitat Connectivity: Improperly vetted infrastructure creates barriers to fish passage.

To ignore these "secondary" effects is to ignore the biological reality of a living river. A project that maintains water chemistry but destroys habitat connectivity is still a failure of conservation.

Protecting Our Investment
NYSCTU and our partners invest millions of dollars and countless volunteer hours into restoring New York’s watersheds. The proposed rule would undermine these efforts by weakening the state’s power to mandate essential mitigation, such as:

Seasonal construction moratoriums to protect sensitive spawning windows.

Minimum flow requirements to prevent localized extinctions during droughts.

Advanced sediment controls to protect macroinvertebrate populations.

Conclusion
This proposal, coupled with recent attempts to roll back protections for ephemeral and intermittent streams, creates a dangerous "death by a thousand cuts" scenario for our headwaters. Clean, cold, fishable water is the backbone of New York’s outdoor economy and the heart of our communities.

We urge the EPA to withdraw this rule. Any revision to Section 401 must empower states to fulfill the Clean Water Act’s original mandate: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of our nation’s waters.

Sincerely,

Cal Curtice
New York State Council of Trout Unlimited

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
As a stakeholder, I strongly oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.

The proposal would unlawfully and dangerously narrow the authority of states and tribes to protect their waters from harmful projects — especially large oil and gas pipelines. By limiting review to narrowly defined “discharges” and constraining timelines and conditions, the rule undermines Congress’s clear intent that states and tribes serve as frontline protectors of water quality.

This rollback is especially concerning for the Great Lakes, which hold 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and supply drinking water to more than 40 million people. In Michigan, the proposal could directly affect decisions regarding Enbridge’s Line 5 oil pipeline, including a proposed tunnel and potential horizontal directional drilling beneath the Straits of Mackinac—an area of extraordinary ecological, cultural, and economic significance.

Enbridge has delayed a final decision on its Clean Water Act Section 404 permit from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. If EPA finalizes this rule, it could affect the outcome by restricting Michigan’s ability—and tribal nations’ treaty-protected authority—to fully evaluate and reject water quality risks.

States and tribes must retain the power to:
 • Consider cumulative and long-term water quality impacts
 • Protect treaty rights, fisheries, and drinking water
 • Deny certification when projects threaten water quality standards

The EPA should withdraw this proposal and instead strengthen, not weaken, Clean Water Act protections. The Great Lakes are too important to risk for the convenience of oil pipeline companies.

Sincerely,

Title: Comment submitted by Lauren Gygax
Comment:
Comment Submitted to Federal Register Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929
Dear EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin, 

I am writing to express my opposition to the EPA's proposed rule to change procedural requirements for water quality certification under Clean Water Act (CWA) section 401. The proposed rules would reduce the ability of states and tribes to evaluate the overall impacts of a federal infrastructure project on local waterways. 

The Clean Water Act empowers states and localities to examine how federal infrastructure projects, including pipelines, may impact local water quality. When a state or locality determines that an infrastructure project harms local waterways, they can leverage Section 401 of the Clean Water Act to reject infrastructure permits. In 2023, the Biden Administration empowered states and tribes to consider all the ways in which infrastructure projects affect water quality, including the risk of spills, possible harms to local wildlife, and impacts on cultural resources. 

The reduced scope of Section 401 under the proposed rule could result in damage to waterways other than through direct pollution; it could cut off drinking water, disrupt the migration of aquatic waterways, and control floodwaters. The proposed rule would also narrow the scope of tribal oversight by requiring that they evaluate projects through a separate authorization program. If finalized, this proposed rule would impose greater demands on tribes to prove that they have large-scale capacity to undergo a rigorous review of federal projects, even as much of the country grapples with restricted local government funding. Reducing the scope of the Clean Water Act's Section 401 to only account for how direct discharges into waterways impact water quality will hinder the ability of state and tribal governments to fully consider how projects impact local communities and wildlife, with possible threats to public health, natural environments, and local economies. 

Because of the ways in which the proposed rule would limit states and tribes' ability to fully protect local waterways and the communities that depend on them, I strongly urge the EPA to withdraw the rule. Instead, the EPA should maintain Section 401 of the Clean Water Act as it is currently written.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I oppose the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed a Section 401 rule change. It would tie the hands of states and tribal nations and make it easier for fossil fuel companies to push through new pipelines and massive oil-and-gas infrastructure, even when those projects threaten local waterways and public health. 

This proposed Section 401 rule change is nothing more than a giveaway to big oil and gas corporate polluters, and a direct attack on climate action and public health. Both of which are in violation of the EPA's mission. 

It would weaken how states and tribes can use the Clean Water Act to stop harmful projects, effectively fast-tracking fossil fuel expansion at the exact moment we need to be slashing emissions. It would also result in more pollution, more climate disasters, and more communities forced to accept pipeline risks they did not agreed to. 

States and tribal nations use the Clean Water Act to protect their communities from projects that threaten rivers, wetlands, drinking water, and public health. More pipelines equal more fossil fuels burned. More fossil fuels burned equals more climate chaos. Pipelines do not just move oil and gas, they lock in decades of emissions. They fuel oil and methane leaks, fires, and explosions, and long-term dependence on energy that is overheating our planet. 

Communities are already living through climate disasters: extreme heat, historic flooding, megafires, and stronger storms. The EPA should be shutting down the fossil fuel pipeline, not rubber-stamping new ones.

I oppose this Section 401 rule change and the EPA's attempt to silence local communities, undercut tribal sovereignty, and make it easier for corporate polluters to override the people who actually live with the consequences.

Title: Comment submitted by Climate Action & Sustainability Planning, LLC
Comment:
Subject line: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929


California’s Central Valley, the breadbasket of America, and one of the greatest surface freshwater systems in the world, relies on wetlands, rivers, and streams to filter pollution, prevent flooding in our communities, and support fishing and recreation.  California’s agricultural businesses generate billions in tax revenue for our nation while feeding millions with the rice, nuts, fruits and vegetables grown here. The Clean Water Act has been instrumental in protecting this economic engine. 


The Central Valley Project and the various reservoirs and river systems that comprise it, all considered Waters of the United States (WOTUS), demand adequate Federal protection from upstream pollution sources. It is critical that the U.S. EPA does not degrade the Clean Water Act and weaken stakeholders’ ability to protect sources that feed WOTUS in California. 


The proposed rule states at page 2040 of the Fed Reg notice:


Section 121.3 Scope of certification. The scope of a Clean Water Act section 401 certification is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements. 


The proposed rule change to Section 401 seeks to take control away from states and Tribes, thus loosening the clean water protections that have broad appeal across all elements of our economy. This rule limits stakeholders to reviewing only the direct water-quality impacts of a project’s discharges into federally protected waters, and would no longer be able to consider broader water-quality effects caused by the project in its entirety. As such, the proposed regulation is an example of Federal overreach into local governance and control of waterways, where states and authorized tribes have the best ability to review overall impacts to water quality from local activities. 


This proposed rule change will further strip protections of wetlands, which provide significant regional economic benefits. These include:


Recreational activities in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. According to the Delta Stewardship Council’s 2013 Delta Plan, recreational activities are worth an estimated $350 million annually to the region. The ability of the community to continue to benefit from this revenue-generating activity is being harmed by harmful algae blooms that result from upstream pollution. Reducing California’s ability to act on upstream pollution sources will harm this significant industry.
In Yolo County, where I live, we have had to switch our drinking water sources from groundwater to a combination of groundwater and river water from the Sacramento River. Our community is dependent now on WOTUS for our drinking water. We need the State of California and our upstream neighbor, the Yocha Dehe Wintun tribe, to be able to implement policies to ensure that the flow of water from Cache Creek is able to recharge groundwater sources as well as feed into the Sacramento River through the wetlands in Yolo County. The proposed rule would diminish their ability to enact policies that ensure clean drinking water for our communities.
Further, in Yolo County, the Cache Creek watershed has been declared an impaired waterway under the Clean Water Act due to mercury discharges from legacy mining operations. As Cache Creek does not always flow directly into the Sacramento River and connects to the Central Valley system through wetlands, this change to the Clean Water Act would remove important protections that are driving action to lower mercury exposure. The proposed rule change opens up the specter of uncontrolled mercury discharge into our local waterways, water supplies and even into the air we breathe during drought years. 
Finally, in the City of Woodland, where I live, 25% of our residents reside within the 200-year flood plain. Our community is vulnerable to flooding along Cache Creek when the Sacramento River system and its wetlands are flooded. The proposed regulation entertains a fiction that these two watersheds are somehow not connected, which impacts the ability of the State of California, County of Yolo and City of Woodland, as well as the Yocha Dehe Wintun Nation upstream, to rely on a consistent, Federally-approved framework for wetlands regulation and flood control. According to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Lower Cache Creek December 2019 Draft Supplemental Environmental Impact Statement, the  potential impact of flooding to our community is as much as $2.3 billion in 2019 dollars. Changing the Clean Water Act in the way proposed here will open up our community to significant financial, human and environmental losses.


I encourage the U.S. EPA to strike this proposed rule and keep in place the rules that govern WOTUS and waters that feed into WOTUS today.

Title: Comment submitted by Zenon Kane
Comment:
Dear All,
I am concerned that the proposed rule limits state and tribal authority, narrows the review of water quality impacts to an unrealistic scope, and prioritizes permitting speed over protecting water quality and public health. These changes risk overlooking cumulative pollution, local conditions, and downstream impacts that directly affect communities.
I oppose the proposed updates to the Water Quality Certification Regulations because they weaken important safeguards that protect water resources people rely on every day for drinking, fishing, farming, and recreation. By restricting what states and tribes are allowed to consider during certification, the proposal may fail to account for how pollution actually behaves in the environment, including runoff, sedimentation, and pollutants that accumulate over time or travel downstream.
From an environmental health perspective, water quality cannot be separated from human health. Even small increases in pollution can contribute to long-term health risks, especially for children, older adults, and communities already facing higher environmental burdens. Wetlands, headwaters, and smaller waterways play a critical role in filtering pollutants and protecting downstream water supplies, yet these areas are more likely to be overlooked under a narrower review process.
The proposal also places strict limits on timelines and standardizes information requirements in a way that may reduce meaningful review. While efficiency is important, rushing decisions or restricting the ability of certifying authorities to request additional information may lead to incomplete evaluations and missed risks. Local and tribal agencies are often best positioned to understand site-specific conditions, historical pollution concerns, and community health impacts, and their expertise should be preserved rather than constrained.
States and tribes have long served as a frontline defense in protecting water quality, and reducing their role could lead to weaker protections and increased public health risks over time.
While timely infrastructure development is important, it should not come at the expense of clean water, public health, or community trust. I urge EPA to reconsider these proposed changes and retain a more flexible, comprehensive approach that allows states and tribes to fully evaluate potential water quality and health impacts before certifications are granted.

Sincerely,
Zenon Kane, MD

Title: Comment submitted by Anna Harris
Comment:
Subject: Comment on Proposed Rule Revising Clean Water Act Section 401 (Docket EPA–HQ–OW–2025–0290)
I am submitting this comment in response to the Environmental Protection Agency’s January 2026 proposed rule revising implementation of Clean Water Act Section 401, 33 U.S.C. §1341. I urge the Agency to withdraw or substantially revise the proposal because it conflicts with the statute’s text, congressional intent, and decades of cooperative practice between Federal and States management.
1. The proposal unlawfully narrows state authority.
Section 401 was enacted to give states and tribes broad power to review, condition, or deny federal permits that may affect water quality within their jurisdiction. The proposed rule improperly limits review to impacts “directly caused by a discharge,” excluding cumulative watershed impacts, construction related effects, and climate related consequences that states have historically evaluated. Nothing in the statute authorizes this narrowing.
2. The proposal undermines cooperative federalism.
Congress designed Section 401 to ensure that states—not federal agencies—serve as the primary protectors of water quality. By expanding federal authority to override state conditions and second guess the scope of state review, the proposal reverses this structure. It also contradicts decades of judicial interpretation recognizing states’ independent certification authority.
3. The proposal is inconsistent with post Sackett realities.
After Sackett v. EPA (2023) significantly reduced the number of waters protected under federal jurisdiction, states and tribes now shoulder even greater responsibility for safeguarding water resources. Weakening Section 401 at this moment creates a regulatory gap: fewer waters are federally protected, and states would have diminished tools to address the impacts of federally permitted projects.
4. The proposed timelines are unworkable and risk forced waivers.
The rule’s compressed review periods increase the likelihood that federal agencies will deem state authority “waived,” even when states are acting diligently. Large, complex projects—pipelines, hydropower, LNG terminals—require robust technical review that cannot be rushed without compromising water quality protections.
5. The rule restricts necessary and science based conditions.
States must be able to impose conditions related to construction methods, cumulative impacts, watershed health, and climate related effects when those factors influence water quality. The proposed rule’s narrow definition of “water quality requirements” prevents states from applying modern science and best practices.
Request for Action
For these reasons, I respectfully request that EPA withdraw or significantly revise the proposed rule to:
•	Restore the full scope of state and tribal certification authority
•	Preserve the ability to consider cumulative and climate related impacts
•	Maintain realistic timelines that allow for thorough scientific review
•	Ensure federal agencies cannot override state decisions that are squarely within Section 401’s statutory authority
Thank you for the opportunity to comment.

Anna Harris
Monrovia, CA

Title: Comment submitted by Alexandra Bell
Comment:
Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Clean Water Act §401)
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

I am writing in support, with recommendations, for the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed updates to the Water Quality Certification Regulations under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. From both a public health and clinical standpoint, water quality certification, when done correctly, is one of the most effective tools to prevent avoidable harm. 

While I do support the proposed clarification of Section 401 certifications, I would strongly urge the EPA to ensure that this clarification does not limit consideration of cumulative or downstream impacts. These downstream impacts may increase the risk of community exposure. From past events, we know that exposure pathways are not always from a single discharge point. This is more common for those communities relying on shared or downstream water sources.

Research literature continues to show that weaknesses in water quality protection are more often due to gaps in regulation, not isolated events. Allaire et al. found in 2022 that drinking water quality violations persist across the United States. These violations disproportionately impact smaller communities with fewer resources. 

My support for this rule is also informed by my experience as a nurse caring for patients during periods of water contamination in the Houston area. During those events, I cared for individuals presenting with gastrointestinal symptoms, skin reactions, and worsening of chronic conditions that were linked to polluted water. These encounters were clinical, but they were examples of failures in environmental protection. What was most impactful about these encounters, was how quickly an environmental issue became a healthcare issue, particularly for patients with limited ability to avoid exposure to contaminated water.

The proposed rule provides transparency and clarity in certification decisions. The proposal notes that certifying authorities must clearly explain how any condition “relates to a specific water quality requirement”. From a public health standpoint, this requirement strengthens the public's trust in the certification process. 

Protecting water quality is a public health responsibility. A strong Section 401 certification process is needed to support the Clean Water Act’s goal of protecting the Nation’s waters and the health of the communities that rely on them.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide comments on this proposed rule.

Allaire, M., Wu, H., & Lall, U. (2022). Drinking water quality violations and environmental justice in the United States. Environmental Research Letters, 17(6), 064010. https://doi.org/10.1088/1748-9326/ac6d1a

Title: Comment submitted by A. A. (no full name provided)
Comment:
Under Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (as of 2023), states and authorized tribes are to review and certify that federal projects meet water quality requirements. States and authorized tribes are able to review “activities as a whole”, meaning reviewing a major project’s development from start-to-finish for any steps that may result in water pollution. 
Congress declared as a national policy that waste treatment management interventions are developed and implemented in each state, and programs designed to control nonpoint-sourced pollution are implemented nationwide in order to effectively control point-source nonpoint-source pollution. Additionally, Congress declared that the states would receive financial support to manage and implement programs with the goal of preventing, reducing, and eliminating pollution. 
The Environmental Protective Agency (EPA) is proposing to change the “activities as a whole” idea- The EPA is proposing limiting state and authorized tribes to conduct assessments of whether facilities’ point-source discharges into water will meet water quality specifications. The proposed revisions do not allow states and tribes to use a comprehensive approach to reviewing projects, which can be detrimental to water safety. Factors such as physical infrastructure, climate change, and nonpoint-source pollution can greatly affect water safety. The proposed revisions have the good intention of streamlining the water quality certification process; however, the revisions may end up setting the United States up for increased water pollution by eliminating the broad, “activity as a whole” approach. Several water-quality problems develop from changes in runoff, sedimentation, temperature, or other water distribution factors that can be quickly addressed during the project authorization process. Thus, not restricting state and tribe authority to review “activities as a whole” allows risks to be managed early, which reduces cost and further risk of water pollution. 
 The EPA states that, “The proposed rule addresses procedural and substantive aspects of the certification process, but does not concern human health.” Limiting state and tribes from addressing nonpoint-source pollution can pose risks for the health and mortality of many aquatic species, many of which provide food, oxygen, economic stability, and climate change mitigation. Posing risks to aquatic life can subsequently threaten human life and viability of the ecosystem in general.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
To the Administrator of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA),

I am writing to provide a comment on the proposed rule redefining the "Waters of the United States" (WOTUS). I appreciate the EPA and the Army Corps of Engineers' efforts to establish a clear and legally durable definition that is consistent with the objectives of the Clean Water Act (CWA) and recent judicial decisions.

A stable, predictable, and scientifically sound definition of WOTUS is essential for the health of our nation’s water resources and for the economic certainty of its communities and industries. The constant shifting of this definition over the past decade has created confusion and uncertainty for all stakeholders, from farmers and developers to conservationists and local governments.

My comment is centered on the following principles:

The Definition Must Be Grounded in Hydrological Science: The core purpose of the Clean Water Act is to "restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation's waters." This goal cannot be achieved if the definition of WOTUS ignores the scientific reality of how water systems function. Hydrological science overwhelmingly demonstrates that smaller water bodies—including ephemeral streams, wetlands, and headwaters—have a significant nexus to the health of larger, downstream navigable rivers, lakes, and coastal waters. These smaller features are not isolated; they are integral parts of the whole, serving to filter pollutants, absorb floodwaters, and provide critical habitat. A definition that arbitrarily excludes these waters based on their size or permanence is a definition that is destined to fail in protecting the quality of our nation’s primary water sources.

Clarity and Predictability are Crucial for All Stakeholders: While environmental protection is paramount, I recognize the legitimate need for regulatory clarity from the agricultural community, land developers, and industry. A successful WOTUS rule must provide a clear and implementable framework that allows landowners to understand their obligations without needing an army of consultants and lawyers. However, this clarity should not come at the expense of environmental integrity. The most effective way to provide long-term certainty is to establish a reasonable, science-based standard that can withstand legal challenges and the test of time, rather than vacillating between overly broad and insufficiently protective definitions with every change in administration.

Protecting Downstream Water Quality is Economically Vital: Failing to protect upstream waters has significant economic consequences for downstream communities. When headwaters and wetlands are polluted or destroyed, the burden of water purification falls upon municipal water treatment facilities, costing taxpayers millions of dollars. The destruction of wetlands that serve as natural sponges for floodwaters leads to increased property damage and higher insurance costs for homeowners and businesses. Therefore, a comprehensive WOTUS definition is not an economic burden; it is a vital, cost-effective investment in protecting our communities and infrastructure.

Conclusion:

I urge the EPA to finalize a definition of "Waters of the United States" that is firmly rooted in science, provides practical clarity for landowners, and honors the fundamental goal of the Clean Water Act. Please codify a rule that protects the critical functions of our nation’s headwaters and wetlands, ensuring safe and clean water for all Americans for generations to come. The best way to end the cycle of regulatory uncertainty is to get it right, right now.

Thank you for your consideration of these comments.
