Title: Comment submitted by Julia Bach
Comment:
I am commenting on Docket ID # EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 and want to share my strong objections to the proposed rule change.
These changes would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water.

The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. 

The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically:

1.) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations.

2.) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park.

3.) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water.

4.) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 

5.) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? 

The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish.

Thank you for considering my comments,

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality.

The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources.

Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections.

Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations.

I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I strongly oppose the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed rule, published on January 13, 2026, that would amend regulations implementing Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). This proposal would significantly weaken the ability of states, Tribes, and local governments to protect their rivers, lakes, wetlands, and drinking water sources from pollution associated with federally permitted or licensed projects, including pipelines, dams, industrial facilities, and data centers.
Section 401 is a cornerstone of the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism framework. Congress deliberately designed this provision to ensure that federal permitting decisions do not override local and regional water quality protections. By empowering states and Tribes to review and condition federal permits to ensure compliance with water quality standards and other water-related requirements, Section 401 serves as one of the most effective safeguards for community waters nationwide.

Title: Anonymous public comment
Comment:
I respectfully oppose the proposed revisions to water quality certification review under Section 401. These changes significantly narrow the authority of states and tribes to evaluate the full impacts of federally permitted projects and weaken long-standing protections intended to safeguard water resources and surrounding ecosystems.

Limiting certification review strictly to specific discharge-related “water quality requirements” creates an artificial boundary that ignores the real-world interconnectedness of water systems. Water quality is not affected by discharges alone — it is influenced by broader ecological conditions, cumulative project impacts, watershed disruption, and long-term environmental stressors. Excluding these factors from review undermines comprehensive protection and prevents regulators from making fully informed decisions.

The proposal also creates inequitable treatment pathways for tribal governments by requiring them to pursue alternative authorization routes instead of recognizing direct certification authority where appropriate. This approach adds procedural burden, delays meaningful participation, and diminishes tribal sovereignty in environmental oversight compared to state counterparts.

Further, standardizing and compressing review timelines may appear efficient administratively, but in practice it reduces the opportunity for careful technical analysis, interagency coordination, and community input. Complex projects with significant environmental consequences require flexible and thorough review periods. Rigid deadlines risk rushed determinations and incomplete evaluations.

Section 401 was designed to give states and tribes a meaningful role in protecting their waters — not a narrowly constrained checkbox function. Weakening scope, limiting authority, and compressing timelines shifts the balance away from resource protection and toward procedural speed at the expense of environmental integrity.

For these reasons, I urge reconsideration of the proposed changes and support maintaining broad, science-based, and sovereign review authority for states and tribes in water quality certification decisions.

Title: Comment submitted by Sharon Fritsch
Comment:
The Honorable Lee Zeldin 	
Administrator 	
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 	
1200 Pennsylvania Avenue 	
Washington, DC 20460 	

Dear Administrator Zeldin:

I strongly oppose the proposed revisions to Section 401 of the water quality certification regulations.  Sierra Club Water Sentinels is asking members to oppose this revision.  They report that the proposed revisions "would dramatically curtail" state and tribal authority to regulate water discharges into protected waters -- that the proposed revisions would "greatly limit the authority of states and tribes to look at thewhole proposed project, and would force them to ignore project-related pollution risks that threaten downstream communities and ecosystems".  

Reading the letter from Daniel Estrin, the legal director of Waterkeeper Alliance, he notes that under the Trump administration, the EPA has sought to "dramatically narrow the scope of waters of the United States under the Clean Water Act", by leaning heavily on arguments about "preserving state authority".  Now the EPA is seeking to restrict state authority.  This means that the people will not have the protection of either the federal government nor local governments.  

 The proposed rules consider benefits to developers, but do not protesct ordinary citizens. 

The new definition of "water" will not take into account waterways such as city canals, seasonal streams and wetlands.  If local jurisdictions are not allowed to protect their watersheds, drinking water will be degraded, and also there will be increased flood risks.   Protection of these components of our water system is extremely important in maintaining water quality.  

The cost of remediating degraded drinking water quality will be disproportionately born by rate payers.  Also, the degradation of upstream watersheds will lead to increased flood risks

I thank you for your attention to these matters.
Sincerely, Sharon Fritsch

Title: Comment submitted by Ursula Phillips
Comment:
My name is Ursula Phillips and I am a Southern Sierra Miwuk tribal member. I am submitting this comment regarding the proposed updates to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.

I am concerned that the proposed rule would limit what tribes are able to review by narrowing the scope of certification to only the discharge point rather than the full project. Water systems are interconnected and impacts do not occur in pieces. Limiting review in this way makes it very difficult to fully consider downstream impacts groundwater connections and effects to cultural or subsistence waters that are vital to tribal communities.

I am also concerned about the proposed limitations on what information tribes can request during the certification process. Tribes must have access to sufficient information in order to make informed decisions that protect water quality. Restricting this ability weakens meaningful review and limits the ability of tribes to identify potential harm before it occurs.

Additionally, the proposed shortening of timelines raises concern for tribes with limited staff and resources. Meaningful water protection requires adequate time to review materials consult internally and consider traditional knowledge. Rigid timelines increase the risk of waiver and reduce the ability of tribes to fully exercise their role in protecting water quality.

These concerns are especially important for tribal members and communities who continue to live within their ancestral homelands and rely on local waters for drinking fishing gathering and cultural practices even when federal recognition and formal regulatory authority are limited or unavailable. Regulatory changes that reduce protections place these communities at even greater risk of harm.

Taken together these proposed changes risk weakening water protections and shifting decision making away from the communities most directly affected. Clean water protection depends on allowing tribes and tribal members to evaluate real world impacts using lived experience cultural knowledge and long term responsibility to place.

To address these concerns, the EPA could consider maintaining flexibility for tribes to evaluate connected and cumulative water quality impacts where appropriate, allowing limited additional information requests necessary for meaningful review, and preserving reasonable timeline extensions to avoid unintentional waiver. Clarifying that applicant involvement cannot override water protective conditions would also help ensure the rule supports water quality rather than limiting it.

I respectfully urge the Environmental Protection Agency to revise the proposed rule to ensure that water quality protections are not narrowed that review authority reflects how water systems function and that communities with longstanding relationships to these waters are not left with fewer safeguards.

Thank you for the opportunity to submit this comment and for considering the concerns of tribal community members whose lives cultures and future generations depend on clean water.

Title: Comment submitted by Ute Mountain Ute Tribe
Comment:
I work in water quality protection and implementation, with a focus on watershed-scale impacts, nonpoint source pollution, and regulatory compliance. I am affiliated with a tribal environmental program and routinely engage with Clean Water Act authorities, including Section 401, as part of my professional responsibilities. I am submitting the questions below to better understand how the proposed revisions to the Section 401 regulations are intended to function in practice, particularly with respect to protecting water quality standards and addressing cumulative and nonpoint source impacts.

1.	How does EPA anticipate water quality standards will be protected from federally permitted activities that result in nonpoint source pollution when Section 401 review is limited to direct point-source discharges?

2.	Given that many water quality impairments result from cumulative impacts rather than single discharges, how does the proposed rule ensure that cumulative effects associated with federally permitted activities are adequately considered during Section 401 certification?

3.	How did EPA evaluate the disproportionate impacts of the proposed revisions on tribal governments, particularly where Section 401 serves as one of the only enforceable mechanisms available to prevent upstream water quality degradation?

4.	If Section 401 authority is narrowed, what enforceable federal or tribal mechanisms does EPA identify as sufficient to address indirect, diffuse, or runoff-related impacts associated with federally permitted projects?

5.	How does EPA reconcile the proposed limitations on preventive review authority with the Clean Water Act’s objective to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters?

6.	How does the proposed rule ensure that tribal water quality standards are meaningfully protected when impacts arise from activities beyond narrowly defined point-source discharges?

Thank you for your time and consideration.
